title: Physical Chemistry I
text:
Welcome
This book collects the video lectures and transcripts from an introductory course on quantum mechanics. Each chapter pairs a video lecture — embedded directly from YouTube — with a full written transcript, making the material accessible in both formats.

title: Physical Chemistry I
section: 0.1 How to Use This Book
text:
0.1 How to Use This Book

Watch the video first to follow along with the instructor’s explanations and worked examples.
Read the transcript for a searchable, referenceable version of the same material, complete with all mathematical notation.

title: Physical Chemistry I
section: 0.2 Course Overview
text:
0.2 Course Overview
The lectures are organized into four parts:

Foundations of Quantum Theory — The quantization of light and matter, including the photoelectric effect and wave-particle duality.
Mathematical Framework — Wavefunctions, quantum mechanical operators, and eigenvalue problems.
Schrödinger’s Equation — Deriving and applying the central equation of quantum mechanics, including the free particle.
Statistical Mechanics — An overview of statistical mechanics and its connection to quantum systems.

title: 2  What is Physical Chemistry?
text: What is Physical Chemistry? (11:38)

title: 2  What is Physical Chemistry?
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 2  What is Physical Chemistry?
section: What is Physical Chemistry? Video Transcript
text:
What is Physical Chemistry? Video Transcript
All right, so before we dive too deep into the details of physical chemistry, you might be curious to know what it is that we’re going to learn. What is physical chemistry? What are the topics in this field, and what delineates physical chemistry as a part apart from some other type of chemistry?
One way to answer that question is to list the various sub-disciplines of chemistry. Physical chemistry, of course, is a sub-discipline of chemistry as a whole. There are a number of different sub-disciplines of chemistry, including analytical chemistry, biochemistry, inorganic chemistry, and organic chemistry, in addition to physical chemistry. This is a fairly common way of breaking up the topics in chemistry. However, it’s not the only way; you might also want to talk about materials chemistry and other sub-disciplines of chemistry that you can consider as well. Many of these areas you probably already have a reasonable understanding of. For instance, you may have had an organic chemistry course or know something about organic chemistry, which deals with carbon-containing molecules, including their synthesis and properties. In contrast, inorganic chemistry focuses on everything else, specifically the synthesis and properties of non-carbon-containing molecules.
This division already separates all of chemistry into carbon-containing and non-carbon-containing substances. However, that’s just about the molecules themselves. A field like analytical chemistry, for example, is not so much interested in the properties of the molecules themselves but in the analysis—the actual act of doing the measurements and the analysis of those molecules. Analytical chemistry focuses on the measurements themselves and, therefore, often involves the construction and principles behind the instruments we use to make those measurements.
Biochemistry, on the other hand, is concerned with reactions and molecules that are biomolecules found in living organisms. It is not about biology or the living organisms themselves but rather the chemistry that takes place within those living organisms. Again, these distinctions are reasonably clear from your prior coursework or from the terminology itself.
If you’re like many students, you might be a little more fuzzy about the boundaries of physical chemistry. What makes something a topic in physical chemistry? The name “physical chemistry” suggests it has something to do with physics or physical processes, and that’s in the right direction. We can gain insight into what makes physical chemistry unique by considering the various topics typically studied in a physical chemistry course.
In a typical physical chemistry course, the subject matter is divided into several different subtopics: gas laws, thermodynamics, kinetics, quantum mechanics, and statistical mechanics. Again, this is not an exhaustive list; many people might want to add more topics or subdivide them further. However, this list provides a reasonable overview of the broad topics we will cover in a physical chemistry course. Some of these topics are likely familiar to you already, while others may be less so.
For example, the ideal gas law, represented by the equation \(PV = nRT\), describes gas laws, where \(n\) stands for moles of gas. Thermodynamics involves relationships between different thermodynamic variables, one of which you might be familiar with is \(\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S\). Kinetics deals with equations that describe how quickly some molecules or concentrations of molecules appear or disappear, thus describing the rate of appearance or disappearance of a species.
You may be less familiar with quantum mechanics if you’re taking a physical chemistry course for the first time, but you’ve probably at least heard of Schrödinger’s equation, which is a fundamental equation in quantum mechanics. We will discuss that equation and all these topics in much greater detail as the course progresses. Statistical mechanics may also be less familiar to you, but it includes important equations that we will spend a lot of time discussing.
Having listed these topics gives us a pretty good idea of how to describe the field of physical chemistry in general. What do gas laws, thermodynamics, kinetics, and quantum mechanics have in common that makes them topics in physical chemistry as opposed to biochemistry or analytical chemistry? The key is that any topic in physical chemistry will involve a collection of equations that represent those particular topics. This is not necessarily true for organic chemistry or all areas of analytical chemistry. While equations do appear in those fields, they are not as central to their study. You won’t get very far in physical chemistry without encountering equations of some sort.
Physical chemists aim to understand and predict how systems behave by using models. These models might take the form of mathematical equations, physical models, or graphical representations that I might sketch on the board. We will use models to try to understand physical and chemical systems. The key word here is “models.” Throughout physical chemistry, we will introduce some model for how a system behaves, often in the form of an equation, and then we will see what that model tells us about the system’s behavior.
Physical chemists are agnostic; we don’t care whether we are making predictions about how a carbon-containing molecule behaves, a non-carbon-containing molecule, or a biomolecule. We are satisfied if we can understand and predict how things work using our models. So, if you encounter a new area of chemistry and wonder whether it falls under physical chemistry or biochemistry, if it involves a model and predicting what is going on, chances are physical chemists lay at least some claim to that topic.
It’s important to note that this is not necessarily a sharp division between different fields. Many other fields of chemistry also seek to understand and predict phenomena, and many use equations or models. Equations appear in biochemistry, analytical chemistry, and so on. The boundaries between these fields are not always as distinct as they might seem.
One last comment about the name “physical chemistry”: it suggests a connection to physics. If I say that all the topics I’ve just described are sub-disciplines of chemistry, there are other scientific fields as well, such as physics and biology. We have subdivided chemistry into different subfields and then physical chemistry into subtopics beneath that.
In a somewhat tongue-in-cheek manner, I can describe the differences between physics, chemistry, and biology relatively simply. Physics is primarily concerned with rules and equations, and in physics, none of those rules have exceptions. For example, the theory of gravity is described by equations that apply universally. Chemistry, on the other hand, loves to use rules, but these are often rules of thumb. You might know periodic trends in the periodic table, but some elements behave a little oddly. Similarly, you might be familiar with named reactions that work well for most members of a certain class of molecules, but there are exceptions.
Biology could be described as a collection of exceptions, with relatively few rules that define it. This is not a rigorous way of defining the content of these fields, but it provides a useful perspective on how physicists, chemists, and biologists think about their respective disciplines. In this sense, the name “physical chemistry” indicates that this branch of chemistry has something to do with physics in addition to chemistry.
Another way to describe what physical chemistry is would be to say that physical chemists appreciate equations and rules, especially when there are not many exceptions to them. It’s as if physical chemists are studying the content of chemistry—chemicals and chemical reactions—while applying the principles or models of physics to do so. This approach allows us to tackle chemistry problems using a physics perspective.
Hopefully, this gives you a reasonable overview of where we’re going. The next thing we will do is talk in more detail about the topics we will cover in the physical chemistry course.

title: 3  The Original Double Slit Experiment
section: The Original Double Slit Experiment Video Transcript
text:
The Original Double Slit Experiment Video Transcript
What is light? That’s a good question, isn’t it? Light is… light is… what is light? Isn’t it an element? Light is brightness, I guess.

We have auras?
We all have auras.
Which are light?
Yes, they are. It lights up the room; it makes it… not dark.

What’s the difference between blue light and red light?

The color. It goes in your eyes, and then you see stuff. The range goes from white to red to orange to green; it’s like the chakras of your body.
Can you see my aura?
No, not particularly right now.
Is it too bright out?
It’s very sunny out here today.
Does that make it harder to see someone’s aura?
Not necessarily. If I were to explain it to a blind person, I would say it would be the difference… You see nothing whatsoever as a blind person, whereas I see things in front of me. To be fair, the question of what light is is not an easy one. For centuries, the greatest minds in science debated this issue. In the late 1600s, Newton proposed that light was a stream of particles or corpuscles. He proposed this in his treatise, Opticks. But at the same time, a Dutch physicist named Huygens proposed that light was a wave. This debate raged on until it was settled by the experiment I’ve recreated today: Thomas Young’s double slit experiment. To make sure I got the experiment right, I went to the original source. With the help of Brady Haran, I managed to get into the vault underneath the Royal Society in London. We’re in! There, I found Thomas Young’s handwritten notes from 1803.

“I brought into the sunbeam a slip of card, about one-thirtieth of an inch in breadth, and observed its shadow, either on the wall or on other cards held at different distances. Besides the fringes of color on each side of the shadow, the shadow itself was divided by similar parallel fringes of smaller dimensions.”
Wow. This is an experiment so simple that you could make it at home, and yet so fiddly that I’ve never seen it done before with sunlight.

I was thinking about doing it in a box, like a fridge box.
And you could take it out on the street.

Could I possibly interview you guys for about a minute? We’re doing a science experiment. What I have here is an empty box, and this is a little eyepiece where we can look in, and this is a hole. I’m going to place this slide above that hole, and if you look closely, you’ll see that there are two openings, very narrow openings side by side. It’s a double slit. Now, before we have a look, we need to tilt it towards the sun a little bit, so we want the sun to hit this double slit directly.

What are we going to see on the bottom of the box?
The obvious thing you think you’re going to see is you’re going to see two lines. Two lines on the bottom of the box. Two broad bands.
Two little lines.
Yeah. I think it will be one… one line, sort of two. I can expect to see the whole box lit up.
Probably a kaleidoscope of some sort.
A bunch of colors. Probably, yeah. Rainbow? Different colors?

There, have a look. You expected to see kind of one line— is that what you see?
No! I see dots. How many? It’s one circle. Oh, there’s one… there’s one in the middle, the strongest, two either side. The two on the outside are multicolored, and the one in the middle is just white. It looks kind of rainbow-like. The rainbow of color as well. Quite a few colors and lots of little dots. And there are more dots appearing. I think I can even see more dots spreading along.
Yeah, that’s amazing.
Yeah, I can see tons of dots now. Not tons, but I can see dots spreading across that way.

On either side?
Yeah, definitely.

Isn’t that amazing?

Yeah, that’s incredible.
And that’s just nothing else apart from…
Two slits. That’s incredible. But all we’re doing is putting light through two very narrow slits side by side, so how does this make any sense? There’s some kind of principle involved in it that the average person is not familiar with. That’s the only explanation. I’m really confused by it, but I’d like to find out why. People were debating: Is light a wave or is it made of particles? So what causes that? Well, if light were behaving as particles, you would expect it to go through each slit and just produce a bright spot underneath, so we would see two bright spots at the bottom of the box. But if light is behaving as waves, then the wave from one slit can interact with the waves from the other slit.

I’ve got a demonstration here on a little pond, where we can see this with water waves. I have two sources of ripples, which are basically like the two slits. When I create ripples with a single source, they travel out with a circular wave front; nothing particularly surprising there. But if I add a second source of ripples, then we start getting an interesting pattern. This pattern is created by the ripples from the two sources interacting with each other. Where they meet up, peaks with peaks and troughs with troughs, the amplitude of the wave is increased; that’s what we call constructive interference. But if the peak from one wave meets up with the trough from the other, then we get destructive interference, and there’s basically no wave there.
And this is exactly what was happening with the light. When the light from one slit met up, peaks with peaks and troughs with troughs, they constructively interfered and produced a bright spot. But if the trough from the wave from one slit met up with the peak of the wave from the other slit, they would destructively interfere, and you wouldn’t see any light there. It’s light canceling itself out.

This is basically the same as having two drops of water falling in a swimming pool,
That’s right, exactly the same pattern.
and then they overlap. As this ripple overlaps with those ripples, down at the bottom, you get a series of… you get like a bright spot, then a dark spot, then a bright spot, then a dark spot, then a bright spot.

Now there is a slight complication, which is that sunlight is composed of many different colors, and they have different wavelengths. So, obviously, they’re going to meet up at slightly different points, and that’s what causes the rainbowing effects as we go further from the central maximum. You saw the ones to the right were slightly colored; that’s because the reds are going to meet up at different places than the blues.
And that’s all that makes color differences— is different wavelengths?

Exactly.
That’s amazing. So the difference between— So that red bin over there on the green pot, it’s just… I’m seeing that—
It’s a different wavelength.
It’s just a different wavelength.
And that’s how we’re bringing these beautiful colors all around us.
Exactly. That’s amazing; I’m amazed! Thanks, man! Thank you! I have been enlightened, literally.

Now, you may have noticed in that experiment that the light on the bottom of the box was not in the shape of slits; rather, they were more like round blobs, and I want to know why that is. Can you write an answer for me in the comment section? I’ll give you a hint: one of the videos I’ve linked is suggestive of the answer. So convincing were the results of Young’s double slit experiment that the scientific community concluded that light must be a wave; there is no way it could be a particle.

title: 4  Quantization of Energy Part 1: Blackbody Radiation and the Ultraviolet Catastrophe
section: Quantization of Energy Part 1: Blackbody Radiation and the Ultraviolet Catastrophe Video Transcript
text:
Quantization of Energy Part 1: Blackbody Radiation and the Ultraviolet Catastrophe Video Transcript
Hey, it’s Professor Dave. Let’s talk about the ultraviolet catastrophe. We know that the classical physics of Newton and his contemporaries reigned supreme for a few centuries, so what were the events that finally exposed its limitations? What was the first thing that suggested there was more to the universe than we had previously thought? This seismic shift was initiated in 1901 when Max Planck solved something called the ultraviolet catastrophe.
The problem went like this: Certain objects are called blackbodies because they emit electromagnetic radiation of all wavelengths. The sun is an example of such an object, and we can take a look at the distribution of the wavelengths of light that we receive from the sun. Most of it is in the visible spectrum, which is why our eyesight evolved to pick up this kind of light. However, we also receive light on either side, in the ultraviolet portion as well as infrared and beyond. A hot piece of metal will also do this, and this was the way we studied blackbodies at the time, noting that the distribution depends not at all on the material but only on temperature, with the particular wavelength that is emitted with maximum intensity shifting left as temperature increases. This maximum will move into the visible spectrum at around 4000 Kelvin and above. This is why very hot objects appear to glow, like a hot oven, a light bulb filament, or the sun and other stars, because objects at these temperatures emit a lot of visible light, as opposed to something like the human body, which at around 310 Kelvin emits essentially no visible light. This is why we can’t see each other in the dark.
The problem with the blackbody spectrum was that classical electromagnetism could not account for it. Mathematical models attempting to produce these distributions were able to fit the data for the longer wavelengths, but they did not predict that the intensity would dip down to the left for the ultraviolet portion of the spectrum, as experimental data illustrated. Instead, the math predicted that the intensity would continue to increase as the wavelength decreased and become infinitely large as the wavelength approached zero. Of course, we know that this can’t be true; otherwise, every time you use the oven, you would get blasted with ultraviolet radiation. This contradiction was dubbed, somewhat dramatically, the ultraviolet catastrophe.
In science, if a theory does not accurately align with observations of reality, it must be revised. Thus, we realized that classical electromagnetism, as powerful as it is, must have some kind of limitations in its ability to describe light and energy. As we said, Max Planck solved this problem, and he did so by introducing a concept called quantization. We know from classical physics that heat is just the transfer of kinetic energy from one place to another. In the case of a piece of solid hot metal, that kinetic energy takes the form of atomic vibrations or oscillations. These vibrations are what generate the light we see in the blackbody spectrum.
Planck proposed that the vibrational energies of these atoms, and by extension the energies of the electromagnetic waves emitted by these atoms, must be quantized, meaning that rather than being able to take on any value from a continuous series, they can only possess specific discrete values from a set of accepted values. In this way, he developed this expression for blackbody radiation, where energy is equal to \(n \cdot h \cdot f\). Here, \(n\) can be any integer, \(h\) is Planck’s constant, equal to \(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\) joule-seconds, and \(f\) is the frequency of radiation. The \(n\) value is what results in quantization, as it can only be an integer and not any fraction or decimal in between, meaning that the resulting energies will also comprise a set of allowed values, with anything in between being forbidden.
This application of quantization and the accompanying Planck’s constant were developed in an ad hoc manner, meaning that they were simply proposed for practical purposes. However, they allowed for the accurate prediction of the true distribution of blackbody radiation at all wavelengths, which meant that this constant was more than a mathematical fluke; it was a clue as to the fundamental nature of reality. The fact that Planck’s constant is so incredibly small explains why the notion of quantization of energy had not cropped up before. It shows that energy is quantized on such an incredibly small scale that the gradations between the allowed values are utterly minuscule, appearing non-existent to any measuring apparatus. Energy appears to be continuous to macroscopic beings such as humans, but on the fundamental level, it is indeed quantized, even though this conclusion was so strange that most scientists of the time, including Planck, couldn’t believe that it had actual concrete physical meaning.
This was the first time that quantization had solved such a big problem in physics, but it wouldn’t be the last. It was the first in a series of developments that would utterly transform the field of physics and, by extension, our perception of reality. While Planck’s work solved one problem, it created another: Why is there quantization of energy? This marked the beginning of the quantum revolution, so let’s continue and see what happened next. Thanks for watching, guys. Subscribe to my channel for more tutorials, support me on Patreon so I can keep making content, and as always, feel free to email me.

title: 5  Quantization of Energy Part 2: Photons, Electrons, and Wave-Particle Duality
section: Quantization of Energy Part 2: Photons, Electrons, and Wave-Particle Duality Video Transcript
text:
Quantization of Energy Part 2: Photons, Electrons, and Wave-Particle Duality Video Transcript
Professor Dave again. Let’s continue our discussion of quantization. We are now moving through the initial discoveries that brought about modern physics. Planck set things into motion in 1901, and the man who was to carry the torch next was none other than Albert Einstein. In 1905, while working as a patent clerk in Switzerland, he published three seminal papers that revolutionized physics. These were about Brownian motion, special relativity, which we will get to later, and the photoelectric effect. The last of these, just like Planck’s work, also made use of quantization, which cemented the concept as more than just a fluke.
The photoelectric effect has to do with the way light is able to eject an electron from a piece of metal. Given that this subject matter is also of great concern to chemistry, we have already covered this topic in the general chemistry course. I highly recommend clicking on the card you see now to view this in-depth analysis of the photoelectric effect, as it is a crucial step in modern physics. If you have familiarized yourself with this concept, we will simply recall that Einstein’s work showed that only light above a certain frequency could eject an electron, regardless of the intensity of the beam. For this reason, he proposed that light was comprised of individual quanta called photons, whereby it was an individual photon of sufficient energy equal to \(h \times f\) that was able to eject an electron.
This meant a number of things. First, it wasn’t just the vibrational energy of the atoms in the blackbody that was quantized. Light is also quantized since photons are quanta. So, quantization seems to be here to stay. Furthermore, since it had already been well established that certain light-related phenomena, like diffraction and interference patterns, are best explained using a wave model, it must be the case that light can be described as both a particle and a wave. This bizarre concept is called wave-particle duality, and though it is essentially impossible to visualize how something can be both a particle and a wave, this is the kind of quantum weirdness we are going to have to get used to if we are to learn modern physics.
In order to grasp the material moving forward, we must let go of our sense experience and its suggestions of what waves and particles must be—things like ocean waves and baseballs—and realize that in the realm of the submicroscopic, these notions simply do not apply. Moving on from Einstein, Niels Bohr showed that quantization of energy also applies to the energy of an electron in a hydrogen atom. Bohr proposed that the electron can only inhabit specific energy levels and that it will move between these energy levels when absorbing or emitting a photon of energy that is equivalent to the difference in energy between the two energy levels involved in the transition. This model was able to explain the emission spectrum of hydrogen and other elements, and by extension, the color of every object that reflects light. More information on the Bohr model can be found in the general chemistry series or by clicking on the card you see right now.
After this, de Broglie demonstrated that it is not just light that exhibits wave-particle duality, but particles of matter as well. This meant that the electron, just like any other particle, has a wavelength that depends on its momentum, which complicated matters for chemistry quite a bit. This notion was soon corroborated when a beam of electrons was shown to exhibit a diffraction pattern, just like a beam of light does. This meant there was no turning back. Waves can act like particles, and particles can act like waves, whether we are talking about light or matter. Because Newtonian mechanics is unable to fully describe this kind of behavior, we had to develop an entirely new field to do so, and that field is called quantum mechanics. This and the figures that contributed to its development will be the focus of the next few tutorials.
Once again, tutorials 12 through 14 from the general chemistry course cover the photoelectric effect, the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom, wave-particle duality, and some basic quantum terminology in greater detail than we have mentioned here. It is highly suggested that you take a moment to review these materials before proceeding with the modern physics course. If you are good to go, let’s move on to some new concepts. Thanks for watching, guys. Subscribe to my channel for more tutorials, support me on Patreon so I can keep making content, and as always, feel free to email me.
crumbs: 5  Quantization of Energy Part 2: Photons, Electrons, and Wave-Particle Duality

title: 6  Wave-Particle Duality and the Photoelectric Effect
section: Wave-Particle Duality and the Photoelectric Effect Video Transcript
text:
Wave-Particle Duality and the Photoelectric Effect Video Transcript
Hey, it’s Professor Dave. I want to tell you about wave-particle duality, so let’s take a break from atoms and look at something else: light. For some time, we had known light to behave as an electromagnetic wave; for this reason, light is also called electromagnetic radiation. Light waves have both a wavelength, which is the distance from tip to tip, and a frequency, which is the number of waves that pass by a point per unit time. These two properties are inversely proportional: the shorter the wave, the more of them can go by per second, resulting in a higher frequency. These variables are related by the following equation:
[ c = f ]
where \(c\) is the speed of light, about 300 million meters per second, the speed limit of the universe. The electromagnetic spectrum shows light of all different wavelengths, from gamma rays to radio waves, including this tiny sliver in the middle, which is visible light—the light we can detect with our eyes.
The wave theory of light was very useful for a time, but it could not explain the photoelectric effect. This was the observation that if a certain metal plate is irradiated with light, an electron is ejected, which can be detected when it interacts with a positively charged wire or plate sensor. The incongruity was that the ability of the light to eject an electron depends only on its frequency and not its intensity. So, if the beam was below a certain frequency, even a very intense beam with a lot of light could not eject an electron, whereas above a certain frequency, the faintest beam possible could do it. This seemed strange, and a famous fellow named Albert Einstein solved the problem by extending a concept developed by Max Planck just five years prior. Planck proposed that energy is not continuous but rather quantized; that is, all energies are multiples of the smallest fundamental unit of energy, the Planck energy. In fact, quantum theory went on to show that everything is quantized—space and time too—so you can’t infinitely subdivide them; you’ll eventually get to a smallest thing that can’t be divided further, but more on that in the physics course.
Einstein extended what Planck said about energy and rationalized that light must also be made of quanta, which he called photons. These are particles of light. This explains the photoelectric effect because an electron is ejected when struck by a singular photon with sufficient energy; it only takes one photon. So, even the faintest beam above a certain frequency will be able to do it. However, if none of them are of that minimum energy, no matter how many photons strike the sample, an electron will not be ejected. It was found that the energy of the photon can be given by the following equation:
[ E = h f ]
where \(E\) is the energy of the photon, \(h\) is Planck’s constant, and \(f\) is the frequency of the photon. We now have to accept that light obeys wave-particle duality, meaning it is both a particle and a wave at the same time. Confused? You should be. This is the first discovery in a series that brought about the quantum revolution, completely changing the landscape of physics and how we view the universe. Newton’s Laws no longer reign supreme in the world of the very small, and the universe became much stranger almost overnight, thanks to Einstein and others who were inspired by him.
Thanks for watching, guys! Subscribe to my channel for more tutorials, and as always, feel free to email me.

title: 7  Quantum Mechanics of the Electron
section: Quantum Mechanics of the Electron Video Transcript
text:
Quantum Mechanics of the Electron Video Transcript
Hey guys, it’s Professor Dave. Let’s talk about quantum mechanics. We have seen how the first steps into quantum theory gave us the idea that all energy is not continuous but quantized, and that light obeys wave-particle duality. The next idea came courtesy of Louis de Broglie—pardon my poor French pronunciation. He reasoned that if waves can behave like particles, then particles must also behave like waves. In his de Broglie relation, he showed that the wavelength of a particle is inversely proportional to its mass. Therefore, we must now understand that all matter has a wavelength, from subatomic particles all the way up to your body and even a star.
A macroscopic object like a baseball will have a wavelength that is trillions of times smaller than the nucleus of an atom, so we don’t need to think of baseballs as waves. Newtonian mechanics work just fine to describe the motion of objects we can see. However, an electron has a wavelength that is very significant compared to its actual size, so we can no longer think of electrons as particles; we must think of them as both a particle and a wave. Wave-particle duality just got extended from light to matter.
Erwin Schrödinger wanted a way to describe the wave mechanics of the electron, so he developed quantum mechanics to do just that. As a result, we can no longer think of an atom as electrons revolving around a nucleus the way planets revolve around a star. We have to think of an electron as a cloud of probability density that represents regions where the electron can be found.
Werner Heisenberg soon showed that as a result of quantum mechanics, there are fundamental limitations to what we can know about a particle like an electron, so he formulated his uncertainty principle. The main ramification for chemistry is that the more we know about the location of an electron, the less we know about what it’s doing or its momentum. Conversely, the more we know about what it’s doing, the less we know about its location. This has nothing to do with the limitations of our measuring instruments; it is an inherent quality of the electron.
If we could know both its location and momentum with complete certainty, then we could describe it as merely a particle, but it is not a particle; it is both a particle and a wave. This seems to defy common sense, so how can it be true? We have to get used to the fact that common sense doesn’t make for good science. Nature is governed by mathematical equations, not by human perceptions of how things should be.
So when the math says that electrons are both particles and waves, or that space is curved around massive objects, or that time flows at different rates for different observers, we have to accept that this is a deeper tier of reality than what we can immediately perceive and comprehend. It seems like common sense that the sun goes around the earth, which is why we believed it for so long, but mathematical calculations made by several astronomers forced us to accept that it is the earth that goes around the sun, because their model predicted the locations of celestial objects with more precision.
We can’t look back at the solar system from afar, but we don’t have to. The math is the evidence. It is our understanding of mathematics that allows us to transcend our five senses and discover the true nature of the universe, from the tiniest particles to the galaxies and beyond. Thanks for watching, guys! Subscribe to my channel for more tutorials, and as always, feel free to email me.
crumbs: 7  Quantum Mechanics of the Electron

title: 8  Light is Quantized
text: Light is Quantized (12:30)

title: 8  Light is Quantized
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 8  Light is Quantized
section: Light is Quantized Video Transcript
text:
Light is Quantized Video Transcript
Okay, so our goal is to understand quantum mechanics well enough to predict the energies of molecules or other chemical or physical systems. To start, we’ll review some concepts you already know about quantum mechanics from earlier chemistry courses.
First, we can discuss the quantum mechanical properties of light. One thing we know about light is that we typically think of it as a wave. Light behaves like a wave, similar to other types of waves, such as water waves and sound waves. It can reflect off objects, diffract or bend in different media, and undergo interference, where different light waves can constructively or destructively interfere with one another. These are all characteristics we associate with wave behavior. However, it turns out that light is also made of particles, so it is not purely a wave; it is a wave made of particles. The individual particles of light are called photons.
One of the key things we know about photons is how to describe their energy. These equations are likely familiar: the energy of light is either \(E = h \nu\) (Planck’s constant times frequency) or \(E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\) (Planck’s constant times the speed of light divided by the wavelength of the light). Perhaps the most common way to think about these equations is as a relationship between frequency and wavelength, which are inversely proportional to one another. The actual meaning of these equations is that the energy of one individual particle of light, one photon, is given by \(E = h \nu\) or \(E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\).
For example, let’s consider a photon of red light. If you look around, you won’t have to search far to find a red LED. Let’s say the light coming from that LED has a wavelength of 671 nanometers, which is a common wavelength for red light. If I ask you what the energy of the photons of light with this wavelength is, we can plug into this expression: \(E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\) to work through the example and see what the units and numerical values are like.
Planck’s constant, as I mentioned, is \(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\) joule-seconds, and the speed of light is another constant, \(2.998 \times 10^{8}\) meters per second. If I divide that by the wavelength, \(671\) nanometers or \(671 \times 10^{-9}\) meters, we have a simple set of numbers to multiply and divide. When we do that, the energy works out to be a tiny number of joules.
So, the important thing to keep in mind about these equations for light is that they not only tell us the relative values of wavelengths and frequencies, but they also indicate that the energy of a single photon of light of this red color, with a wavelength of 671 nanometers, is quantized. When I say light is quantized, I mean that light comes in quanta; it comes in discrete packets that cannot be subdivided any further. If I have one photon of light, it has a specific amount of energy, let’s say \(3 \times 10^{-19}\) joules. I can have that much energy from one photon, or if I have two photons of light, I can have twice that much energy. However, I cannot have \(4 \times 10^{-19}\) joules worth of red light; I can only have one photon, two photons, three photons, or a large number of photons, but it has to be an integer number of photons. Each photon has this much energy if they all have the same wavelength.
This quantization is a key feature of quantum mechanics. The reason we call it quantum mechanics is that it describes how things are quantized. For each of these features of quantum mechanics that I will point out over the next few video lectures, I will highlight something that you already knew: light is made up of photons and some equations. I will also discuss the evidence for how we know nature behaves this way.
How do we know that light is made up of photons as opposed to being a continuous wave? We have known this about light ever since we started studying the photoelectric effect over a century ago, in 1905. The photoelectric effect is something you may be familiar with, even if you haven’t heard the term before. It is what opens the grocery store door for you when you walk into an automated opening door or what keeps a garage door from closing on you if you pass your hand or a car in that invisible beam that triggers the door to go back up. This all works based on the photoelectric effect.
The name tells us that “photo” means light and “electric” refers to electricity or electrons. The photoelectric effect occurs when I shine light of a particular frequency or wavelength on a surface, usually a metal surface, although it could be any surface. Under certain conditions, that surface can eject an electron. If the energy of the incoming light is greater than the energy required to bind the electron to the metal surface, which we call the work function (usually denoted by the symbol \(\Phi\)), then I can send in a photon with enough energy to remove an electron, and that electron gets kicked out and flies away through vacuum or air or whatever medium is above the metal.
In terms of equations, the kinetic energy of the ejected electron is equal to the energy of the incoming photon minus the work function. If we study the photoelectric effect and examine how the kinetic energies or other properties depend on the color of light we send in, we can measure what is called the photocurrent, denoted as \(I\). Essentially, this is just the number of electrons that get ejected from the sample. These electrons can be used as electricity, and I can measure how many of them there are, which shows up as a current.
If I ask how many electrons get kicked out as I change the frequency of the light, the graph looks like this: at low frequencies, no electrons get ejected by the photoelectric effect. Once I reach a cutoff frequency, which I will call \(\nu_0\) (or \(\nu\) naught), I suddenly start getting electrons ejected, but that number does not change as I continue to increase the frequency.
I could also ask how the kinetic energy of those electrons changes as I increase the frequency, changing the light from red to yellow to green to blue to violet to ultraviolet. By increasing the energy of the photon through changing its frequency, we can see what happens to the energy of the electrons that get ejected. This equation tells us that \(E = h \nu\) is the incoming energy, \(\Phi\) is the amount I have to pay to remove the electron, and as long as I have enough energy to remove an electron, all the excess energy goes into the kinetic energy of the ejected electron.
As long as I have more than enough energy to kick out an electron, all the excess energy goes directly into the kinetic energy of the electron that gets ejected. These two graphs make sense when we think of this as individual photons coming in, having enough energy to pay for the work function or not, with all the excess energy going into the kinetic energy. This understanding aligns with the idea that light is composed of photons.
Before 1905, when we did not know that light was made of particles and thought of it purely as a wave, these results did not make sense. At that time, the natural way to think about the kinetic energy graph would have been to expect that if you shine a continuous wave of light at a surface, the energy of the electrons ejected from that surface would depend on the energy of the incoming wave. This is akin to asking what happens when water waves approach a beach and how much energy is transferred to the sand particles that get dislodged.
The expectation would have been that the more energy I put into the surface, the more energy the displaced particles would have. There was no idea of a threshold because, even if the individual waves had relatively low frequency, we could always increase the amplitude of the waves. We could always send more waves at the beach or shine a brighter light onto a surface, and surely that would dump more energy into the surface, thus kicking out electrons with higher energy.
This is what would have been predicted if light behaved like a wave, where more frequency clearly means more energy. However, this is not what actually happens. It took some reevaluation, and eventually, we realized that light is made of individual particles. Only one quantum, one particle of light, has enough energy to pay to remove an electron. This is the threshold at which electrons are either ejected or not. If they are ejected, all the excess energy goes into the kinetic energy of the electron.
As I mentioned, this is one piece of evidence for why we know light is actually made of photons. It turns out that light is not the only thing that is quantized; it is composed of discrete individual amounts of energy. Next, we will discuss other things that are quantized besides light.

title: 9  Energies Are Quantized
text: Energies Are Quantized (4:43)

title: 9  Energies Are Quantized
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 9  Energies Are Quantized
section: Energies Are Quantized Video Transcript
text:
Energies Are Quantized Video Transcript
We know that light is quantized; it is made up of individual particles. We must have an integer number of those particles—one, two, three, or some integer number of photons—to make up a light beam. It turns out that not just the energy of light is quantized, meaning we can have the energy of one photon, two photons, or three photons, but we cannot have the energy of one-and-a-half photons. We cannot have a non-integer number of photons, so we have certain allowed energies of light. Thus, we say the energy of a light beam is quantized.
It turns out that not just light, but the energies of other things are quantized as well. This is something we have some experience with from general chemistry or earlier chemistry classes. When we write an energy level diagram, let’s say for something like the hydrogen atom, the electrons in 100 nm can occupy certain states, and those states have certain energies. We can refer to these states as s, p, and d orbitals. For now, if I say the energy of that electron can have this value \(E_1\), or this value \(E_2\), or this value \(E_3\), or various other values, what I have essentially done is say that the energy levels of the hydrogen atom are quantized. I can have this value, or I can have this value, or this value; I cannot have any values in between the values that I have drawn. There is no way to make the hydrogen atom have this amount of energy; it can only have these discrete individual quantized amounts of energy that I have drawn on this diagram.
This is something we know about hydrogen atoms, and in fact, every molecule, every atom, and every chemical system has quantized energy levels. As usual, we did not suspect that this weird quantum behavior was true, even if it seems fairly commonplace now. It wasn’t always so. The evidence that first told us that energy levels were quantized came from understanding how light is either emitted or absorbed by atoms and molecules, something called the atomic emission spectrum.
If I bring up a picture of the atomic emission spectrum for hydrogen, this diagram tells us the frequencies of light as a function of the frequency of light given off by hydrogen. We see that only certain frequencies of light are emitted. For example, this reddish color of light can be emitted by a hydrogen atom if I excite it to some higher energy level. It can drop down to a lower energy and emit a photon of this reddish color, or this bluish color, or these violet colors, but only these colors. I never see hydrogen emit the orange, yellow, or green frequencies of light that would be here. Only certain discrete or quantized photons are emitted by hydrogen, and that’s because when hydrogen is in this state, it can only fall, let’s say, to this state or to this state; it can’t fall anywhere in between because there is no level there to accept it.
For example, this reddish photon is emitted when hydrogen falls from its third state down to its second state. Each one of these different photons corresponds to a different individual transition between discrete quantized states of the hydrogen atom. This is true not just for hydrogen. If I pull up another picture of what that looks like for helium, this is the emission spectrum for a helium atom. Notice that the colors of light given off by a helium atom are not the same as they are for hydrogen because the energy levels are different for hydrogen than they are for helium. Still, they are discrete and quantized; only this color of light, or this color of light, or this color of light, and so on can be given off by helium—not anything in this range where we don’t see emission.
So, it is not just the energy of light that is quantized, but also the energy levels of matter, meaning atoms, and molecules made of atoms. Those energy levels themselves are quantized as well. Interestingly, it turns out that it is not only energies that are quantized, but other properties of matter can be quantized as well, and that is the next thing we are going to talk about.

title: 10  States of Matter Are Quantized
text: States of Matter Are Quantized (6:17)
crumbs: 10  States of Matter Are Quantized

title: 10  States of Matter Are Quantized
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel
crumbs: 10  States of Matter Are Quantized

title: 10  States of Matter Are Quantized
section: States of Matter Are Quantized Video Transcript
text:
States of Matter Are Quantized Video Transcript
We understand now that energies are quantized. Both the energies of things like light, which has a certain discrete number of quanta—quantum mechanical particles or photons—and the energy levels of matter, such as atoms and molecules, are also quantized. However, it turns out that not only are the energies quantized, but also the states that matter can exist in are quantized.
When I say “states of matter,” that might immediately bring to mind states like solid, liquid, or gas. Many textbooks might use the term “state of matter” to describe solids, liquids, or gases, while I will use the term “phases of matter” to describe different physical phases like solid, liquid, or gas. When I say “states of matter,” I mean the same thing as when I refer to the states that have a certain energy of a molecule, such as the gauche or anti state of butane. I’m just talking about the arrangement of the molecules in a way that describes the system. So, “states of matter” doesn’t have anything to do with solids, liquids, or gases.
It’s already a little bit strange, depending on how accustomed you are to this quantum mechanical fact: the energy levels of matter are quantized. A particular molecule or system can have energy \(E_1\) or energy \(E_2\), but it can never have an arbitrary amount of energy. If I want the molecule to have a specific amount of energy, I simply can’t do it; it can only have these specific discrete quantized amounts of energy.
What’s even weirder is the fact that the arrangement of the particles in the matter itself is quantized, meaning they can only exist in certain ways as well. This is definitely not how we are used to thinking about macroscopic objects behaving. For example, I imagine that I can make this marker take on any orientation I want. However, at the microscopic level, that’s not true. When we talk about things like electrons, atoms, and molecules, it turns out I can make them take on certain arrangements, but I can’t make them take on other arrangements. That begins to feel a little strange.
To illustrate this, let’s consider an example that provides evidence for why we know this is true about at least small quantum mechanical objects. We’ll go back to the example of the hydrogen atom, particularly thinking about the orbitals in hydrogen atoms. We know that the electron in a hydrogen atom can occupy the 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 3d orbitals, and so on. There are many different orbitals that the electron can occupy.
If we were to draw them, we know that an s orbital has spherical symmetry, so the electron is delocalized or spread out in this spherical electron cloud. This differs from, say, a p orbital, which has a dumbbell shape, where again, the electron is spread out or delocalized in this dumbbell-shaped electron cloud. There are a large number of different orbitals, each with its own shapes. A d orbital has a cloverleaf shape, while f orbitals have eight lobes instead of four.
Notice that there are shapes that do not fall within this collection of different orbitals. For example, the shapes I’ve drawn are all perfectly fine; these are allowed states of matter, configurations that the electron in the hydrogen atom is allowed to have. However, there are other states that are not allowed. I cannot make the electron in a hydrogen atom take on a heart shape, even if I delocalize the electron and spread it out around that shape. That’s simply not a configuration that an electron takes on in a hydrogen atom in the real world. There are no heart-shaped electrons; no matter how many different orbitals I consider, I will not find one that corresponds to a heart shape or a diamond shape for an electron.
You are already familiar with the fact that certain shapes of electrons are allowed while others are not. This is because nature does not allow those disallowed shapes to exist. So, it’s not just the energy levels that are quantized; the reason the energy levels are quantized is that the physical arrangement of the electron itself is quantized. Only certain discrete shapes, orientations, or arrangements are permissible. Each of these corresponds with one of the different energies. Discrete quantized energies exist because the electron itself can only exist in certain patterns. Perhaps if I were able to create a heart-shaped electron, it would have some other energy, but it would be one of these disallowed energies.
This is one of the other strange features of quantum mechanics: it forces matter, such as electrons, atoms, and molecules, to take on only certain arrangements. Next, we will conclude our tour of the weird properties of quantum mechanics by discussing how matter also behaves a little bit like a wave, in addition to acting like a particle.
crumbs: 10  States of Matter Are Quantized

title: 11  Matter Has Wave-like Properties
text: Matter Has Wave-like Properties (18:23)

title: 11  Matter Has Wave-like Properties
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 11  Matter Has Wave-like Properties
section: Matter Has Wave-like Properties Video Transcript
text:
Matter Has Wave-like Properties Video Transcript
Even though we normally think of light as behaving like a wave, we’ve seen that it’s actually made of particles. Sometimes, it behaves like a particle when we consider individual photons of light. Individual particles of light are required to be thought of as particles instead of waves. It turns out that matter also behaves a little bit schizophrenically like this. Normally, we think of matter—atoms, molecules, baseballs—as being composed of individual particles. However, it is sometimes necessary to think of matter as behaving like a wave. These are the two different aspects of what’s called wave-particle duality. Every quantum mechanical object, whether it’s a photon of light, an atom, a molecule, or an electron, has what’s called wave-particle duality. Sometimes it behaves like a particle, and in other ways, it behaves like a wave.
In particular, when we think of matter having wave-like properties, it begins to sound a little bit weird. So let’s talk more about what that means. The different properties of matter that show it is, in some respects, like a wave include that matter is delocalized. We are used to making statements about matter that describe its particle behavior; it has a position, it has momentum, and we can describe where that particle is and where it’s going. But at other times, like a wave, if I ask you where a wave is in an ocean, the wave is delocalized—it’s spread out over some region of space. The same thing turns out to be true about small quantum mechanical objects as well.
Two other things that particles can do, which matter can also do, are diffraction and interference. If I take two waves and cross them, they can either interfere constructively or destructively, depending on whether two troughs or peaks hit at the same time or whether a peak hits at the same time as a trough, resulting in destructive interference. That type of interference and diffraction—when a wave bends as it passes around an object or through different media—are properties that we think of as describing how waves behave, but they also describe how matter behaves under certain circumstances.
To delve deeper into this for small quantum mechanical particles, let’s talk about something called the de Broglie wavelength. It’s not surprising when we talk about the wavelength of light or the wavelength of a wave; the wavelength being the distance between two troughs or two peaks of a wave. The length of a wave turns out to be applicable to particles as well, in a particular form we call the de Broglie wavelength. I can calculate the wavelength of a quantum mechanical particle as Planck’s constant divided by the momentum of the particle, which is mass times velocity.
So, what does the wavelength of a particle mean? If I ask, “What’s the wavelength of this marker?” or “What’s the wavelength of some baseball or some physical object?” it’s hard to even understand what that means. Let’s do a few calculations to see what these wavelengths are like.
Let’s say a particle is moving with a velocity of 100 meters per second, and we want to know what its wavelength is. I’ve given you the velocity, we know Planck’s constant, and the only other thing we need to know is the mass. We’ll do this a few different times, first for the smallest particle we can think of, at least in a chemistry class, which would be an electron. The mass of the electron is \(9.1 \times 10^{-31}\) kilograms. That’s a tiny mass.
If we do the full calculation, we can calculate the wavelength. The way we do this is by running through it exactly once: the wavelength is Planck’s constant divided by mass times velocity. Planck’s constant, which is in joule-seconds, divided by the mass \(9.1 \times 10^{-31}\) kilograms, divided by the velocity of 100 meters per second.
To make the units work out, we have to recognize that the joule in Planck’s constant is a composite unit; one joule is one kilogram meter squared per second squared. Once we remember that, if I multiply by seconds, that gets rid of one of these seconds. Dividing by kilograms in the denominator gets rid of kilograms, and dividing by meters per second gets rid of one of these meters and the other seconds. All I’m left with is meters.
If I plug those numbers into a calculator, I find that I get \(7 \times 10^{-6}\) meters, or seven micrometers. So, an electron, according to this equation, has a de Broglie wavelength of seven micrometers when it’s flying around at 100 meters per second. Seven micrometers is small; \(7 \times 10^{-6}\) meters, but it’s certainly a lot larger than an electron itself. The electron itself is no bigger than \(10^{-10}\) meters or so, so that is considerably bigger—many orders of magnitude bigger than the electron itself. The size of the electron, the radius of the electron, will be important in just a second after we consider the next few examples.
Let’s consider another small quantum mechanical object, but not quite as small—a single atom. Maybe a hydrogen atom, or let’s take a somewhat larger atom, the mass of a carbon atom, for example. We know that the mass of a carbon atom is 12 grams per mole. If I convert that to kilograms, convert grams to kilograms, and use Avogadro’s number to get rid of grams from the per mole, that turns out to be about \(1.0 \times 10^{-29}\) kilograms or so, maybe \(1.0 \times 10^{-28}\) kilograms. That’s several thousand times heavier than an electron.
When I use that mass in the de Broglie wavelength calculation, it turns out that the de Broglie wavelength of a carbon atom flying around at 100 meters per second works out to be \(3 \times 10^{-10}\) meters. In SI units, that would be about three angstroms, and a hydrogen atom itself is about half an angstrom in size. So, that’s larger than, or at least on the same order of magnitude as, the atom itself—it’s comparable to the size of the atom.
If I continue with an example that’s a little bit larger, let’s combine several atoms together and make a molecule. It’s not really important what molecule we’re talking about; maybe a benzene molecule or a caffeine molecule, or some molecule made up of a handful of atoms. Let’s say, roughly speaking, I’ve combined a bunch of carbons and hydrogens and other atoms together. That molecule might have a mass of somewhere around 100 grams per mole.
If we calculate the de Broglie wavelength of that molecule, we find that, again, because the mass is in the denominator and this mass is getting larger, the de Broglie wavelength is going to get shorter. So, this de Broglie wavelength has gotten a little bit shorter; the mass has gotten bigger by almost a factor of 10, so the de Broglie wavelength got shorter by almost a factor of 10. It’s now only a fraction of an angstrom. The molecule itself is now several angstroms large because I’ve combined several molecules, each of which might be about an angstrom in size.
This size is now smaller than the original object, so the de Broglie wavelength of a molecule is smaller than the molecule itself when the velocity is about 100 meters per second. As a last example, let’s compare those small quantum mechanical objects—atom, electron size—with something large, something that we can see in the real world, like a baseball, a pen, or a marker. Just as a rough estimate, let’s say we’re talking about something with a mass of 1 kilogram (2.2 pounds or so).
If I plug that number into the equation for the de Broglie wavelength, this is now many orders of magnitude heavier than an individual molecule. What I find is that the de Broglie wavelength has gotten quite a bit shorter. If I use a kilogram moving at 100 meters per second, I find that the de Broglie wavelength of that object is \(1 \times 10^{-36}\) meters—ridiculously small. An atom, remember, is only about \(1 \times 10^{-10}\) meters in size, so this is about 26 orders of magnitude smaller than an individual atom.
The wavelength of this macroscopic object is so small that we can’t even measure it, see it, or detect it in any way. It’s not only smaller; it’s much, much smaller than the original object. That last statement is why it seems a little bit nonsensical for us to talk about the wavelength of a marker or the wavelength of a baseball or something like that. The wavelength itself is so small that we can’t measure it effectively.
What this wavelength means is the same thing it would mean for a water wave, a pressure wave, or a sound wave. It’s the length of the wave that determines how that thing diffracts or interferes with itself. It’s sort of nonsensical to say that if I throw two baseballs at each other and they collide, they can interfere constructively or destructively. Of course, that’s not true; they just collide the way particles do. This is because the delocalization or the interference of those baseballs happens on a size that’s only a tiny fraction of the molecules themselves.
Only undetectable levels do they interfere or diffract off of one another, whereas quantum mechanical objects like molecules, atoms, or electrons have wavelengths that are comparable to, or sometimes much larger than, the objects themselves. They can actually behave more like a wave than a particle if their de Broglie wavelengths are much larger than the size of the particles themselves.
This certainly seems weird at the macroscopic level because we are not used to seeing objects behave that way, but at the quantum-mechanical, small atomic level, things definitely do behave. Matter does behave like a wave. Let me illustrate for you one of the ways we know this is true, or one of the first pieces of evidence we had that illustrated that small objects do, in fact, diffract and interfere the way waves do. This is an experiment called the two-slit experiment.
To start with, I’ll describe what a one-slit experiment would look like when we’re talking about not particles but waves. Let’s imagine a wave, like a wave in the ocean. Imagine a line of waves, a top view of a line of waves that are moving in the ocean, about to break on the shore. Before they reach the shore, they get to a wall that someone has built near the shore that has a gap in it.
What’s going to happen? If the wall were solid, the waves would just crash into the wall and wouldn’t move past it. But if we put a gap in the wall, or a slit in the wall, what happens when the waves reach the slit is that they won’t pass through regions where the wall is solid; they will pass through the gap, but they’ll diffract around that opening. The ripples will move out in the same pattern that you see if you threw a rock into a pond—the ripples would move out in a circular pattern.
That’s how we understand waves behave in a two-slit experiment. If our wall has two gaps in it and again we have waves approaching the wall with the slits, what will happen is the waves will diffract out from these openings. The ripples that get formed when the waves diffract out of these openings will begin to overlap themselves at some point. The places where I’ve drawn the troughs or peaks of these waves occurring at the same place will be sites of constructive interference, where the wave will be twice as high as it would be if there were only one wave from a single slit.
There will be various places where there’s constructive interference and various other places where there’s destructive interference, where the peak of one wave overlaps with the trough of another wave. So, we’ll see constructive and destructive interference the way we’d expect normal waves to behave.
Now, things get a little bit weird when I imagine what should happen and what actually does happen for particles. These are waves moving towards a wall with some gaps in it. I can also show what happens when particles arrive at a wall with gaps in it. Let’s imagine we’re throwing baseballs or shooting bullets at a wall with a gap in it.
Instead of a lot of waves, I have a lot of particles that I’m sending toward this gap. Certainly, what we’d expect to happen is that a particle that hits the wall won’t pass through, but a particle that arrives at the gap will pass straight through. For one slit, things aren’t too different than what you’d expect. If I then draw another wall back here and ask where these particles hit on the wall, you’d expect them to hit only in places that allowed them to pass through the slit. For the most part, that’s what you see. Occasionally, you’ll get one hitting a little further away because it gets bent or diffracted as it passes through the slit, but that’s not too weird yet.
Where it gets really strange is in the two-slit experiment. If I have particles being sent toward this wall with two gaps and I ask where I find that the particles land after having passed through these slits, again what you’d expect is that maybe particles can pass through here and can pass through here, so they ought to land here and they ought to land here. But that’s not what we see. We see perhaps some particles landing there, but we might see some particles landing here, maybe even more particles landing here.
So, I’m drawing a little histogram or diagram of where we see these particles, and what we see is a series of peaks and valleys. There are certain places where a lot of particles hit, while other places have relatively few particles hitting, and there are some places where no particles hit at all. The only way to understand how that can happen is if these particles, as they pass through the wall, don’t fly through like bullets. Instead, they diffract through the slit and constructively and destructively interfere with one another. The places where we find a lot of particles on this wall are where there’s a lot of constructive interference, while the places where we never find them are where there’s complete destructive interference.
If the particles were purely particles, macroscopic bullets or baseballs wouldn’t behave that way, but small atoms and electrons do, in fact, behave that way. The only way we can understand that they do is the fact that these electrons and atoms behave as much like waves as they do like particles and can constructively and destructively interfere with each other.
That was one of the first experiments that let us know for sure that small objects do, in fact, behave like waves. This completes our tour of the quantum mechanical properties at the qualitative level that you may already know something about. Our next step will be to get a little more quantitative and to understand quantum mechanics at the level of equations and numbers so that we can begin to make concrete predictions.

title: 12  Harmonic Oscillator Model
text: Harmonic Oscillator Model (8:37)

title: 12  Harmonic Oscillator Model
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 12  Harmonic Oscillator Model
section: Harmonic Oscillator Model Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator Model Video Transcript
So far, in trying to understand how diatomic molecules behave, we have considered two different components of their motion: their translational motion, as their center of mass moves around in the container they occupy, and their rotational motion. We have described the translational motion using the particle-in-a-box model, which allows us to describe the coordinates \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\).
Let me go ahead and draw a diatomic molecule. Its center of mass is somewhere, and that center of mass has some \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) coordinates. The rotation of the molecule, as it rotates in various directions, is described using the angles \(\theta\) and \(\phi\). We have allowed the molecule to move around in \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\), and we have allowed it to rotate in \(\theta\) and \(\phi\). However, what we have not examined so far is the changes in the bond length of the molecule.
The bond length is the variable we need to describe the vibration of the molecule. As the bond length changes, we refer to that motion as vibration. We need to understand how to quantum mechanically describe the states of the molecule and, eventually, the thermodynamic properties of the molecule due to those vibrations. So, that is the next thing we will tackle.
We are going to back up all the way to quantum mechanics and solve Schrödinger’s equation again—not for the particle in a box to describe the motion of the molecule, nor for the rigid rotor to describe the orientation of the molecule, but for the bond length to describe these vibrational motions.
The first step we always need to take in any quantum mechanical problem is to write down the potential energy. We cannot write down Schrödinger’s equation until we know what potential energy to plug into that equation. The potential energy will depend on this variable \(r\), the bond length.
Remember that in the previous two approaches, both for the particle in a box and the rigid rotor, we started out by saying, “Well, it may not be exactly true, but let’s say the potential energy is equal to zero.” Can we do that for the vibration of a molecule? We definitely cannot. If you think about how the potential energy of the molecule changes as I change its bond length, it becomes clear.
For a diatomic molecule, if I stretch it or compress the bond, the potential energy of the molecule will definitely change. Unlike rotation, where I should not expect the energy of the molecule to differ if I simply place it at a different orientation, or translation, where moving it to a different position in the box does not change its energy, the situation is different for bond length.
If I draw a qualitative picture of what the potential energy should look like as I change the bond length, I have a molecule at some bond length. If I stretch the bond, I will have to put energy into the molecule to do so, which raises the energy of the molecule. Likewise, if I compress it below the bond length that it naturally tends to have, that will also require energy.
There is some bond length, which I will denote as \(r_e\), where the energy of the molecule is lowest. If I increase the bond length beyond that or decrease it below that, the energy will rise from that point. So, there is a minimum energy, which I will draw approximately in the right place and call \(V_e\) at \(r_e\). The “e” stands for equilibrium. If I let the molecule alone, the bond length it naturally tends to have is this equilibrium bond length, which corresponds to the lowest energy.
The energy goes up as I stretch the bond, but it does not keep increasing indefinitely. Eventually, if I keep stretching the bond, the molecule will break. If I have two atoms separated by a long distance and move them further apart, I am not changing their energy anymore. Thus, this energy will rise and approach zero as I have drawn the graph.
On the other side, if I consider what happens when I compress the bond, I have molecules at their equilibrium bond length. If I move them closer together, that raises their energy. If I continue to move them closer, I will eventually start overlapping the two atoms, which makes it energetically difficult to do so. Therefore, as the bond length approaches zero, the energy will begin to rise very rapidly and become very large.
This is a general qualitative shape for what the potential energy should look like, and it is not a very good approximation to say that I can model that curve with just a flat \(V = 0\) curve. We will have to do a little more work to determine the quantum mechanics this time. We will need to figure out what this potential energy curve looks like and how to insert it into Schrödinger’s equation.
The bad news is that it is not actually known. I cannot write down an equation for what this curve looks like for real-world molecules. If I could, it would be quite a complicated expression, and if I inserted it into Schrödinger’s equation, that equation would be very difficult to solve—certainly on a blackboard without using very powerful computers.
What we will do, as physical chemists often do when faced with a problem that cannot be solved, is come up with a model, an approximation, a simplification. Essentially, we are saying, “I cannot solve this problem, so let me think of a simpler problem that I will be able to solve.”
If I cannot solve the problem for this green curve that I have just drawn, I will draw a simpler curve that resembles it. The curve I have just drawn is a parabola. As the distance away from \(r_e\) increases, let me make it increase symmetrically. If I make it increase as the square of the distance away from \(r_e\), then what I have drawn is a parabola with some constant out front.
This expression I have just drawn for the potential energy is a simpler equation, and it turns out that when we use that for the potential energy in Schrödinger’s equation, we will be able to solve it. It is not terribly different from the original expression, especially near the bottom. We will see as we go along that usually, for diatomic molecules, they spend most of their time near the equilibrium bond length.
Thus, this approximation is not actually too bad. We will simplify this expression a little bit by defining a new variable \(x\) to be the quantity \(r - r_e\), which represents how much I have stretched the bond. When \(x\) is a positive number, I have stretched the bond to a larger bond length than the equilibrium bond length. When \(x\) is negative, I have compressed the bond to less than its equilibrium bond length.
This quantity is called the bond displacement, which indicates how much I have displaced the bond away from its equilibrium bond length. With that change, I have a potential energy that depends not on bond length but now on this new variable \(x\), the bond displacement I have just defined. This is the expression we will use and insert into Schrödinger’s equation to solve for the quantum mechanical behavior of the vibrations of these diatomic molecules.
That model is called the harmonic oscillator model, and that name may sound a little cryptic. It is not necessarily obvious what is harmonic or what is oscillating in this particular model. You might think we would call it the vibrational model or something similar. The next step will be to explain a little more about this model and why it is called the harmonic oscillator model.

title: 13  Classical Harmonic Oscillator
text: Classical Harmonic Oscillator (10:47)

title: 13  Classical Harmonic Oscillator
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 13  Classical Harmonic Oscillator
section: Classical Harmonic Oscillator Video Transcript
text:
Classical Harmonic Oscillator Video Transcript
We are mainly interested in using the harmonic oscillator model to treat the vibration of diatomic molecules. Any time we have a potential energy that looks like some variable squared, it ends up being called the harmonic oscillator model. It’s worth spending a few minutes explaining why this model is called a harmonic oscillator and what it looks like when you’re not solving quantum mechanical problems with it.
This expression for potential energy looks like the square of some variable. Let me draw what that potential energy would look like in graphical form. There is some variable, which for us might be a bond length but could generally represent many different things. The potential energy will look like the square of that variable, increasing symmetrically on either side of zero for that variable, resembling a parabola. Any variable that behaves like this, whose potential energy behaves like the square of that variable, will act essentially like a classical Hooke’s Law spring.
For example, consider a spring with an object at the end, maybe attached to a wall. If I stretch that spring and let it go, it will snap back and then oscillate back and forth. If I compress the spring, it will snap forward and then oscillate back and forth. The reason for this behavior on the potential energy curve is that if I move the value of \(x\) to some positive number where the energy is large, it will be able to reduce that positive energy by moving the \(x\) coordinate smaller, and the particle will slide down this potential energy well, sliding back up to a point on the other side where the potential energy is equal to where it started. Likewise, if I compress it, it will roll back and forth.
I can think of it as a spring, or as placing a ball inside of a parabolic bowl that will roll back and forth, or as a diatomic molecule, which is a lot like a spring. The reason we call it a Hooke’s Law spring is that the more I stretch it, the larger the restoring force gets, and the restoring force is proportional to the amount of stretch. Mathematically, what we are saying is that the force, if I stretch this spring, is the negative derivative of potential energy with respect to position. Since I know what \(V(x)\) is, I can write:
[ F = - = -( k x^2) = -kx ]
This is our Hooke’s Law spring. If I double the stretch in the spring, I have doubled the restoring force in the negative direction that the particle feels. The other thing we should note is that this constant \(k\), when we talk about springs, is called the force constant. A strong, stiff spring, one that is very hard to stretch because the energy rises quickly as I stretch it, has a large value of \(k\) or a large force constant. A very soft spring with a low force constant would be one that is very easy to stretch, like a slinky, for example.
We also know about the behavior of a system like this that relates to force.
