The other physics equation that you may be familiar with is \(F = ma\), which relates mass times acceleration, or if we prefer, mass times the second derivative of position with respect to time. Since the variable we use for mass when discussing the harmonic oscillator is not \(m\) but typically the reduced mass of our object, we will write this as \(\mu \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}\).
So, we have two equations for force: the force for this potential energy must be \(F = -kx\), and force is also equal to the mass (or reduced mass) times the second derivative of \(x\). If I equate those two terms, we have:
[ -kx = ]
Let’s rearrange this to have the second derivative on the left side:
[ = - x ]
What must be true about any system with this quadratic potential energy is that the second derivative of position, the acceleration, has to be equal to negative the force constant divided by mass times the original \(x\). The \(x\) we are discussing is the \(x\) that we are taking the time derivative of. When I illustrate this by stretching the spring to a certain point and letting it go, I stretch it to some initial value of \(x\) where it has some initial potential energy. But when I let it go, the force on the spring will cause its position to change, and it will go back to its initial position, compressing and stretching back and forth.
This oscillation back and forth changes the value of \(x\) as a function of time, so \(x\) will change over time in a way that oscillates. The second derivative of that function with respect to time must equal the original function itself multiplied by some negative constant. This equation should seem familiar; it’s exactly the same differential equation we solved when we considered the one-dimensional particle in a box. We were looking for some wave function whose second derivative is negative the original function. There, we took \(x\) derivatives and called the function \(\psi\), but the differential equation is exactly the same.
Building off of what you’ve done in a physics class or a differential equations class, we can say that this \(x(t)\) function, the only functions whose second derivatives are the negative of the original functions, are cosines and sines. So, we can express it as some amount of cosine plus some amount of sine function, depending on \(t\). We can also have some variable inside the sine function along with them, so we write:
[ x(t) = A (t) + B (t) ]
where \(\omega\) is the angular frequency. Cosines and sines, when I take their second derivatives, will return to me the negative of the original values, pulling out some factors of \(\omega\) as I take those derivatives. In fact, if I solve for what \(\omega\) must be, such that if I take two derivatives, pulling out an \(\omega\) and then a minus \(\omega\), we find:
[ ^2 = ]
Thus,
[ = ]
The angular frequency with which this particle oscillates, after I have stretched it to some positive position and let it go, will oscillate back and forth like a cosine or sine wave.
We are often interested not in the angular frequency but in the frequency. The difference between these is that angular frequency measures how many radians per unit time, while frequency measures the number of round trips per unit time. The only difference between angular frequency and frequency is this factor of \(2\pi\) radians per complete round trip in a circle. To convert angular frequency into regular frequency, I divide by \(2\pi\):
[ = ]
Let’s denote this frequency, thinking ahead for the harmonic oscillator, because we will be talking about the reduced mass of a diatomic molecule. The frequency we will discuss for diatomic molecules will be the equilibrium vibrational frequency, typically labeled with a subscript \(e\). Thus, we have:
[ _e = ]
This explains the name “harmonic oscillator.” We have clarified why the molecule oscillates; “harmonic” just means it is oscillating as a pure sine or cosine wave with a single frequency—a term related to music. This has all been a classical, purely Newtonian physics description of the oscillation of this system with a quadratic, parabolic, or harmonic potential energy function. The next step will be to plug that into Schrödinger’s equation and see how it behaves in a quantum mechanical system, allowing us to describe the vibration of diatomic molecules.

title: 14  Harmonic Oscillator Schrödinger Equation
text: Harmonic Oscillator Schrödinger Equation (4:17)

title: 14  Harmonic Oscillator Schrödinger Equation
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 14  Harmonic Oscillator Schrödinger Equation
section: Harmonic Oscillator Schrödinger Equation Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator Schrödinger Equation Video Transcript
Okay, so let’s put some pieces together and use our harmonic oscillator model. The potential energy for a harmonic oscillator will be inserted into the Schrödinger equation as we begin to solve that quantum mechanical problem. We know the Schrödinger equation in a couple of different forms, which are different ways of looking at the same equation. The Hamiltonian acts on a wave function to give us back an energy times the same wave function. The Hamiltonian always has the form
[ - ]
where \(m\) for a diatomic molecule is the reduced mass. This represents our kinetic energy term. We add to that the potential energy, multiplying the wave function, and that should equal energy times the wave function if that function indeed solves Schrödinger’s equation.
For us, particularly when talking about the harmonic oscillator, our potential energy has the form
[ k x^2. ]
So, I’ll just rewrite that one more time: our potential energy looks like
[ k x^2 ]
multiplying a wave function.
Here’s the version of Schrödinger’s equation for the harmonic oscillator. I’ve used the harmonic oscillator potential energy
[ k x^2. ]
Notice that the function we are solving Schrödinger’s equation for is a function of \(x\); it is a one-dimensional function. Each of these wave functions is just \(\psi(x)\), and Schrödinger’s equation tells us we are looking for a function that, if we take these derivatives and multiply by the constants times the second derivative, add it to a quadratic function multiplying the wave function, we should get back the original function.
So, that is the differential equation we need to solve. This one has a different character than the ones we solved before because of the \(x^2\) in the potential energy. That is the next task we have to solve, and we will do that in a separate video lecture.
But let me encourage you, before you get to that next video lecture, to ask yourself: what would that function look like? What types of functions are there that, if we take their second derivatives and add them to \(x^2\) times the function, some things cancel and we get back the original function?
For example, if you want some things to try, we have seen before that \(\sin(x)\) or \(\sin(a x)\) seemed to be good at solving the rigid rotor and the particle in a box function. So, is that what our wave function should look like? Alternatively, maybe the wave function looks like some polynomial.
Let’s say, for instance, \(\sin(a x)\) or \(a x^3\) or \(a x^4\) times a different constant. So, maybe \(\psi\) is a polynomial; it could involve \(x^4, x^3, x^2\), or some polynomial involving powers of \(x\). That is another typical option for what a function might look like.
You can also consider exponential functions, \(e^{a x}\). Some function of this type is going to end up being useful to solve Schrödinger’s equation. I encourage you, before you move on to the next video, to go ahead and take some second derivatives, add them to \(x^2\) times these functions, or at least do as much of that math as you need to decide whether this will or won’t solve Schrödinger’s equation with the harmonic oscillator potential energy.
After you’ve done that, I’ll see you in the next video lecture, where we’ll explore what these solutions do in fact look like.

title: 15  Harmonic Oscillator Solutions (Qualitative)
text: Harmonic Oscillator Solutions (Qualitative) (13:17)

title: 15  Harmonic Oscillator Solutions (Qualitative)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 15  Harmonic Oscillator Solutions (Qualitative)
section: Harmonic Oscillator Solutions (Qualitative) Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator Solutions (Qualitative) Video Transcript
So now it’s time to talk about which wave functions actually solve the harmonic oscillator Schrödinger equation. What functions are there that we can take the second derivative of, add them to \(x^2\) times the same function with various constants thrown in, and get back the function that we started with? We can consider at least some trial and error solutions to the Schrödinger equation and learn something about what type of functions will solve it.
We can start with a relatively simple equation. Let’s just take a function like \(x^3\) or \(x^5\) or \(x^n\) for some power \(n\). We could also consider a polynomial with multiple terms, but if we start simple, let’s just take one term. We can throw a constant in front of that, and then we will need the first derivative of that function, as well as the second derivative, in order to plug it into Schrödinger’s equation.
Taking them one at a time, the first derivative of that function is going to look like \(3a x^2\), and then the second derivative will pull down the constant and the \(x^2\) down to just \(x\), pulling down the 2 into the prefactor. Now I’ve got \(6a x\) as the second derivative. If we go to plug those into Schrödinger’s equation, I have some constants multiplying the second derivative of the wave function. So that looks like more constants times \(x\), and to that, I want to add some constants, \(\frac{1}{2} k\), multiplying \(x^2\) times the wave function.
So \(x^2\) times the original \(x^3\) gives me a total of \(x^5\). Thus, constants times \(x\) plus constants times \(x^5\) does not equal constants times the original function, which is \(x^3\). So is it true that a linear function plus a fifth-order term equals a cubic term? Definitely not; the \(x\) terms don’t match in this expression. This means that this guess at a solution failed. If we start with a function like \(x^3\), the reason it didn’t work is that the second derivative knocked the power of \(x\) down by 2 to just \(x\). This \(x^2\) raises it by 2 to \(x^5\). Neither of those matches the \(x^3\) that we had in our original function, so we’re going to have to try something different. Just \(x^n\) is not going to work.
We can try something else. Part of this problem was that the second derivative kept knocking down the power of \(x\), and we wanted eventually to be equal to the original function. We certainly know some functions that don’t get knocked down in powers as we take the derivatives. Those are functions like \(e^{ax}\) or \(e^{bx}\). If I take derivatives of an exponential, the first derivative just pulls down the derivative of the exponent, pulling down a \(b\). The second derivative is going to pull down another factor of \(b\).
So why don’t I just say \(\psi'' = ab^2 e^{bx}\)? If I plug those into Schrödinger’s equation to see if they work, the second derivative looks like \(e^{bx}\) on the right side. I’ve got constants times the original function \(e^{bx}\), so that’s good news; this term is matching this term. That looks good so far. But when I include this potential energy term \(\frac{1}{2} k x^2\) times the wave function, I’ve got constants. The \(x^2\) I can’t lump in with the constants; that’s part of the \(x\)-dependent term. So I’ve got \(x^2 e^{bx}\).
That’s bad. Better than we had in our first try, this \(e^{bx}\) matches this \(e^{bx}\), but the \(x^2\) from the potential energy term is causing problems. So we’re going to have to work a little bit harder to figure out a function that has a chance of working. If we think about what went right and what went wrong in this solution, it was good that our function didn’t get knocked down in powers of \(x\) by taking the derivatives, but what we really need is something whose second derivative gets knocked up by a couple of powers of \(x\) so that these two terms have some terms that match each other or perhaps cancel each other.
If we think about it a little bit, we can find a function that does that. What I’m looking for is a function that, if I take derivatives, pulls down not just extra constants in front of the function but extra factors of \(x\) in front of the original function. I can do that with a function like an exponential again, but now instead of \(e^{bx}\), I’m going to have \(e^{b x^2}\). I’m going to have more than one power of \(x\) in the exponent, so that when I take the derivative of the exponent, it’s going to pull down some factors of \(x\) out front.
In particular, the first derivative is going to be \(a e^{b x^2}\) times the derivative of the exponent. The derivative of \(b x^2\) is \(2bx\), so that’s doing what I wanted it to. The first derivative has pulled down a factor of \(x\). The second derivative gets a little more complicated because now I’ve got two factors of \(x\) to worry about: the \(x\) in front of the exponential and the \(x^2\) in the exponential. But the product rule tells me the derivative of this function is going to look like \(2ab e^{b x^2}\) (taking the derivative with respect to \(x\) of this polynomial term in front of the exponential) plus \(2ab x e^{b x^2}\) times the derivative of the exponent.
So I’m going to pull down another factor of \(2bx\). The 2 becomes a 4, and the \(b\) becomes \(b^2\). So that’s what I get for my second derivative of this function. I’m sorry; it pulls down \(2b\) and it pulls down another factor of \(x\). Yes, so \(2 \times b \times x\) in addition to the original \(2\) and \(b\) and \(x\). This is the second derivative of this function, and it’s done what I claimed we wanted to happen. When I take the second derivative, I get some \(x^2\) factors pulled down; they’re going to match this term, and I also have something that looks like the original function that’s going to match the term on the right side.
So plugging those all into Schrödinger’s equation again, we’ve got constants times the second derivative of the wave function giving me \(e^{b x^2}\), and also some constants times \(x^2 e^{b x^2}\) from the original wave function. That is supposed to be equal to energy or some constants times the original wave function. So is it true that \(e^{b x^2}\) with some constants in front plus \(x^2 e^{b x^2}\) is equal to \(e^{b x^2}\)?
At first glance, that might look like it’s not true. I have some terms on the left, this \(x^2 e^{b x^2}\), that don’t match any terms on the right. But depending on what these constants are, if the value of this constant is not zero, that’s just some particular value. The value of this constant and the value of this constant, when I add them together, can cancel each other. If there’s some cancellation that occurs and causes these two terms to cancel each other, then I’m left with something that can work.
So at least if we choose our constants right, if we choose the value of this \(b\) constant, this function can solve Schrödinger’s equation, whereas these first two cases that we talked about cannot solve it for the harmonic oscillator. So this is our candidate solution. Possibly, functions that look like \(e^{b x^2}\) are going to solve Schrödinger’s equation.
What does that mean physically? If I draw a graph of what that wave function looks like, what would the wave function look like? So \(c e^{b x^2}\) (where \(c\) is some constant) is what we have. We don’t know what the constant is yet, but certainly, we know what the function \(e^{x}\) or \(e^{x^2}\) looks like. If it’s \(e^{x^2}\), then the function is going to look like this: the larger \(x\) gets, the larger \(x^2\) gets, and the function is going to rise pretty quickly as I move away from \(x = 0\).
That begins to look a little strange. Remember what the wave function means. In order for us to interpret what the wave function means, remember that the probability of finding the particle at some point on the \(x\)-axis is proportional to the wave function squared. So that means I get more and more likely to find the particle at \(x\) values as \(x\) gets either very large or very small, and that probability reaches infinity as the value of \(x\) approaches infinity.
That sounds like it doesn’t make any physical sense. I can’t have an infinite probability of finding the particle at any location. But the good news is I haven’t decided what this constant \(b\) is yet. It doesn’t have to be a positive constant. So this graph is what the function would look like if \(b\) were a positive number. If \(b\) is a negative number, then this is going to look like \(e^{-c x^2}\) for some constant \(c\). That sounds like a Gaussian function or a bell curve.
So we know what Gaussian functions look like. If \(b < 0\), then the wave function is going to look like this, and now the good news is the further \(x\) gets away from the origin, the smaller the wave function gets. Thus, the smaller the probability of finding the particle at that distance. Likewise, the area under this curve or the area under the \(\psi^2\) curve is bounded and finite, so we have a chance of normalizing this function and making the probability equal to one. If I ask what’s the probability of finding it anywhere under this curve, what that leads us to conclude is that a possibly valid version of the wave function would be some constant times \(e^{-\alpha x^2}\).
If I rename my constants instead of calling them \(a\) and \(b\), I’ll go ahead and start using \(n\) for the normalization constant out front, \(e^{-\frac{\alpha}{2} x^2}\). In particular, so that we don’t end up with this problem of these twos proliferating every time I take a derivative, \(x^2\) causes some twos to fall down out of the exponent. Either one or more of those factors of two show up as I take derivatives. To avoid the arithmetic complications when those factors of 2 show up, I’m going to make the exponent half as big as I had in the original definition.
So still a constant times \(e^{-\frac{\alpha}{2} x^2}\), my constant is just now negative. This thing I’m calling \(\alpha\) is equal to negative some other constant. I’ll change its name to \(\alpha\). In particular, this is at least a reasonable guess for a function that will solve the Schrödinger equation for the harmonic oscillator. To find out whether it does in fact solve Schrödinger’s equation and what this value of \(\alpha\) needs to be, what the value of \(n\) needs to be, we need to do a little more work and actually plug this into Schrödinger’s equation and not be so cavalier with the constants that we cancel. And that’s what we’ll do next.

title: 16  Harmonic Oscillator Wavefunction (Ground State)
text: Harmonic Oscillator Wavefunction (Ground State) (10:07)

title: 16  Harmonic Oscillator Wavefunction (Ground State)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 16  Harmonic Oscillator Wavefunction (Ground State)
section: Harmonic Oscillator Wavefunction (Ground State) Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator Wavefunction (Ground State) Video Transcript
If we have the harmonic oscillator version of the Schrödinger equation and we’ve convinced ourselves that functions of this form ought to be solutions to the Schrödinger equation, let’s convince ourselves even further that this is indeed the case. We can do this by plugging this wave function into both the kinetic energy term and the potential energy term on the right side of the equation. To do that, we need to take derivatives of this function.
The first derivative of the wave function shows that the derivative of an exponential is an exponential times the same constant out front, multiplied by the derivative of the exponent. The derivative of \(x^2\) is \(2x\), and I have a negative \(\alpha\) divided by 2. The 2 from \(x^2\) and the 2 from dividing by 2 cancel each other out, leaving me with a total of, allowing room for my negative sign, \(-2\alpha n x e^{-\alpha x^2}\). I’ve pulled down an extra factor of \(-\alpha x\) by taking the derivative of this exponent, which gives us \(-n \alpha x e^{-\alpha x^2}\).
For the second derivative, the derivative of this derivative again pulls down another factor of \(-\alpha x\). Now I have \(\alpha^2 x^2 n e^{-\alpha x^2}\). Additionally, taking the derivative of \(x\) out in front and leaving the rest alone gives me \(-\alpha e^{-\alpha x^2}/2\). So that should be the second derivative of our hypothesized wave function.
If I look a little more closely at that, I see that \(n e^{-\alpha x^2/2}\) shows up in this derivative. Let’s simplify that a bit by writing it as \(\alpha^2 x^2\) times the original wave function minus \(\alpha\) times the original wave function. This will help when we go to plug the second derivative into the Schrödinger equation.
When we do that, the Schrödinger equation becomes:
[ - ( ^2 x^2 - ) + k x^2 = E . ]
Let’s collect some terms. We have constants times \(x^2\) times \(\psi\) and other terms that look like constants times the wave function. If we collect the terms with similar powers of \(x\), the first term in parentheses here, \(-\text{constants} \times \alpha^2\), becomes \(-\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu} \alpha^2\), which will multiply \(x^2\) and the wave function. I also have another term that multiplies \(x^2\) and the wave function, which is \(\frac{1}{2} k\).
So, I’ve collected the two terms that look like \(x^2\) times the wave function. In addition to that, I have a term that looks like constants times the wave function. The negative times negative leaves a positive sign, giving us \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu} \alpha\) times the wave function. All of this should equal \(E\) times the wave function.
Is it true that the stuff on the left equals the stuff on the right? Constants times the wave function could easily equal constants times the wave function if we have the appropriate value for \(E\). However, this equality is only true if the stuff in parentheses cancels each other out, leaving the term in parentheses equal to zero so that the \(x^2\) times \(\psi\) term is zero.
If this whole first term is zero, then indeed the Schrödinger equation is satisfied. We need the term in parentheses to be equal to zero. The only term in here that we have any control over is the constant in the wave function that we are trying to write down as a solution for the Schrödinger equation. We get to choose the value of \(\alpha\).
If we rearrange to make the term in parentheses equal to zero, solving for \(\alpha\), we find that \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu} \alpha^2\) must equal \(\frac{1}{2} k\). Rearranging and solving for \(\alpha^2\), we get:
[ ^2 = k. ]
This simplifies to
[ ^2 = k . ]
To find \(\alpha\), we take the square root of both sides. The square root of 4 and \(\pi^2\) and \(h^2\) are straightforward, while \(k\) and \(\mu\) give us the square root of \(k \mu\).
What I’ve determined here is specifically what the value of \(\alpha\) must be. Not just any \(e^{-\alpha x^2/2}\) will be a solution to the Schrödinger equation; only if \(\alpha\) is equal to this collection of constants will the \(x^2\) times wave function term from the kinetic energy cancel the \(x^2\) times wave function term from the potential energy. If that cancellation happens, then this function will solve the Schrödinger equation with that particular value of \(\alpha\).
In the process of inserting this wave function into the Schrödinger equation, we’ve also determined the value of \(E\). This is a solution to the Schrödinger equation only if \(E\) is equal to this collection of constants. Therefore, the energy of the wave function that we’ve now determined is:
[ E = . ]
Now that we know what \(\alpha\) is, we can substitute it in:
[ E = . ]
A lot of cancellation will occur here. We have \(2\) divided by \(8\), which leaves us with \(4\) in the denominator. We have \(\pi\) divided by \(\pi^2\), leaving \(\pi\) in the denominator. Also, \(h^2\) divided by \(h\) gives us one factor of \(h\) in the numerator.
For \(k\), there is no cancellation, but for \(\mu\), we have a square root of \(\mu\) in the numerator and dividing by \(\mu\) in the denominator, which gives us \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu}}\) after some cancellation.
If I rearrange that a little, I will write it as:
[ = . ]
For reasons that will become clear shortly, I have a factor of \(h\) and \(\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}\). This should look somewhat familiar; \(\frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}\) is what we called the equilibrium vibrational frequency for the classical harmonic oscillator.
Thus, I can write this now as:
[ E = h , ]
where the quantity in pink has just been rewritten as the vibrational frequency \(\nu\). The energy of this wave function that we’ve written down, when \(\alpha\) takes on this particular value, is \(\frac{1}{2}\) times Planck’s constant times the vibrational frequency of that same oscillator.
This combination of the wave function, this constant, and this energy now compose a solution for the harmonic oscillator. This wave function solves the harmonic oscillator wave function with this particular energy. You may already be wondering if this is the only solution, similar to other quantum mechanical problems where there are more than one solution. You probably won’t be surprised when I tell you that there is indeed more than one solution. This is one solution, but there is a whole family of solutions that are somewhat similar to this function. The next step will be to explore what those other solutions to the Schrödinger equation look like.

title: 17  Hermite Polynomials
text: Hermite Polynomials (9:20)

title: 17  Hermite Polynomials
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 17  Hermite Polynomials
section: Hermite Polynomials Video Transcript
text:
Hermite Polynomials Video Transcript
We know one function, \(\psi(x) = e^{-x^2}\) with some constants, that solves the harmonic oscillator Schrödinger equation. When we insert this equation for \(\psi\) into the Schrödinger equation, the left side is equal to the right side. The primary reason this happens is that when we take the second derivative, it pulls down two extra powers of \(x\), which end up canceling the \(x^2\) times the wave function in the potential energy term. This feature—pulling down two extra powers of \(x\)—is a characteristic of the \(e^{-x^2}\) term, and it would occur with many other mathematical functions as well.
For example, if I write down a function that looks like \(n \cdot x \cdot e^{-\alpha x^2 / 2}\), it is also true that this function solves the Schrödinger equation. We won’t do the derivatives here, but you can convince yourself of this by taking the derivatives yourself. When I take the second derivative of this function, despite having some \(x\) terms in front and some \(x\) terms in the exponent, I will end up with terms that have pulled down two extra powers of \(x\) into the polynomial prefactor term. These can cancel with the two powers of \(x\) multiplying the wave function in the potential energy term, making this a valid solution to Schrödinger’s equation.
Likewise, there are functions that look like \(x^2 \cdot e^{-\alpha x^2 / 2}\) with normalization constants in front. This one gets a little more complicated because there is now some cancellation that needs to happen between the various product rule terms involved in taking this derivative. Because of that extra cancellation, if I use \(4\alpha\) and \(x^2\) along with a constant term, this function also solves the harmonic oscillator Schrödinger equation.
As you can see, there is a whole series of solutions: one with just a constant multiplying this Gaussian, one with a linear term in \(x\) multiplying the Gaussian, and one with a quadratic function of \(x\) multiplying the Gaussian. There are also cubic and quartic functions, and so on. In general, we can assign numbers to these solutions. We call the first one the zeroth solution because the polynomial in front of the Gaussian is a zeroth-order polynomial. The linear function, or first-order polynomial, is the \(n = 1\) solution. There is an \(n = 2\) solution, and in general, for any \(n\), I can write a solution that looks like a normalization constant times a polynomial.
We will discuss the features of that polynomial shortly. In fact, I will write that polynomial not just as a polynomial of \(x\), but you will notice that every time we see an \(x\), it is paired with some \(\alpha\). So, this \(x^2\) is paired with an \(\alpha\); these \(x^2\) terms are paired with an \(\alpha\). Every time we see an \(x\), it is paired with \(\alpha^{1/2}\). Thus, this polynomial is a function of \(\alpha^{1/2} \cdot x\), all multiplying the Gaussian term \(e^{-\alpha x^2 / 2}\). This is the same for every one of these solutions.
The polynomial term is different for each case, and the normalization constant will also differ for each case. These represent the general form of the solutions to the harmonic oscillator Schrödinger equation. To write down any particular solution, there are a couple of approaches. You could write out a polynomial. If I want to know the \(n = 5\) solution, I would need to determine which fifth-order polynomial should be placed in front of this Gaussian to solve the Schrödinger equation.
We could do it the hard way by writing down a fifth-order polynomial, plugging it into the Schrödinger equation, and figuring out what constants need to cancel other constants. However, now that I have written the general form of this polynomial, or at least named it, we can refer to this polynomial as a Hermite polynomial. This function was well-studied and understood by mathematicians before anyone realized it was useful in quantum mechanical problems.
Notice that I have written a Hermite polynomial as a function of some arbitrary argument. Normally, I would say \(H(x)\), but for the harmonic oscillator problem, \(x\) has a very specific meaning: it is the bond displacement. Here, I am just writing \(H\) as a function of some argument. We can write down the first few of these Hermite polynomials to get a sense of what they look like.
The zeroth polynomial, as we have already seen, is just a constant, and its value is one. The first Hermite polynomial, \(H_1\), is twice the argument of the function. If I insert \(\alpha^{1/2} x\) as the argument, the \(n = 1\) polynomial becomes \(2 \alpha^{1/2} x\). The \(n = 2\) polynomial is \(4 \cdot (\alpha^{1/2} x)^2 - 2\). We can also write the \(n = 3\) version of this polynomial, which is \(8 \cdot (\alpha^{1/2} x) - 8 \cdot (\alpha^{1/2} x)^3 - 12 \cdot (\alpha^{1/2} x)\).
This series continues, but these examples are only useful if we do not want to go beyond \(n = 3\). What is useful is to be able to predict what the \(n\)-th polynomial is, and we can do that with a recursion formula. If we want to know the \(n + 1\) polynomial, it will be twice the variable we are discussing, multiplied by the previous polynomial, plus or minus twice the number \(n\) times the polynomial prior to that.
This recursion formula tells me the \(n + 1\) polynomial in terms of the \(n\)-th and the \(n - 1\)-th. If I want to know the fourth polynomial, I can plug in the second and third polynomials here and use that to solve for the \(n = 4\) polynomial.
Another important aspect of these polynomials is their parity. If we look carefully, we can see that the \(n = 1\) and \(n = 3\) polynomials have their arguments raised to the first and third powers, respectively. Only the odd powers of the argument show up in the \(n = 1\) and \(n = 3\) cases, so these functions have odd parity. They only include odd powers of their argument.
In contrast, the even values of \(n\)—0, 2, 4, 6, and so on—include only even powers of their argument. For example, \(H_2\) has not only the argument squared term but also an argument to the zero term, but it does not include an argument to the first term. Thus, when \(n\) is an odd number, the polynomial includes only odd powers of the argument, while when \(n\) is an even number, it includes only even powers of the argument. This distinction will be important as we delve further into discussing the parity of the wave functions. Knowing what the Hermite polynomial is and how to predict it for any arbitrary value of \(n\) will now allow us to write down the wave function for any arbitrary value of \(n\) as well.

title: 18  Harmonic Oscillator - Orthonormality
text: Harmonic Oscillator - Orthonormality (12:38)

title: 18  Harmonic Oscillator - Orthonormality
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 18  Harmonic Oscillator - Orthonormality
section: Harmonic Oscillator - Orthonormality Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator - Orthonormality Video Transcript
The general form of the harmonic oscillator wave functions is a polynomial in \(x\), specifically a Hermite polynomial, multiplied by a Gaussian function with carefully chosen constants to ensure it is an actual solution to the Schrödinger equation. We haven’t yet discussed the normalization constant in front of these wave functions. As usual, once we obtain a solution to the Schrödinger equation, we can guarantee that the function is normalized. We can also investigate whether the family of solutions is mutually orthogonal. That’s what we’ll do next.
Normalization involves ensuring that if I integrate the probability of finding the particle over all positions, it must equal one. Probability is given by the wave function squared, which involves the complex conjugate. However, we don’t need to worry about that too much in this case because neither the Hermite polynomial nor the Gaussian involves any imaginary numbers or square roots of negative one. Therefore, complex conjugation is not needed for the harmonic oscillator wave functions since they are not complex.
Let’s do one example of normalization, specifically the simplest one we can think of, because normalization is not particularly fun. Watching someone normalize a wave function is certainly not enjoyable, but it’s worth doing one example just to ensure we know what variable we’re integrating over. Since this wave function is a one-dimensional function that depends on \(x\), the variable we integrate over is \(x\). Remembering what that variable means, it represents bond displacement—how much I have stretched or compressed the bond. This displacement can range anywhere between negative infinity and infinity.
Negative infinity, representing an infinite amount of compression, might seem nonsensical. If you’re thinking of the bond as having potential energy as a function of bond length, I can only compress the bond from its equilibrium position, where \(x = 0\), down to fully compressed until the two items are sitting on top of each other. However, we’re not solving the Schrödinger equation for the correct potential energy function; we’re solving it for an approximation where we’ve treated the bottom of this well as a parabola. We acknowledge that we’re making some mistakes in both regions, and this function can continue to negative values of bond displacement. Therefore, if we treat the potential energy as a parabola, \(x\) can range from negative infinity to positive infinity.
Now, let’s normalize the zeroth wave function, which is equal to some normalization constant that we have not yet determined. The Hermite polynomial is 1, and the Gaussian looks like \(e^{-\frac{\alpha x^2}{2}}\) for this particular value of \(\alpha\). Normalization means I need for 1 to equal the integral from negative infinity to infinity of the wave function, which is \(N_0 e^{-\frac{\alpha x^2}{2}}\) integrated over \(x\). The \(N\)s can be pulled out of the integral. The two Gaussians combine, and \(e\) to the exponentials multiplied together is \(e\) to the sum of the two exponents. One-half of this quantity and one-half of that quantity add up to just give one of that quantity. Luckily, that’s an integral we know how to do; we’ve seen Gaussian integrals before. The integral of a Gaussian function from negative infinity to infinity is \(\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\alpha}}\).
Thus, we need for 1 to equal \(N_0^2 \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\alpha}}\). Since we’re solving for \(N_0\), I’ll rearrange this to say \(N_0^2 = \frac{\sqrt{\alpha}}{\pi}\), or when I take the square root, \(N_0 = \left( \frac{\alpha}{\pi} \right)^{\frac{1}{4}}\). So that’s the normalization constant. If we care about the full details of the zeroth wave function, the value of this normalization constant is \(\left( \frac{\alpha}{\pi} \right)^{\frac{1}{4}}\), where \(\alpha\) is this collection of constants. Often, we don’t particularly care about those values, but when we do, here’s how to do the normalization.
The variable we integrate over and the ranges of that integral can also lead us to discuss the orthogonality of those wave functions. Are they orthogonal to one another? Here, I can’t ask if \(\psi_0\) is orthogonal; I need to ask if one wave function is orthogonal to another wave function. For example, I can ask if \(\psi_0\) is orthogonal to \(\psi_1\), \(\psi_2\), \(\psi_3\), and so on—any pair of wave functions I want. In general, to answer that question, I would need to determine whether the \(n\)-th wave function is orthogonal to some \(n'\), a different wave function.
To answer that question, I ask if it is true that the overlap integral between \(n\) and \(n'\), which is the integral of \(\psi_n\) multiplied by the complex conjugate of \(\psi_{n'}\) (which we don’t need for the harmonic oscillator), is equal to zero. If it is for \(n\)s that are different from one another, then those two wave functions are orthogonal to one another. We can certainly solve one such problem mathematically. We can take the \(\psi_0\) wave function and the \(\psi_1\) wave function, insert the integral, and do the calculation. It’s a little easier to see how that works out visually.
If I draw a graph of what the \(\psi_0\) wave function looks like, it resembles a Gaussian function. Ignoring the normalization constant, it looks like \(e^{-x^2}\). The shape of the function is that of a Gaussian. If I am asking whether that’s orthogonal to \(\psi_1\) or \(\psi_n\) or some other wave function, I need to know what those look like as well. If I draw \(\psi_1\), it has the shape of a first-order function \(x\) times the Gaussian, again with normalization constants in front. The shape of that function generally looks like this. We know it looks like that because it’s the same Gaussian we started with before, multiplied by \(x\).
When \(x = 0\), the function has a value of 0. For small values of \(x\), I’m multiplying my Gaussian by a small number that is either positive or negative, depending on whether \(x\) is positive or negative. As \(x\) gets larger, I’m multiplying by a larger number, but it doesn’t blow up because the Gaussian decays fairly quickly, faster than the value of \(x\) grows. Thus, it is negative when \(x\) is negative and positive when \(x\) is positive.
Let me shade the area under this integral to illustrate that if I were to compute the overlap integral between the zeroth and first wave functions, the overlap integral between \(\psi_0\) and \(\psi_1\) would yield zero. Both wave functions are symmetric; the first wave function is symmetric about \(x = 0\). If I had drawn it as carefully as I should, what it looks like on the left matches what it looks like on the right. The \(\psi_1\) wave function is odd; remember, the Hermite polynomial is an odd function. Therefore, the value on the left is the negative of the value on the right.
When I multiply the two functions together, the areas where it’s positive times positive exactly cancel the areas where it’s positive times negative. By symmetry, we can see that the overlap integral will indeed equal zero. We can continue and do a very similar thing for one more case: the \(\psi_2\) function. Ignoring the constants, \(\psi_2\) looks like \(x^2\) minus a constant times the Gaussian. The second-order Hermite polynomial multiplies my Gaussian function, so it resembles a Gaussian not multiplied by a linear function, which has one zero. This gives the wave function a single node at \(x = 0\).
However, I’ve got a quadratic function with two zeros, which will cause the wave function to cross zero at two different locations, starting out positive, dipping negative, crossing back positive again, and then dying at either extreme because of the Gaussian. Again, shading the area under the integral illustrates where it’s positive and where it’s negative. If we compute the overlap integral between wave function one and wave function two, we can see that because of symmetry, this is an even function. If I multiply it by an odd function, the places where it’s positive and negative will cancel out.
Thus, by symmetry, these two wave functions are orthogonal to one another. It gets a little more complicated when I talk about the overlap between function zero and function two. Here, I can’t use symmetry in quite the same way. This is an even function, and this is also an even function. There’s no guarantee that an even function times another even function will integrate to zero. However, I can observe that there are some regions where positive multiplies positive, and in the middle, there are regions where positive multiplies negative.
It just so happens that because of the features of this Hermite polynomial—essentially due to the recursion relation between different Hermite polynomials—the negative areas of this product will exactly cancel the positive areas of this product. Thus, this overlap integral will indeed be zero, although it’s not obvious that it must be the case from the pictures we’ve drawn here.
In fact, it is true that every pair of different harmonic oscillator wave functions has an overlap integral of zero, meaning those two wave functions are orthogonal. The full set of harmonic oscillator wave functions is indeed mutually orthogonal. This summarizes both the normalization and the orthogonality of these integrals. Since the wave functions, with these choices of normalization constants, are normalized and mutually orthogonal to one another, we say that the harmonic oscillator wave functions form an orthonormal set of functions. Now that we know this, the next step is to return to the wave function and see what we can learn about the energies of the wave functions.

title: 19  Harmonic Oscillator Energies
text: Harmonic Oscillator Energies (6:30)

title: 19  Harmonic Oscillator Energies
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 19  Harmonic Oscillator Energies
section: Harmonic Oscillator Energies Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator Energies Video Transcript
We have managed to come up with a whole family of solutions for the wave functions to solve the harmonic oscillator Schrödinger equation. They all have the form of some Hermite polynomial multiplying a Gaussian. In particular, we have seen that for the ground state wave function, the simplest of these functions has just a constant in front of the Gaussian. When we plug that wave function into the Schrödinger equation, the energy turns out to be \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\), where \(h\) is Planck’s constant and \(
u\) is the vibrational frequency of the harmonic oscillator we are discussing.
We have not plugged any of the other wave functions into the Schrödinger equation, and we won’t do that now. That would be a good problem; you will likely work on those as homework problems at some point, or they could serve as good exercises. If you want to, between videos, take any one of these wave functions and plug it into the harmonic oscillator Schrödinger equation. Some terms will cancel, and the terms that do not cancel will end up telling you the energy of the wave function multiplied by the original wave function.
If I plug the wave function with the linear polynomial in front of the Gaussian into the Schrödinger equation, I do not get \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\) as the energy of the wave function; I get \( rac{3}{2} h 
u\). Similarly, if I plug the \(\psi_2\) wave function into the harmonic oscillator Schrödinger equation, I will get the energy of the \(n = 2\) wave function, which turns out to be \( rac{5}{2} h 
u\). This pattern repeats. Notice that the energies are \( rac{1}{2}\), \( rac{3}{2}\), \( rac{5}{2}\); they are increasing by \( rac{1}{2}\) each time. In other words, the energy is \( rac{1}{2}\) for the \(n = 0\) wave function, \(1.5\) for the \(n = 1\) wave function, \(2.5\) for the \(n = 2\) wave function, and for the \(n\)th wave function with this general form, the energy is \(\left(n +  rac{1}{2}ight) h 
u\).
If I draw an energy ladder for the energy levels of a harmonic oscillator, here is the bottom: there is zero energy. The ground state, the lowest energy wave function, is this one with energy \(E_0\), which is equal to \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\). The next one up is three times larger, \( rac{3}{2} h 
u\), so somewhere around here will be the energy of the \(n = 1\) wave function, and then an equal amount higher will be the \(n = 2\) wave function, and so on. Notice that the gap between the \(E_0\) and \(E_1\) wave functions, \( rac{3}{2} h 
u -  rac{1}{2} h 
u\), is one factor of \(h 
u\); that is the difference in energy between these two successive states. Likewise, the difference between \(E_2\) and \(E_1\), \( rac{5}{2} h 
u -  rac{3}{2} h 
u\), is also one factor of \(h 
u\), and this continues as we go up the ladder. Every pair of states increases its energy, and every successive pair of states has a difference of energy of \(h 
u\) because every energy level increases by an increment of exactly \(h 
u\) as we climb the ladder.
There are two very important features of the harmonic oscillator energies that we have just written down. First of all, \(\Delta E = h 
u\) between every pair of successive states. In other words, as I climb the ladder, the gap between states remains exactly the same. That is different from what we observed for the particle in a box or for the rigid rotor, where that spacing changed as I climbed the energy ladder. Here, the energy gaps remain constant as I climb the ladder.
The other important point worth mentioning is that the ground state energy, the energy for the \(n = 0\) state, is equal to \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\). The relevant fact here is that this number is not zero; it is greater than zero. The lowest energy is a positive energy, so there is no way to lower the energy of the system any lower than \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\). In particular, I cannot get the energy all the way to zero. No matter what energy level the system is in, it cannot have zero energy; it must at least have \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\) worth of energy, and often more than that. Therefore, we say that the system has a zero-point energy, often abbreviated as ZPE. Saying the system has a zero-point energy essentially means that if we bring it down to its lowest, zeroth energy level, it has some non-zero amount of energy. When it is at that zero point, no matter how cold we make the system or how low the state it occupies, it always has some non-zero energy. We can never fully remove all the energy from the system because we cannot drop below the zeroth energy level; we cannot drop the energy below \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\).
So, when we say this system has a zero-point energy of \( rac{1}{2} h 
u\), or if the system has a zero-point energy in general, it just means that it has some energy that we cannot fully get rid of in the system. These two features—the fact that our harmonic oscillator has a zero-point energy and the fact that the gaps between these energy levels remain constant as I climb the ladder—will combine to have some pretty important consequences when we describe the properties of a harmonic oscillator, particularly the properties of a diatomic molecule vibrating and acting as a harmonic oscillator. This will affect their spectroscopy, the colors of light that the molecule will absorb, as well as their thermodynamic properties. But before we go on to talk about those properties of the molecules, since we have now written down both the energies and the wave functions, we can discuss a little more about how those two properties of the harmonic oscillator interact and what that tells us about the harmonic oscillator.

title: 20  Harmonic Oscillator - Interpretation
text: Harmonic Oscillator - Interpretation (11:44)

title: 20  Harmonic Oscillator - Interpretation
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 20  Harmonic Oscillator - Interpretation
section: Harmonic Oscillator - Interpretation Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator - Interpretation Video Transcript
So, this is a good point to step back and look at the big picture and see what we understand about the harmonic oscillator so far. As a quick recap, we started by saying that if the potential energy is quadratic, given by \(\frac{1}{2} k x^2\), we can use that to solve Schrödinger’s equation and obtain these wave functions. These wave functions have a particular set of energies.
Let’s draw some pictures and see what that tells us about the properties of this harmonic oscillator. To start with the energy, I will draw an energy ladder, as we’ve seen before. Here are the energies: I’m going to draw the energy of each level. There is an energy at \(\frac{1}{2} h \nu\), somewhat higher there is an energy of \(\frac{3}{2} h \nu\), and then an equal amount higher, there is an energy of \(\frac{5}{2} h \nu\). That ladder continues with equal spacing as I climb up. So, that’s what we know about the energies.
Since this is a graph of energies, let’s draw the potential energies on the same graph. The potential energy is also a function of \(x\). If I make this a two-dimensional graph with energy or potential energy as a function of \(x\), then that parabola, which represents the quadratic function, is zero when \(x = 0\) and increases parabolically as \(x\) increases. I’ve drawn a poor parabola, but that’s supposed to represent the potential energy function.
Now, if I attempt to superimpose a graph of what the wave functions themselves look like on top of the same graph, we can use that composite graph to understand a little bit about the harmonic oscillator. Let me do that with a different color. We have different wave functions at every energy level. The zeroth level has the wave function \(\psi_0\), the first energy level has the wave function \(\psi_1\), and the second energy level has the wave function \(\psi_2\).
I can draw several different wave functions. If I treat this as the baseline for the wave function, I know what the zeroth wave function looks like; it looks like a Gaussian, which we’ve drawn before. So, the wave function looks like that. Squaring the wave function gives us a Gaussian shape but with a different exponent.
Now, I’ll draw \(\psi_1\) as well. Remember, \(\psi_1\) looks like \(x e^{-\alpha x^2 / 2}\). If I draw that wave function and superimpose it on top of the \(n = 1\) energy level, it starts out negative, goes positive, and decays as \(x\) becomes large in either direction. So, that’s the wave function with a single node at \(x = 0\).
The wave function \(\psi_2\) is similar but has two nodes. Here’s a different baseline: I’ll draw \(\psi_2\) starting positive, going negative, then positive again, and decaying at the edges.
This shows us several things. First of all, make sure you understand what we’ve drawn here. I’ve drawn three different things on the same graph: the energy levels themselves, the shape of the potential energy function, and now with this last curve, the blue curve, I’ve drawn the shape of the wave functions. In particular, those wave functions are all at different baselines. This is \(\psi = 0\) for the \(\psi_2\) wave function, this level is \(\psi = 0\) for the \(\psi_1\) wave function, and this is \(\psi = 0\) for the \(\psi_0\) wave function.
I’ve drawn the \(x\)-axis essentially at different heights for each of these three wave functions to keep them from sitting on top of each other and make them easier to see. The other thing this shows us is that remembering what the wave function means, the wave function tells us where I’m likely to find the particle. In particular, I have to square the wave function to find out where I’m likely to find the particle.
In the \(\psi_0\) wave function, I’m most likely to find the particle near the center, near \(x = 0\), where the potential energy is zero. So, that makes sense: I’m most likely to find the wave function where the potential energy is small. As I switch to more and more excited states, I’m able to find the particle in other regions, not just where the wave function is large because the potential energy is low, but also in some regions where the potential energy is considerably higher.
Thus, the higher the excited state of the harmonic oscillator, the more likely I am to find the \(x\) value far away from the origin. Think about what that means for an oscillating diatomic molecule: I don’t just find it at the equilibrium distance; I also find it at stretched or compressed values of the bond as well. So, when I excite the harmonic oscillator, a quantum mechanical molecule, I find that it’s more commonly found at small or large values of the bond displacement.
There are also some similarities between the wave functions we’ve just drawn for the harmonic oscillator. This is all for the harmonic oscillator model. If we think back to what a similar graph would have looked like for the particle in a box, we never drew that graph, but if I draw a similar graph for the one-dimensional particle in a box, I will do that quickly. Remember, for the particle in a box, the particle was found between \(0\) and \(a\). The potential energy for the particle in a box is zero when I’m inside the box and infinitely large when I’m outside the box.
So, the graph goes up to infinity as I reach the edges of the box. The energy levels, which I should have drawn in pink, are represented by \(E_n\). Those were \(E_2\), \(E_3\), and so on, which correspond to \(\frac{h^2}{8m a^2} \times 1\), \(\frac{h^2}{8m a^2} \times 4\), \(\frac{h^2}{8m a^2} \times 9\), and so on.
Now, the wave functions I will draw in blue. These had perhaps one node, or perhaps two nodes, or perhaps three nodes, depending on which state of the particle in a box we were discussing. Those were functions that look like \(\sin\left(\frac{3 \pi x}{a}\right)\), for example.
Now that I’ve drawn both of those, we can see there are some similarities and some differences. In particular, the wave functions tend to oscillate. Both the harmonic oscillator wave function and the particle in a box wave function tend to oscillate. The third wave function, not the ground state but the second excited state, has three individual regions: it goes positive, then negative, then positive. The same is true for the harmonic oscillator: positive, then negative, then positive.
As a side effect of that, the wave functions have nodes. If I’m switching from positive to negative, I have to have a node; I have to have a region where the wave function reaches zero. There are two nodes in this wave function for the harmonic oscillator, just like there are two nodes in the third wave function for the particle in a box as well.
However, there are some distinctly different aspects between the harmonic oscillator and the particle in a box. The reason for the oscillation, or the mathematical form of the oscillation, is a little bit different in these two cases. I’ve got \(e^{-\alpha x^2}\) for the harmonic oscillator and \(\sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{a}\right)\) for the particle in a box. So, there’s clearly a difference between the trigonometric oscillations and the exponential Gaussian-shaped oscillations.
The nodes in this case are formed by the zeros of the polynomial that sits out in front of the wave function, which is the Hermite polynomial. The zeros for the particle in a box are caused by the trigonometric functions themselves.
Another difference is the boundary conditions. For the particle in a box, you’ll remember we spent quite a bit of time ensuring that the wave function reached zero at the edges of the box because the potential energy was becoming infinite. The probability of finding the particle at the edge of the box had to reach zero, so we had to make sure the wave function reached zero at the edge of the box.
That is quite clearly not happening for the harmonic oscillator. These Gaussian functions don’t reach zero when they hit the potential energy wall of this box; they asymptotically decay to zero and do so very gradually, never quite reaching zero until \(x\) reaches negative infinity or positive infinity. So, that’s an important difference between the harmonic oscillator, which in principle can have any amount of bond stretch or bond compression, and the particle in a box, where the particle is not allowed to escape the box.
Another similarity, rather than a difference, between these two models is that they both have some zero-point energy. We’ve talked about how the harmonic oscillator has some energy even in the ground state; the energy of \(\frac{1}{2} h \nu\) is above zero. The same is true for the particle in a box: the ground state, or \(E_1\) state, has an energy that’s larger than zero. So, both models do have some zero-point energy.
The last point to note between these two models is the types of energy that we’re discussing. Remember that for the particle in a box, the potential energy was zero, which meant that the only type of energy that model had was kinetic energy. In the harmonic oscillator, we do have some potential energy, so the harmonic oscillator has both kinetic energy and potential energy. This distinction will turn out to be important when we talk about the thermodynamic properties of these models—thermodynamic properties that we’ll eventually derive from the partition function for the models.
Now that we have the energies of the harmonic oscillator energy levels, \(\frac{1}{2} h \nu\), \(\frac{3}{2} h \nu\), \(\frac{5}{2} h \nu\), and so on, we have enough information to plug those into the equation and obtain the partition function for the harmonic oscillator. So, that’s what we’ll do now.

title: 21  Harmonic Oscillator Partition Function
text: Harmonic Oscillator Partition Function (9:33)

title: 21  Harmonic Oscillator Partition Function
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 21  Harmonic Oscillator Partition Function
section: Harmonic Oscillator Partition Function Video Transcript
text:
Harmonic Oscillator Partition Function Video Transcript
So far, for the harmonic oscillator, here’s what we’ve managed to do. We started with our assumption for what the potential energy is for our harmonic oscillator: a quadratic function, \(\frac{1}{2} kx^2\). Quantum mechanics allowed us to solve for the wave functions of particles that obey the Schrödinger equation with that potential energy, as well as the energies of the functions that have those wave functions. As we’ve done before for both the particle in a box and for the rigid rotor, now that we know those energies, we can use them to obtain the partition function and also the probability of occupying any particular energy level. Once we have the partition function, we will be able to do many things, such as calculating energies, entropies, heat capacities, pressures, and so on.
We start with quantum mechanics, then proceed to statistical mechanics, which serves as our link to thermodynamics. If we want to discuss the energies and pressures of vibrating diatomic gaseous molecules, the partition function needs to be our next step. So, how do we find the partition function for a vibrating diatomic molecule? The partition function for a harmonic oscillator, as always, is the sum of the Boltzmann factors:
[ Q = e^{-} ]
for all the different energies that the system can have. We know exactly what the energies are for a harmonic oscillator. Each of those energies, \(E_n\), where \(n\) is the vibrational quantum number, can range anywhere from 0 all the way up to infinity. Therefore, we just need to complete that sum.
If I substitute this expression for \(E_n\), I’ll get
[ e^{-(n + ) h } kT ]
This can simplify a little bit and eventually turn into something useful. First of all, \(n + \frac{1}{2}\) gives me a sum inside the exponent of this exponential, so I’m going to break that up into two different exponentials. Inside the sum, I have
[ e^{-} e^{- } ]
I’ve just broken up this exponential into the product of two different exponentials. The reason I’ve done that is that one of these two, the second one that I’ve written down, \(e^{-\frac{1}{2} \frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), does not have any \(n\) in it. The summation variable \(n\) does not appear anywhere in this term, so this term does not depend on the value of \(n\), and I can pull it outside of the sum.
Thus, I have
[ e^{-} e^{-} ]
I can’t pull that out of the sum, obviously, because it has this \(n\) in it. So, let’s think about what this sum means. The first term is still \(e^{-\frac{h \nu}{2kT}}\). What it’s multiplying is the sum of these Boltzmann factors: the Boltzmann factor with \(n = 0\), the Boltzmann factor with \(n = 1\), the Boltzmann factor with \(n = 2\), and so on.
When \(n = 0\), I have \(e^{0}\). That one’s easy enough. I’m adding the term when \(n = 1\), \(e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}}\). Then I need the term with \(n = 2\), so that’s \(e^{-2 \frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), and so on. The next one would be \(e^{-3 \frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), \(e^{-4 \frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), and so on.
So, that’s just what our infinite sum means: this sum of terms. But notice now that \(e^{-2 \frac{h \nu}{kT}}\) is the same thing as \(e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}} \cdot e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}}\). In fact, every one of these terms will be this basic Boltzmann factor \(e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}}\) raised to some power. I have \(1\), \(e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), \(e^{-2 \frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), \(e^{-3 \frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), and so on.
If I rewrite this more simply, letting \(x = e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), this looks like \(1 + x + x^2 + x^3 + \ldots\), where I’ve just let \(x\) be short for this Boltzmann factor \(e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}}\). The next step is to recognize that that sum \(1 + x + x^2 + x^3 + \ldots\) is a Taylor series for the quantity in parentheses, which is a Taylor series for \(\frac{1}{1 - x}\) or a Maclaurin series, if we want to be technical.
If we do that in reverse, if you take the function \(\frac{1}{1 - x}\) and obtain the Taylor series for it, the Taylor series you’ll end up with is indeed this Taylor series \(1 + x + x^2 + x^3 + \ldots\). So, if I rewrite everything now using that Taylor series, collapsing this infinite sum of an infinite number of terms down to this simple, more compact term, and remembering that \(x\) is equal to this Boltzmann factor \(e^{-\frac{h \nu}{kT}}\), my partition function has now become
[ Q = e^{-} ]
I’ll go ahead and put that in a box because that’s our final result for the partition function. This is the partition function, the sum of all the Boltzmann factors for a harmonic oscillator, which has these energy levels. Notice, importantly, what we’ve done: this is no longer an infinite sum. I don’t have to sum up an infinite number of terms. If I know the values of these various constants, I can just put them in a couple of exponentials, combine them together, and calculate the partition function directly.
Notice also that I didn’t make any approximations in obtaining this partition function. If you think back to what we did to calculate the partition functions for the particle in a box or the rigid rotor, they involved taking the classical limit and converting sums to integrals and things like that. We didn’t end up having to take any shortcuts like that in this particular case. It just happens to be that the infinite sum we are interested in happens to be the Taylor series for a more compact form, allowing us to write it in this way.
So, we didn’t have to make any approximations for this partition function. The next thing we can discuss about the partition function is what it means. We can plug some values in and obtain numbers, and we’ll do that. But really, what we’re after is the question: Is this partition function large or small?
Thinking back to what the partition means physically, if the partition function is large, that means many of the states on this energy ladder are occupied; quite a few of the excited states for the harmonic oscillator are occupied. However, if \(Q\) is small, that means very few of the states are occupied, maybe just the ground state. The reason that would be true is if the gap between the energy levels is large or small compared to \(kT\).
That will tell us whether we’re occupying just the bottom few states or many excited states. Again, that energy difference compared to \(kT\) will determine the size of this partition function. So, that’s our next task: to figure out which of those two cases is true.

title: 22  Diatomic Molecules as Harmonic Oscillators

title: 22  Diatomic Molecules as Harmonic Oscillators
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel
crumbs: 22  Diatomic Molecules as Harmonic Oscillators

title: 22  Diatomic Molecules as Harmonic Oscillators
section: Diatomic Molecules as Harmonic Oscillators Video Transcript
text:
Diatomic Molecules as Harmonic Oscillators Video Transcript
We have seen a lot of equations for harmonic oscillators, and we have managed to get this far without plugging in any actual numbers for real-world diatomic molecules, which are the molecules we are supposed to be treating with this harmonic oscillator model. So, what we will do next is examine the properties of these harmonic oscillators when we use them to describe actual real-world molecules. The real question is going to be: how does the difference in energy between these harmonic oscillator states compare to \(kT\)?
Remember, every pair of successive states is separated by the same amount of energy, \(h \nu\), regardless of whether it is from the zeroth to the first state, the first to the second, or the second to the third. If \(kT\) is large compared to that difference in energy, then thermal energy is enough to excite many of these energy levels. Conversely, if \(kT\) is small compared to that difference in energy, then we are stuck in the ground state. This distinction will significantly affect the properties of these molecules.
We could ask whether \(h \nu\) is large compared to \(kT\). Looking at the partition function, this will affect the size of the exponent. Is \(h \nu\) large compared to \(kT\)? Is \(h \nu\) large compared to \(2kT\)? This will influence the size of the partition function. Equivalently, instead of asking whether \(h \nu\) is large compared to \(kT\), if we divide both sides of this expression by \(k\), we are asking whether \(\frac{h \nu}{k}\) is large compared to the temperature.
All I have done is rearrange that collection of variables so that instead of asking a question about the difference in energies compared to \(kT\), which is an energy, I am asking a question about this collection of constants compared to the temperature. This collection of constants has units of temperature, and in fact, we call this collection of constants the vibrational temperature. You can compare that to a similar constant we defined for the rigid rotor model, where we defined the rotational temperature, which was just a collection of constants that had the units of temperature. This told us whether the rotational properties of the molecule were large or small compared to the temperature we are interested in under a given set of conditions.
This is the same idea for the harmonic oscillator: a collection of constants, this energy difference divided by Boltzmann’s constant, tells us something about the difference in energy levels. In terms that are easier to compare, it tells us whether that collection of constants, with units of temperature, appears large or small compared to the physical temperature of the system.
Let’s go ahead and work through an example to see how that works out. Our main goal will be to find out how the vibrational temperature for a real-world molecule compares to real-world temperatures. Let’s take the carbon monoxide molecule, a diatomic molecule that we have considered before. We know that the reduced mass for that molecule, which we have calculated before, is given by the mass of carbon times the mass of oxygen divided by the sum of the two masses. This results in a reduced mass of \(1.14 \times 10^{-26}\) kilograms.
Now, a number that we haven’t seen before is the spring constant. Notice that we will have two different types of \(k\). The \(k\) that appears in \(kT\) is Boltzmann’s constant, but we have a different \(k\) here, which is the spring constant. This spring constant, in the potential energy expression \(\frac{1}{2} k x^2\), tells us how quickly or slowly the potential energy increases as I stretch the bond in this diatomic molecule when I treat it as a Hooke’s law spring.
The spring constant for a carbon monoxide molecule will have units of energy over distance squared. If I stretch the bond on a carbon monoxide molecule by a full meter, it would cost me 1880 joules if it were a Hooke’s law spring, meaning that if that bond didn’t break, stretching it by a full meter would require that amount of energy. Of course, in practice, we stretch the bond by only fractions of a nanometer, and the energy increases by tiny fractions of a joule. However, this gives us the spring constant for the stretches of that bond.
With that information, we can first calculate the vibrational frequency of this carbon monoxide molecule. We have seen this expression: the vibrational frequency, if we know the spring constant and the reduced mass, is given by \(\nu = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}\). So, let’s plug those numbers in, mainly to see how the units work out.
If I insert the spring constant and the reduced mass, remembering that a joule is \(\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2\), in the numerator I have units of \(\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2\) divided by an extra factor of \(\text{m}^2\). This cancels the squared meters in the denominator of this large fraction. The kilograms cancel as well, and what I am left with is that the units inside this square root are \(\text{s}^{-2}\). Once I take the square root, the units will be \(\text{s}^{-1}\).
If I do the arithmetic, \(\frac{1}{2\pi} \times \sqrt{\text{spring constant}/\text{reduced mass}}\) works out to be \(6.5 \times 10^{13}\) per second. This means that if we treat this carbon monoxide molecule with this spring constant and this mass as a classical vibrating Hooke’s law spring, it will oscillate with a frequency of \(6.5 \times 10^{13}\) hertz. This tells us how quickly that molecule oscillates.
We are not so much interested in the vibrational frequency as we are in the vibrational temperature, which will be a much more intuitive number. The reason we calculated the vibrational frequency is so we could insert it into the expression to calculate the vibrational temperature. The vibrational temperature is given by \(\frac{h \nu}{k}\).
Again, just to ensure the units will work out, I will insert Planck’s constant and multiply it by this vibrational frequency, \(6.5 \times 10^{13}\) per second. Those are both multiplied together in the numerator, and if I divide by \(k\), which is Boltzmann’s constant, I will take a quick look at the units. Planck’s constant has units of joules times seconds, and the frequency involves \(\text{s}^{-1}\), so the seconds cancel. I have joules in the numerator and joules in the denominator, which cancel, leaving me with units of \(\text{K}\).
If I do the arithmetic in this expression, I find that the vibrational temperature of a carbon monoxide molecule works out to be 3100 kelvin. What does that mean? Is that a large number or a small number? Unlike the frequency or the difference in energy, if we calculate that in joules, that number immediately gives you an impression of whether it is large or small compared to temperatures we are used to dealing with, such as 300 kelvin for room temperature. That clearly appears to be a pretty large temperature.
In general, this will be true for any real-world diatomic molecule. Typical reduced masses and typical spring constants for diatomic molecules will result in vibrational temperatures that are quite a bit larger than room temperature. If this is true for carbon monoxide, it is also generally true for most diatomics; the vibrational temperature will typically be larger than room temperature, often by a full order of magnitude, sometimes by a little more and sometimes by a little less.
This is true at 300 kelvin, but it is not true if I were to conduct an experiment at 3000 kelvin, of course. However, for typical temperatures and typical molecules, the vibrational temperature ends up larger than the temperature. Thinking back to what that means, if the vibrational temperature is larger than the temperature, that is equivalent to saying \(h \nu\) is larger than \(kT\). The difference in energy levels is larger than \(kT\).
This difference is large compared to \(kT\). If I were to scale it, drawing something one-tenth as big, \(kT\) would only be this small, while the difference in energy levels would be this large. The thermal energy we have accessible to us at room temperature (300 kelvin) can only excite energy levels that are within this distance, or a few multiples of that distance, above the ground state. This means that very few of these vibrational states are occupied at 300 kelvin. We do not have enough thermal energy to populate states that are 3100 kelvin or 6200 kelvin above the ground state in units of temperature.
As a result, very few of those states end up occupied, and this means that if we were to calculate a numerical value for the partition function, it would end up being fairly small. A small partition function indicates that relatively few of the states are occupied. While we have only worked through the example for one molecule, carbon monoxide, those trends will generally be the same for all diatomic molecules. It is an important feature of diatomic molecules that their vibrational states are not very well populated at room temperature. We can do some examples to calculate specifically how populated those excited states are, and that is coming up next.
