title: 40  Particle on a Ring
text: Particle on a Ring (14:46)

title: 40  Particle on a Ring
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 40  Particle on a Ring
section: Particle on a Ring Video Transcript
text:
Particle on a Ring Video Transcript
All right, so we’ve seen a couple of quantum mechanical problems so far: the free particle and the one-dimensional particle in a box. Now, let’s consider a third problem, which turns out to be very similar to the particle in a box but with some important differences. This problem will be called the particle on a ring, for reasons that will become clear shortly. Mainly, we want to highlight the differences between the particle in a box and the particle on a ring.
Let me summarize a few things we know about the particle in a box. Namely, if we take a particle and confine it to a one-dimensional box between \(0\) and \(a\) on some axis, then the particle has a wave function. There are many different wave functions I could have drawn, and each of those wave functions has to hit zero at the edges of the box. These wave functions have a particular mathematical form and a specific energy. Again, each one of those different wave functions, whether \(n = 1\), \(2\), or \(3\), has a different energy.
Now, let’s consider a different problem. We’ll still confine our particle to a one-dimensional line, but now let’s take that line and bend it around until it forms a circle. I could still say the length is \(a\), but now that length is the circumference of the circle. To describe where the particle is, instead of describing its position along the x-axis, I’ll describe it in terms of the angle \(\theta\) that locates it around the circle. This circle has some radius \(r\), and I can use \(\theta\) to describe the angle and thus where I am around that circle.
I’m still going to have a wave function that depends on the coordinates of the particle. Now, because the particle can be anywhere in the plane of the board, it feels like a two-dimensional problem. However, since I’m confining the particle to live on the circumference of this circle, \(r\) has to be constant. Therefore, the only variable that can change is \(\theta\). It’s still a one-dimensional problem, but now I’m describing the wave function as a function of \(\theta\) rather than as a function of \(x\). This is one of the first differences between this problem and the particle in a box problem. We can see now why it’s called the particle on a ring; the particle is confined to this ring. We could have called it the particle on a circle, or if we think of this string that we’ve now wrapped into a circle, we could call it the particle on a rope. There are many different names you could give to this particle, but we’ll call it the particle on a ring. Some people refer to it as the particle in a ring.
So, we’re going to have a wave function that depends on \(\theta\). The boundary conditions, which for a particle in a box required the wave function to hit zero at the edges of the box, are no longer required here. The wave function doesn’t have to hit zero at the edge because there is no edge to this box. If I have a wave function that starts somewhere and oscillates as it goes around the circle, all I need is that when I get back to where I started, the wave function must have the same value as when I started. Otherwise, if I returned and had a different value than when I started, I wouldn’t have a single function. I need to have the same value on every trip around this loop. So, that’s the boundary condition that will replace the zero-at-the-edges boundary condition for the particle in a box.
This is another difference between this problem and the particle in a box problem. I’m not going to go through the solution in detail; I’ll skim through the details of the particle on a ring problem because we’ve already seen something very similar in the particle in a box. If we solve this problem, we still need to write down the Schrödinger equation. I’ll write that down for us and then explain the differences between it and the one for the particle in a box.
This looks a lot like the one-dimensional particle in a box Schrödinger equation. In that problem, our second derivative was \(\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\). Here, the variable we’re interested in is \(\theta\). I don’t just take \(\frac{d^2}{d\theta^2}\); I take \(\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d^2}{d\theta^2}\). You can take my word for it that this is what the Schrödinger equation of the Laplacian looks like for this problem. If you want to double-check me, what this means is that if I write down \(\frac{d^2}{dx^2} + \frac{d^2}{dy^2}\) for this two-dimensional problem, and then convert those \(x\) and \(y\) to polar coordinates while requiring \(r\) to be constant, what survives is just \(\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d^2}{d\theta^2}\). If that sounds like fun, pause the video and do that little exercise right now. If that doesn’t sound like fun, as I said, you can just take my word for it; that’s the Schrödinger equation for the particle on a ring problem.
So again, it basically boils down to the same problem. Just like the particle in a box, we’re going to be looking for some function such that if I take the second derivative of it, I get back the same function but with some constants and a negative sign. As before, a function whose second derivative is the negative of itself is a trigonometric function like sine. Therefore, \(\sin(k\theta)\) is still going to be a good solution. Unlike the previous case, cosine also works. Cosine didn’t work before because we needed something that would start at zero; the wave function had to be zero at the edges of the box. Sine is zero at \(x = 0\), but cosine is not. So, sine was a good solution for the particle in a box, but cosine is also a good solution for the particle on a ring.
If you’re not too terrified by complex numbers, we can also use \(e^{i k \theta}\), which will also solve this equation perfectly well. If you don’t like complex numbers, we can put off thinking about them until a little later in the course, and you can pretend I never wrote that down. For the moment, let’s just stick with constants times cosine and sine.
The next step would be figuring out what that value of \(k\) needs to be in order for this to be true. Whether you’re thinking about it graphically or visually, what does that \(k\) have to be so that after I complete a full loop, I get back to where I started? Or, whether you think about it mathematically and consider how to obey this boundary condition, we can convince ourselves that \(\cos(n\theta)\) works. So, \(\cos(\theta)\), \(\cos(2\theta)\), \(\cos(3\theta)\), or even \(\cos(0\theta)\) or \(\cos(-1\theta)\), \(\cos(-2\theta)\) are all valid solutions. Any integer multiple of \(\cos(n\theta)\) is a perfectly valid solution to this Schrödinger equation. This just means that the sine or cosine needs to oscillate one full round trip as it goes around the circle, which would be \(\cos(\theta)\) for one full oscillation, \(\cos(2\theta)\) for two full oscillations, and so on. For \(\cos(0\theta)\), the wave function remains constant without oscillating as it goes around the circle.
Each of these different solutions with a different value of \(n\) is a different wave function that we can call \(\psi_n\), just like we did for the particle in a box. If I want to know what the energy of those solutions is so that I can compare them to the particle in a box solution, we just need to plug these wave functions into the Schrödinger equation. Plugging into the Schrödinger equation, I can say
[ - ( A (n) ) = E ( A (n) ). ]
The second derivative of cosine is negative cosine, and I’ll pull out an \(n^2\). So, I’ve got \(A\) and \(n^2\) pulled out, which gives me
[ - (n) = E A (n). ]
This means that the energy of the \(\psi_n\) wave function is equal to
[ E = . ]
Again, this feels very similar to what we did for the particle in a box. If I compare these directly, they’re not the same. I no longer have a box length to worry about; instead, I have \(r^2\) in the denominator and a \(\pi^2\) that survived, which didn’t previously exist. But aside from that, the energies do depend on \(n^2\), just like they did before.
The last step would be to determine the value of this normalization constant. To normalize this function, I need to take the wave function times itself or its complex conjugate, and that has to equal one when integrated everywhere. For us, this means that if I integrate \(\theta\) from \(0\) to \(2\pi\), the wave function squared will look like
[ A^2 ^2(n). ]
If I care about what the normalization constant is, I would have to complete this integral, solve for \(A\), and that tells me what the normalization constant is. It doesn’t end up being \(\sqrt{2/a}\) as it did for the particle in a box. Skipping the details of doing that integral, this integral turns out to be \(A^2 \pi\), so \(A\) results in
[ A = . ]
That’s the normalization constant.
The last thing worth mentioning about this particle on a ring model is why we should care. Why did I say to take this straight line and bend it around into a circle? What in the world would we ever use this particle on a ring model for? The answer is that if the particle in a box was a useful model for simulating conjugated hydrocarbons that had the shape of a straight line, then if I take one of those conjugated hydrocarbons and bend it around into a circle, the particle on a ring model will be a good model for those molecules. For example, a conjugated hydrocarbon bent into not a circle but a hexagon would be benzene, and there are a variety of other cyclical molecules that we could use as applications for this particular model.
The reason it’s worth talking about benzene as an example in particular is that if I draw a graph or chart of the energies of these molecules, the \(n = 0\) solution has an energy of zero times some constants, so the energy of the ground state is down here. There are two states, \(n = 1\) and \(n = -1\), that have the same energy as each other, and then somewhere higher, there are two more states, \(n = 2\) and \(n = -2\), that also have the same energy as each other. Some of our states are degenerate; there are two states with the same energy at this level and at this level. However, the ground state is not degenerate; there’s only one state.
If I were to start filling these states with electrons, notice that I fill up the first level after putting in six electrons. Not coincidentally, benzene has six \(\pi\) electrons. So, the six \(\pi\) electrons in benzene occupy these lowest wave function energy levels that correspond, at least approximately, to these particle on a ring energy levels. The reason benzene is a very stable aromatic compound is that it fills up the ground and first excited states. You may remember from an organic chemistry class that Huckel’s rules about aromaticity specify that if you have \(4n + 2\) electrons, a molecule will be aromatic. This is a consequence of the particle on a ring model. Two electrons per energy level, \(4n + 2\) electrons, is like \(2n + 1\) energy levels. So, if I fill up to here, six electrons give me an aromatic benzene molecule. If I fill one level higher, I would need ten electrons to make the next aromatic ring size. Huckel’s rule about aromaticity is really an example of the particle on a ring quantum mechanical model.

title: 41  kT As a Benchmark
text: kT As a Benchmark (10:17)

title: 41  kT As a Benchmark
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 41  kT As a Benchmark
section: kT As a Benchmark Video Transcript
text:
kT As a Benchmark Video Transcript
As we’ve used the Boltzmann distribution, we’ve discovered that it’s useful to be able to compare the difference in energy between two systems to \(kT\). This ratio, \(\frac{\Delta E}{kT}\), is significant because it appears in the exponential of the relative probability distribution.
For example, suppose that this ratio \(\frac{\Delta E}{kT}\) is one, two, or three—some relatively small numbers. This means that the difference in energy is a few times the value of \(kT\), which we can express as a few \(kT\). Consequently, \(e^{-1}\), \(e^{-2}\), or \(e^{-3}\) are small numbers; they are less than one but not vanishingly small. They might be thirty percent, ten percent, or five percent. This indicates that the population in the upper state \(P_i\) relative to the population of the lower state is a small number but not negligible. Thus, the probability of occupying state A is less than the probability of occupying state B because its energy is higher if \(\Delta E\) is greater than zero, but not so small that we can ignore state I.
On the other hand, let’s consider the extreme case where the difference in energy is large. For it to be significantly larger than \(kT\), it needs to be more than just a few \(kT\)—perhaps even \(10 kT\). It doesn’t have to be \(100 kT\) or \(1000 kT\); just \(10 kT\) is sufficient. In this case, the ratio \(\frac{\Delta E}{kT}\) becomes \(10\), and \(e^{-10}\) is a considerably small number. This means that the probability of being in state I is much smaller than the probability of being in state J, often small enough that we can completely ignore it. If \(e^{-10}\) is a very small number, much less than one, the probability that molecules are in state I is significantly smaller than the probability that they are in state J.
However, let’s consider another extreme where state I is still the higher energy state, but the difference in energy between state I and state J is small compared to \(kT\). In this case, the ratio \(\frac{\Delta E}{kT}\) is quite small, much less than one. This means the exponent we are evaluating is \(e^{-x}\) for some small number \(x\), such as \(e^{-0.01}\) or \(e^{-0.001}\). A negative but small number results in a value close to one. Therefore, the probability of being in state A (state I) is about the same as the probability of being in state J. It will be slightly less because the energy is a little higher, but for most practical purposes, the probabilities of occupying those two states are quite similar.
This understanding tells us immediately that if we know how many \(kT\) units the difference in energy represents—whether it’s a few, many, or a tiny fraction of \(kT\)—we can gain qualitative insights into how populated these two states are. To utilize this, however, we need to know the value of \(kT\), which depends on the temperature.
Focusing on room temperature, around \(298\) K or \(300\) K, we find that the product of Boltzmann’s constant and the temperature, \(1.38 \times 10^{-23}\) joules per kelvin, multiplied by something in the vicinity of \(300\) K results in units of joules. The units of joules and kelvin cancel, leaving us with just joules. When we multiply this out, we find that \(kT\) is approximately \(4.1 \times 10^{-21}\) joules. This is a small but significant number because it serves as a benchmark against which we compare other energies to determine whether states are populated or relatively unpopulated at room temperature.
If we convert this to different units, such as kilojoules per mole, we find that multiplying by Avogadro’s number and dividing by \(1000\) gives us about \(2.5\) kilojoules per mole. Alternatively, if we prefer to work in kilocalories per mole, a non-SI unit still commonly used for describing energies of chemical reactions, it amounts to about \(0.6\) kilocalories per mole. These values are worth memorizing because they provide context for evaluating energy values.
For example, if I tell you that the bond energy of a carbon-hydrogen bond in a typical alkane is about \(400\) kilojoules per mole, you can assess its significance. Until this discussion, that number might not have meant much to you; it was simply an energy value with a specific unit. Now, however, we can compare \(400\) kilojoules per mole to \(kT\), which is only \(2.5\) kilojoules per mole. Thus, \(400\) kilojoules per mole is many multiples of \(kT\)—specifically, more than \(100 kT\).
This indicates that the probability of a molecule being found in the higher energy state, where the carbon-hydrogen bond is dissociated, is extremely low. At temperatures like \(300\) K, there is almost no probability that the molecule will be found in this higher energy state. Carbon-hydrogen bonds do not spontaneously dissociate because the energy required to break them is so much higher than \(kT\). This is fortunate, as it means that most of the carbon-hydrogen bonds in the organic molecules that make up our bodies are stable at room temperature.
Now, let’s consider a different type of bond, such as the hydrogen bond between two water molecules. This is not a covalent bond; rather, it is a hydrogen bond, which has an energy of about \(6\) kilojoules per mole. To determine if this is a large or small number, we again compare it to \(kT\). In this case, \(6\) kilojoules per mole is just a couple of \(kT\)—specifically, between \(2\) and \(3 kT\).
This means that the more energetic state, where the hydrogen bond is broken, is less likely than the associated state, but not dramatically so. In a sample of liquid water, perhaps \(5\) to \(10\) percent of the molecules have their hydrogen bonds broken at any given moment. This is a significant fraction that we cannot afford to ignore. In fact, this dynamic is what allows liquid water to behave as a liquid. If all the hydrogen bonds were permanent, like covalent bonds, water would not diffuse as it does. However, if \(5\) to \(10\) percent of water molecules can break their hydrogen bonds and then form new ones with different molecules, this leads to the unique properties of water.
In summary, by comparing the size of an energy to \(kT\), we can qualitatively assess the probabilities of different states based on how they relate to one another in the context of the Boltzmann distribution.

title: 42  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Wavefunction)
text: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Wavefunction) (7:20)

title: 42  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Wavefunction)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 42  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Wavefunction)
section: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Wavefunction) Video Transcript
text:
Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Wavefunction) Video Transcript
So far, we’ve talked about the one-dimensional particle in a box. That’s our only example of solving a quantum mechanical problem and solving Schrödinger’s equation. This has given us a little taste of quantum mechanics, so we have some experience under our belt. Before we move any further, let’s discuss what are called the postulates of quantum mechanics, which are the basic foundations for doing quantum mechanics. These will help us understand much better what the answers mean when we solve more complicated problems than the particle in a box.
It turns out there are five of these postulates of quantum mechanics. The first one refers to the properties of what we’ve been calling the wave function. This first postulate states that the state of a quantum mechanical system is completely described by its wave function. There are two important terms that deserve a little discussion: what do I mean by the state of a system, and what do I mean by the wave function? We’ll also talk about what it means for the system to be completely described.
Let’s start with the wave function. We’ve talked about wave functions before, but there’s one property that these wave functions must satisfy that we haven’t discussed in detail yet: they must be square integrable. This is a technical term, so let me explain what it means. If I have a wave function, \(\psi\), and I calculate the integral of the wave function multiplied by its complex conjugate, integrated over all space, that integral must be finite. For something to be a bona fide wave function, it has to be square integrable in this sense, meaning the integral must yield a finite number. This requirement ensures that we can normalize the wave function.
If you remember when we talked about the free particle, a sine wave that oscillated forever in all directions out to infinity resulted in an infinite integral of the square of that wave function. That’s one of the reasons we didn’t like that wave function, and we had to start discussing particles confined to boxes, where the integral of their probability, or the square of the wave function, would be finite. So, a wave function is the type of thing we’ve seen before, with the additional requirement that it must be square integrable.
When I refer to the state of the system, I mean the same as if I were describing the state of a problem back when we were doing lattice models. If I describe all the properties of the system, I’ve given you all the details, and I’ve completely described the state of the system. This postulate tells us that anything that is possible to know about a quantum mechanical system can be obtained from its wave function. If I know the wave function, there is a way to extract enough information to describe everything I can about the system.
So, what do I mean when I say that I can extract the properties from the wave function? We have seen just one example of this so far. The wave functions for the one-dimensional particle in a box are given by \(\sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{a}\right)\) with a normalization constant out front. The one property we know how to inquire about for this wave function is probability. Remember, the probability of finding an electron or a particle between two different limits, \(a\) and \(b\), is given by the integral from \(a\) to \(b\) of the squared wave function.
For example, let’s consider a particle in a box from \(0\) to \(a\). If we take the \(\psi_2\) wave function, it looks like this, and the square of the wave function looks like that. If I were to ask, for example, what the probability is that the molecule is in the left half of the box, meaning in the range between \(0\) and \(\frac{a}{2}\), I would integrate from \(0\) to \(\frac{a}{2}\) the square of the wave function, \(\frac{2}{a} \sin\left(\frac{2 \pi x}{a}\right)^2\).
We could compute that integral mathematically, or we could just look at the graph and note that the whole area under this curve is \(1\) because we have normalized our wave function. Since the left half is symmetric with the right half, by symmetry, I know that if I perform the integral, it will yield \(\frac{1}{2}\). Thus, there is a \(50\%\) probability that the electron is in the left half of the box. The particle exists at different positions in the box with different probabilities given by the wave function.
That’s an example of how I can find out something about the state of the system: where the particle is and with what probability it occupies certain regions. I can obtain that information from the wave function, provided I know what the wave function is, and there is a method for how to find the property I am interested in.
Notice that when I say the state of the system is completely described by the wave function, if I have two systems that have the exact same wave function, I will always get the same properties when I ask questions about the state of those systems. If two systems have the same wave function, they are identical in every way. This is a consequence of the fact that the state is completely described by the wave function.
So far, all we know how to do is find answers to questions about probability using the wave function. If we want to answer questions about other properties beyond probability, we need more information to tell us how to extract those properties from the wave function. That will be the topic of the next few postulates of quantum mechanics.

title: 43  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Operators)
text: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Operators) (8:32)

title: 43  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Operators)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 43  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Operators)
section: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Operators) Video Transcript
text:
Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Operators) Video Transcript
The first postulate of quantum mechanics tells us that everything possible to know about a quantum mechanical system can be obtained from its wave function. All the different properties of that system can be derived in some way from the wave function. Before we can understand how to do that, we should discuss what these properties are that we can obtain from the wave function. It is important to distinguish between properties that are what we call physical observables and those that are not.
A physical observable is any property that can be measured and assigned a concrete value without disagreement about that value. This may seem like a silly definition; of course, if a property exists, it has a value that we can measure. However, some properties, especially for us as chemists, are somewhat ambiguous or subjective. For example, in organic chemistry, we might discuss the quality of a leaving group or the amount of electron-withdrawing character that a functional group has. These properties are poorly defined, and different people may disagree about how to define them or whether one leaving group has more or less leaving group character than another. Thus, these are not physical observables; there is no instrument we can use to measure the quality of a leaving group.
In contrast, properties like mass, energy, or momentum describe the physical characteristics of a system and are not ambiguous or subjective. These are the properties we refer to as physical observables. For each of these carefully defined properties, there is a corresponding quantum mechanical operator. We have seen operators before, and I will remind you of a few of them. This postulate states that whatever property we want to measure—whether it is energy, momentum, or something else—there is an operator that corresponds to that property. There is an energy operator, a momentum operator, and so on.
We have indeed seen several examples of these operators corresponding to individual properties. Let me provide a few examples. For instance, the kinetic energy operator, sometimes referred to as \(\hat{T}\), appears in the Schrödinger equation as a second derivative operator. If I take a wave function and apply this operator to it, it is expressed as:
[ - ]
This operator acts on the wave function, giving us the kinetic energy in a way we have not yet fully discussed. This is the kinetic energy operator in one dimension. If we are solving a three-dimensional problem, it is often easier to write using the Laplacian operator \(\nabla^2\):
[ ^2 = + + ]
Another operator that appears in the Schrödinger equation is the potential energy operator, which is expressed as the potential energy times the wave function that follows it. In three dimensions, we would have the potential energy as a function of three-dimensional coordinates multiplying the wave function.
The whole purpose of the Schrödinger equation is to use the Hamiltonian operator, which is the sum of the kinetic energy and potential energy operators. This can be expressed as:
[ = - ^2 + V() ]
When this operator acts on a wave function, it gives us back the total energy multiplied by the wave function. This is what it means to be the total energy operator, and we can write that down in three dimensions as well.
To discuss a few properties we have not yet covered, the momentum operator, which we use to find the momentum of a particle, is denoted as \(\hat{p}\). It resembles the kinetic energy operator but involves a single derivative instead of a second derivative. It is expressed as:
[ = -i ]
In three dimensions, it looks similar:
[ = -i ]
Along with momentum, we often want to know the position of a particle. The operator that tells us the position of a particle is simply \(x\) in one dimension or \(\mathbf{r}\) in three dimensions.
Some operators, like momentum and energy, involve derivatives, while others, like position and potential energy, only involve multiplication. The last operator we will discuss for now, which will be particularly relevant once we address three-dimensional problems, is the angular momentum operator, denoted as \(\hat{L}\). This operator is also complex and contains the imaginary unit \(i\). If you remember enough physics, you know that angular momentum should be expressed as \(\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{p}\). This is exactly what we have here; it represents the cross product between the position operator \(\mathbf{r}\) and the momentum operator \(-i\hbar \nabla\).
In quantum mechanics, this operator is indeed the cross product of the position operator with the momentum operator. The second postulate tells us that each physical observable property we are interested in has some associated operator. It is still a bit unclear how to use that operator to actually obtain a value for the property, so we need one more postulate to explain how to do that, which is the next step.

title: 44  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Eigenvalues)
text: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Eigenvalues) (5:03)

title: 44  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Eigenvalues)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 44  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Eigenvalues)
section: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Eigenvalues) Video Transcript
text:
Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Eigenvalues) Video Transcript
Okay, so we’ve seen that when we solve a quantum mechanical problem, the energies are often quantized or discretized; only certain energies are allowed. The same is true for other properties that we tend to measure, and that’s because the third postulate of quantum mechanics tells us which values of certain properties are allowed.
The postulate states that a property, or a physical observable—maybe the energy, maybe the momentum, or whatever property I’m interested in—has an associated operator. Let’s just call this property \(A\). The second postulate told us that each of those properties has its own operator. So, if I call the operator corresponding to that property \(\hat{A}\), the only values that this property can take on are the eigenvalues of the operator.
Remember what an eigenvalue is. I’ll give an example in just a second, but an eigenvalue is the value that results when I operate with the operator on some function, yielding a constant times the same function. Those constants are the eigenvalues. Therefore, the specific values of the property that are allowed are only the eigenvalues of the operator.
We’ve seen this in one example already: for a particle in a box. In this one-dimensional problem, the Hamiltonian operator, which corresponds to the potential energy of zero, was just the kinetic energy portion of the operator—specifically, the second derivative multiplied by constants with a negative sign. This Hamiltonian operator corresponds to the property of energy.
If I’m looking for some function that I can act on with the Hamiltonian and get back some eigenvalue times the same function, the way we usually write that expression is in the form of the Schrödinger equation. I am looking for eigenfunctions, which are functions that I can act on with the Hamiltonian operator to obtain eigenvalues or constants times the original function.
When we solve the Schrödinger equation, the goal is to find the various different solutions for the eigenfunctions and the eigenvalues. When we did that for the particle in a box, we found that the energies were given by the specific set of values \(\frac{h^2}{8m^2} n^2\). With the energy levels we’ve drawn a few times, I can have this energy \(E_1\), this energy \(E_2\), or this energy \(E_3\), and so on. However, I can only have these specific discrete quantized energy levels.
This is an example of the third postulate in action: the only allowed values of the energy are these specific values \(E_1, E_2, E_3\), and so on. This is because those values are the eigenvalues of the Hamiltonian operator. If I want to ask what values of the momentum, position, or some other property are allowed for the one-dimensional particle in a box, I would need to find the eigenvalues for each of those operators—the momentum operator, the position operator, and so on.
By far, the most useful property in a chemistry context is often the Hamiltonian because we’re interested in the energies of the molecules. This tells us how to find the allowed values of energy. What it hasn’t told us yet is which values we are actually going to see. If I have a quantum mechanical system, it is allowed to have energy \(E_1\), \(E_2\), or \(E_3\); which one is it actually going to have? What energy can I expect to see for that particle? That requires one additional postulate, the fourth postulate, and that’s what we’ll talk about in the next video lecture.

title: 45  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values)
text: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values) (17:32)
crumbs: 45  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values)

title: 45  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel
crumbs: 45  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values)

title: 45  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values)
section: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values) Video Transcript
text:
Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Expectation Values) Video Transcript
The third postulate of quantum mechanics tells us that the allowed values of any observable property are the eigenvalues of the operator associated with that property. However, this does not quite answer the question of what value we are actually going to see when we make a measurement. This question is a little difficult to answer because quantum mechanics is inherently probabilistic; it is not deterministic.
For example, if we consider the one-dimensional particle in a box, where the wave function, let’s say in particular wave function number one, is given by
[ (x) = () n = 1, ]
the wave function itself looks like a sine wave. The probability of finding the particle at any particular position is given by \(\psi^2\), which is the wave function times its complex conjugate. Therefore, the square of this wave function is
[ ^2 = ^2(). ]
Graphically, if I plot the probability or \(\psi^2\), the graph of \(\sin^2\) looks like this: it is zero when \(x = 0\) and also zero when \(x = a\), reaching a maximum at the midpoint when \(x = \frac{a}{2}\).
Since we know that probabilities are related to the square of the wave function, all we know about the particle is the probability of finding it at different locations. For a particle in the \(\psi_1\) wave function, which is the ground state wave function for the particle in a box, it is most likely to be found in the middle of the box. The probability is highest at the center, but there is some non-zero probability of finding it at other locations as well. The only places where we will not find it, where the probability is zero, are at the edges of the box or outside of it.
Thus, all we know about the particle is its probability of being found at various locations. I cannot definitively say that it will be found at \(\frac{a}{2}\), \(\frac{a}{4}\), or any specific value; it has some probability of being measured at any point within the box. This is what I mean when I say quantum mechanics is probabilistic. Often, all we know is the probability of finding a particle at certain locations or with certain properties.
However, that does not mean we do not know anything about the value we will measure when we measure a particular property. This brings us to the fourth postulate of quantum mechanics, which states that if I have a system with a particular wave function, and I want to know the value of a specific property, I can predict something called the expectation value. This is essentially another term for the mean or average.
If I have some property \(A\) that I am interested in—perhaps that property is energy, position, momentum, or something else—we know that this property has some operator associated with it. To find the average value I will obtain after making multiple measurements, we express the expectation value as
[ A = ^* , dx, ]
where \(\hat{A}\) is the operator acting on the wave function \(\psi\), and \(\psi^*\) is the complex conjugate of the wave function. If I integrate this expression over the entire space, the result will give me a number, which will be the average value of the property I expect when I make multiple measurements.
For example, if I ask about the average position of the particle, it will be in the middle of the box. Sometimes it will be on the left side, sometimes on the right side, but on average, I expect to find it in the middle. Any single measurement will be unpredictable, but if I make a number of measurements one after the other, I can predict what the average of those measurements will be.
To calculate an expectation value, I need to know what property I am interested in, what the operator of that property is, and which wave function the system is occupying. If I want to find the average value for the position, I can use the fourth postulate to calculate that. We need to calculate the wave function times the operator associated with the position. Remember that the position operator is simply multiplication by \(x\).
Writing this out, the wave function looks like
[ (x) = (), ]
and the position operator is just \(x\). Therefore, we need to evaluate the integral
[ x = _0^a x ( ())^2 , dx. ]
This simplifies to
[ x = _0^a x ^2() , dx. ]
This integral can certainly be computed using integration techniques such as the double angle formula and integration by parts. For now, I will leave that as an exercise for you to work on or look up in an integral table. If you complete that integral, you will find that the answer is
[ . ]
This indicates that the average position where I find the particle, when performing this integral, is indeed \(\frac{a}{2}\), which is in the middle of the box. By symmetry, we expected this result because the particle is equally likely to be found on the left side as on the right side.
Now, let’s consider another example where we will compute the integral. What would be the average momentum? The wave function itself does not directly tell us much about the momentum of the particle. It gives us information about the probability of where I will find the particle, but it does not directly indicate the momentum without additional work.
The fourth postulate tells us that the average momentum, or the expectation value of the momentum, is again going to be the wave function sandwiched around the operator associated with that property. The operator associated with momentum is
[ = -i . ]
Now we have a slightly different integral to compute. Instead of \(x\) times the wave function twice, I have some constants times the derivative of the wave function times the original wave function. The derivative of sine will yield a cosine, pulling out some constants.
Thus, we have
[ p = _0^a (-i ( ())) ( ()) , dx. ]
The derivative of \(\sin\) gives us
[ (), ]
and the integral now looks like
[ p = -i _0^a () () , dx. ]
There are two ways to solve this integral. One approach is to use \(u\)-substitution. If we let \(u = \sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\), then \(du = \cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right) \frac{\pi}{a} dx\).
Alternatively, we can use symmetry. The sine function is symmetric within the interval from \(0\) to \(a\), while the cosine function starts positive and goes negative, making it asymmetric about the midpoint of the integral. Thus, we can predict that the answer to this integral will be zero by symmetry.
If we proceed with the \(u\)-substitution, we find that when \(x = 0\), \(u = 0\), and when \(x = a\), \(u = \sin(\pi) = 0\). Therefore, regardless of what the interior of this integral evaluates to, it will be
[ u^2, ]
evaluated between \(0\) and \(0\), multiplied by all the constants out front. The result of this integral will be zero, confirming our expectation from symmetry.
Thus, we discover that the average momentum of the particle in a box, when it is in the \(\psi_1\) wave function, is zero. This is true because a particle in this one-dimensional box, described by the \(\psi_1\) wave function, will move to the left as often as it moves to the right. Consequently, the average momentum, which is the sum of positive and negative values, cancels out to zero.
These are two examples of how to use the expectation value and the fourth postulate to calculate the expectation value for a property. We have seen that we can predict the average value of a property even when we cannot necessarily predict the specific value that a property will have. I cannot tell you exactly where the particle is, but I can tell you that on average, if I make many measurements, I will find it in the middle of the box.
However, there is one exception to my claim that quantum mechanics is probabilistic and not deterministic. There is one case where we can tell exactly what the result of a measurement will be, which we can illustrate with this example. Let’s consider one more property: the energy.
For the same particle in a one-dimensional box with wave functions \(\psi_n\), I want to know what the average energy is. Again, using the postulate, the average energy will be given by
[ E = ^* , dx, ]
where \(\hat{H}\) is the Hamiltonian operator associated with energy. When I perform this integral, I do not even have to worry about whether this is a one-dimensional particle in a box or which particular \(n\) value it is.
I know that if this is the wave function, then
[ _n = E_n _n, ]
where \(E_n\) is the energy associated with that wave function. Since \(E_n\) is just a constant, I can pull it out of the integral, and what remains is simply the square of the wave function with one of the two being the complex conjugate.
If the wave function is normalized, that integral equals 1. Therefore, the average value for the energy of a particle in a wave function \(\psi_n\) is simply
[ E = E_n. ]
This means that the only value the energy can take is the energy corresponding to that wave function. This is true not just for energy but for any property. If I have a wave function that is an eigenfunction of the operator I am inquiring about, then the only possible measured value of that property is the corresponding eigenvalue.
Since the wave functions for the one-dimensional particle in a box are not eigenfunctions of the position operator, we have many different values, and all we can predict is the average. However, for energy, the wave functions are eigenfunctions of the Hamiltonian, so they will always have that particular value.
This illustrates how to apply the fourth postulate, and we have one more postulate to cover, which we will discuss in the next video.

crumbs: 46  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Time Dependence)

title: 46  Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Time Dependence)
section: Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Time Dependence) Video Transcript
text:
Postulates of Quantum Mechanics (Time Dependence) Video Transcript
The previous four postulates of quantum mechanics have described features of the wave function and, importantly, how to calculate properties given the wave function. The fifth and last postulate of quantum mechanics has a slightly different flavor; it tells us about the time dependence of the wave function.
So far, whenever we’ve talked about a wave function, we’ve described it as a function of position. For a one-dimensional particle, we might simply say \(\psi(x)\); for a three-dimensional particle, we might say \(\psi(x, y, z)\). However, notice that there’s no time in that formulation of the wave function. We call that a time-independent wave function because it does not change with time. The value of the wave function, whether it’s today or yesterday, remains the same.
In contrast, the wave function of any particle might not be the same today as it was yesterday or as it will be tomorrow. In general, there might be some time dependence of the wave function; it may change as time progresses. Thus, we would call that a time-dependent wave function.
If we want to understand how the wave function changes over time, we need the fifth postulate of quantum mechanics. This postulate states that for a system with a particular wave function, if it is described by \(\psi\), then the time evolution—or the way that the wave function changes with time—provides information about how the wave function evolves. Specifically, if we take \(i \frac{h}{2\pi}\), that collection of constants equals the Hamiltonian acting on the wave function.
Not only does \(H \psi = E \psi\), but \(H \psi\) also equals \(i \frac{h}{2\pi} \frac{d\psi}{dt}\). This tells us something about the time-dependent behavior of the wave function. I should note that treating the time-dependent behavior of the wave function is something we won’t spend a lot of time on in undergraduate physical chemistry; it’s a somewhat more advanced topic. For the most part, we will consider wave functions to be in what’s called a stationary state. We can understand most of the physical chemistry we want to explore without explicitly treating the time dependence of the wave function, with a few exceptions that will arise later in the course.
However, for completeness, we do have this fifth postulate. To understand what it means, it’s not particularly surprising that there is a statement like this. For example, if we set aside quantum mechanics and think only about classical mechanics, we can recall what we learned about cannonballs and predicting the motions of objects. If I completely describe the state of a system—a cannonball flying through the air, for instance—it has some position, some momentum, and a mass. If I describe the state of that system completely, you can predict not only its current properties, such as kinetic energy, potential energy, and momentum, but also where that system will be in the future. You can predict how the state of the system changes over time.
This is the equivalent statement in quantum mechanics, just as Newton’s laws of motion are in classical mechanics. It tells us how the state of the system, described by the wave function, changes over time and provides a prescription for how to calculate that change. What is remarkable, if you think about it, is that the same equation predicting the time evolution of the system depends on the same operator that informs us about the energy of the system.
We have now completed our tour of the postulates of quantum mechanics. This is the fifth and last postulate. Remember that what these postulates overall tell us to do is, once we know the wave function, to calculate the properties. These properties include energy. We have also seen that Boltzmann’s distribution and statistical mechanics tell us how to work with those energies; they inform us about the probability that particles will occupy any one of those individual energy levels. Additionally, we can use those properties to calculate further thermodynamic properties. The next step will be to use quantum mechanics to calculate some energies and then use those to derive additional properties.

title: 47  Hydrogen Atom - Schrödinger Equation
section: Hydrogen Atom - Schrödinger Equation Video Transcript
text:
Hydrogen Atom - Schrödinger Equation Video Transcript
All right, so now we’re going to talk a little bit about quantum mechanics in this lecture. Quantum mechanics, as we’ve seen so far, has been applied to model systems summarized here: either a particle in a box, a rigid rotor, or a harmonic oscillator. Quantum mechanics has been very useful so far; we’ve been able to take the results of quantum mechanical calculations, derive energy levels, and use those to perform statistical mechanics, making predictions about what systems are found in what states. We can extend that a little further to discuss thermodynamics, and we’ve gained a lot of knowledge from being able to analyze the thermodynamic properties of various systems.
However, I want to point out that the only cases where we’ve actually solved quantum mechanical problems—solving Schrödinger’s equation—have been for fairly approximate systems. It is not true that molecules behave like particles in a box, perfect rigid rotors, or perfect harmonic oscillators. These are only approximations; they are model systems.
It turns out there is one system, the next one we will study, where we can analyze not just a model system but the way this system actually behaves in the real world. The next quantum mechanical problem we will study is the hydrogen atom. Just like for the particle in a box, rigid rotor, and harmonic oscillator, we first need to determine the potential energy that the system obeys.
The hydrogen atom consists of one proton in the nucleus, which has a charge of +1, and one electron, which has a charge of -1 in units of elementary charge. Quantum mechanically, we think of the nucleus, which is a point at the center of the atom, as being surrounded by the electron. If we solve for the wave function, it will tell us the probability of finding the electron at various locations around the nucleus. At any given position, we can consider the electron to be located in position space. The wave function provides information about the probability of finding that electron and generates a probability cloud.
What we need to understand is how to calculate the potential energy of the electron, which is at some point in three-dimensional space around the center of the atom, specifically in spherical coordinates (r, θ, φ). However, it doesn’t matter if we think of this atom as being located at some point in space around the nucleus. The interaction between the positively charged proton and the negatively charged electron is Coulombic. Therefore, it doesn’t matter whether the electron is north, south, east, or west of the nucleus; the angular coordinates do not affect the potential energy.
We can express this potential energy using only the distance between the proton and the electron. Coulomb’s law tells us that the interaction potential energy between two point charges is given by the product of their charges divided by the distance between them. In this case, the charge is -1 (for the electron) times +1 (for the proton), resulting in -1 times -1 elementary charge, which gives us the expression \(-\frac{e^2}{r}\). To ensure the units are correct, we can express this as \(-\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r}\), where \(\epsilon_0\) is the permittivity of free space. You can think of \(4 \pi \epsilon_0\) as a unit conversion factor that allows us to calculate charge squared divided by distance and obtain units of potential energy.
This is the expression for the potential energy of an electron at some distance from a proton. Interestingly, we can use a similar expression for other systems. For instance, the potential energy expression would be almost identical for a helium ion (\(He^+\)). The helium ion has two protons in the nucleus, and because it is a positively charged ion, it has only one electron in the electron cloud surrounding the nucleus. The only difference between the diagram for a hydrogen atom and a comparable diagram for a helium ion would be that the nuclear charge for helium is +2 instead of +1.
Likewise, if we consider ions of lithium or beryllium, as long as we create ions that are positively charged enough to have only one electron surrounding the nucleus, the only difference is that the nuclear charge is +z, where z is the atomic number of the ion we are discussing. The electron charge remains -1, so the product of the two charges will be \(-z \cdot e \cdot e\). We can use the same expression, just including the atomic number of the element we are discussing, and apply it not only to a hydrogen atom but also to certain ions we call hydrogen-like ions. These ions are hydrogen-like in the sense that they have only one electron, similar to a hydrogen atom, but with a differently charged nucleus.
Now that we have our expression for the potential energy, we can use it in Schrödinger’s equation to find the equation we need to solve for the wave function of the electrons in the hydrogen atom. Schrödinger’s equation is given by \(H \psi = E \psi\). This becomes more meaningful when we write down what the Hamiltonian operator is. The Hamiltonian consists of constants and is expressed as:
[ H = - ^2 + V ]
We will end up solving for the wave function, which will depend on \(r\), \(\theta\), and \(\phi\)—the three-dimensional spherical polar coordinates. We are working in spherical polar coordinates because it is easier to discuss distance and angles away from the center of a coordinate system. This means we also need to express \(\nabla^2\) in spherical polar coordinates.
The term \(-\frac{h^2}{8 \pi^2 m} \nabla^2\) in polar coordinates is somewhat complicated. As we recall from our studies of the rigid rotor, it looks like:
[ ^2 = ( r^2 ) + ( ) + ]
We still need to include the potential energy times the wave function, which will look like:
[ - ]
Thus, the left-hand side of Schrödinger’s equation becomes:
[ - ^2 - ]
This is equal to the right side of Schrödinger’s equation, which is \(E \cdot \psi\). If we clean that up a little, we should think about the mass we will use in this expression. Ask yourself whether it will be the mass of the proton or the mass of the electron. The answer is that it will be the mass of the electron because that is the particle for which we are solving the wave function.
To summarize, the coordinates \(r\), \(\theta\), and \(\phi\) will allow us to solve for a wave function as a function of these variables, which represent the position of the electron relative to the nucleus. The derivatives \(\frac{d}{dr}\), \(\frac{d}{d\theta}\), and \(\frac{d}{d\phi}\) are derivatives with respect to those coordinates of the electron. The \(r\) in our equations is the distance of the electron from the proton.
What we have here is the Schrödinger equation for the hydrogen atom or for hydrogen-like ions that we will need to solve if we want to understand the wave function of a hydrogen atom.

title: 48  H Atom (Separation of Variables)
text: H Atom (Separation of Variables) (10:40)

title: 48  H Atom (Separation of Variables)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 48  H Atom (Separation of Variables)
section: H Atom (Separation of Variables) Video Transcript
text:
H Atom (Separation of Variables) Video Transcript
All right, so we’re talking about the hydrogen atom and solving the Schrödinger equation for the hydrogen atom. The wave function that we’re trying to solve looks like this: it’s a three-dimensional wave function that depends on the radial position of the electron, its distance from the nucleus, and also on two angular variables that describe the angular position of the electron with respect to the nucleus. The full Schrödinger equation is this long equation I’ve written here. There’s a kinetic energy term, which is this term in brackets, and a potential energy term that describes the Coulomb energy of the electron interacting with the nucleus. You can see I’ve written the terms that depend on the radial coordinate of the electron in green and the terms that depend only on the angular coordinates of the electron in a different color, orange.
So the question is: how do we solve this Schrödinger equation? We’re looking for a wave function that, if we take these various derivatives, take the radial derivative, multiply by \(r^2\), take another radial derivative, divide by \(r^2\), do another set of things with the theta derivatives, do another set of things with the phi derivatives, and add in the potential energy times the wave function, somehow we need to get back just the original wave function itself multiplied by a constant that we call the energy. Solving this differential equation is not trivial, but it’s certainly possible, so that’s what we’ll spend some time doing.
The first step we can see is that these \(r^2\) terms show up in nearly every one of these terms. So the first thing we can do is multiply through on both sides of this equation by \(r^2\). I can do that relatively easily by just taking out the \(\frac{1}{r^2}\) here, getting rid of this \(\frac{1}{r^2}\), and multiplying by \(r^2\) converts this \(\frac{1}{r}\) to an \(r\). On the right side of the equation, we also need to multiply it by an \(r^2\). So that equation already looks a little bit better; it almost looks like we’ve got it separated into terms that only involve radial terms and completely separate terms that only involve angular terms. The only thing that’s not completely separable about this equation as it stands so far is the wave function itself. Everywhere I’ve got the wave function written, it depends both on the radial coordinates and on the angular coordinates.
To get around that problem, we can hypothesize that the wave function, which depends on both radial and angular coordinates, might be a product of one function that purely depends on radial coordinates and another function that depends on angular coordinates. So if the wave function has this form, a piece that looks like \(R(r)\) multiplied by a piece that looks like \(Y(\theta, \phi)\), then that turns out will let us separate the variables in this equation. We’ve called the radial part of the wave function \(R(r)\) and the angular part of the wave function \(Y\).
What that means is these derivatives that we need to take—the derivative of the wave function with respect to \(r\) or with respect to \(\theta\) or with respect to \(\phi\)—take the following forms. The \(r\) derivative affects only the radial part of the wave function. Likewise, if I take the \(\theta\) derivative of the wave function, it doesn’t affect the \(R\) portion, and the \(\theta\) derivative only affects the angular part of the wave function. The \(\phi\) derivative of the wave function is the radial part multiplied by the derivative of the angular wave function with respect to \(\phi\).
We can then take those derivatives and substitute them back into the original Schrödinger equation. Again, I’ll attempt to do that without rewriting the entire equation. Where I see the derivative of the wave function with respect to \(r\), I’ll write \(\frac{dR}{dr} \times Y\). So this derivative of \(\Psi\) looks like \(\frac{dR}{dr} \times Y\), and because the \(r\) derivatives don’t affect the \(Y\) term, I can write that anywhere I like. Likewise, the derivative of the wave function with respect to \(\theta\) becomes \(\frac{dY}{d\theta} \times R\). The second derivative of the wave function with respect to \(\phi\) becomes \(R \times \frac{d^2Y}{d\phi^2}\).
Now I’ve separated the variables; I’ve written my wave function as a product of two functions that involve the radial and the angular terms, rewritten the Schrödinger equation to involve those terms, and now you can see what I meant when I said the wave function itself involves both \(R\) terms and \(Y\) terms. I’ve got a mix of the green terms and the orange terms in the individual terms in this equation. But now I can solve that problem. As the next step, what I can do is divide through by \(R \times Y\) on both sides. The term where that’s easiest to see is right here. If I take this term and I divide by \(R\) and \(Y\), the \(R\) and the \(Y\) disappear. Likewise, in this term, if I divide by \(R\) and \(Y\), the \(R\) and the \(Y\) both disappear. If I take these terms, if I divide by \(R\) and \(Y\), dividing by \(Y\) makes this term go away, and dividing by \(R\) brings in a \(\frac{1}{R^2}\) term. I can divide by \(R\) and then divide by \(Y\). The third term in brackets I can divide by \(R\) and then again divide by \(Y\).
Now we can see that we’ve completely separated the variables. I’ve got a purely radial term, a purely angular term, another purely angular term, another purely radial term, and then this is also a purely radial term. So as one last step, let’s bring this \(E R^2\) term over to the left-hand side of the equation. Now I’ve got a sum of a bunch of radial terms and then a sum of a bunch of angular terms that must sum to zero. The only way that all the radial terms can always add up with all the angular terms to zero is if all the radial terms sum up to one number and all the angular terms sum up to the opposite of that number.
If I write down in equation form what that means, the radial half of the Schrödinger equation will look like this:
[ ( r^2 ) - R = -R. ]
The angular portion of the Schrödinger equation then, if I write the terms that involve the angular terms, I’ve again got the typical constants out in front of the Schrödinger equation. The first angular term looks like
[ ( ) + = . ]
That’s the end of my angular terms. Those must sum to some particular value; I don’t know what that value is yet, but I can call that \(\alpha\). What I do know is that if I sum this green equation and this orange equation together, they must give me zero. So whatever the radial terms sum to, that must be the negative of whatever the angular terms sum to.
What we’ve succeeded in doing at this point is breaking down the Schrödinger equation into a form of two separate parts. We’ve separated the radial parts and the angular parts. What we have here are two different differential equations. Now, if we solve these differential equations for the function \(R(r)\) and if we solve the second differential equation for the angular function \(Y(\theta, \phi)\), then we can recombine those two to find the total wave function.

title: 49  H Atom (Angular Component)
text: H Atom (Angular Component) (7:53)

title: 49  H Atom (Angular Component)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 49  H Atom (Angular Component)
section: H Atom (Angular Component) Video Transcript
text:
H Atom (Angular Component) Video Transcript
Okay, so we have seen that the angular portion of the Schrödinger equation for the hydrogen atom, once we break it down into a radial piece and an angular piece, looks like this. We have this differential equation. Notice that we’re not solving for a wave function anymore; we’re solving for \(y\), which is only the angular component of the hydrogen atom Schrödinger equation. Still, we’ve got a differential equation to solve. If we can figure out the function \(y\) that obeys this differential equation, if we take these derivatives of it and add them to these derivatives, we get back a particular constant. So that’s what we’re looking to do: find the solutions to this differential equation for the function \(y(\theta, \phi)\).
As a start, we can do the following: if we take this equation and multiply by \(y\) to get rid of this term, and if we divide by \(r^2\) for reasons I’ll mention in just a second, then what this equation looks like is as follows. Multiplying by \(y\) gets rid of this term, and dividing by \(r^2\) does that. On this side, if I multiply by \(y\) and divide by \(r^2\), then I’ve transformed the equation accordingly.
The reason I’ve done those two particular things is that this differential equation might look very familiar. In particular, the \(\theta\) and \(\phi\) portions of the kinetic energy term and the lack of a potential energy term look exactly like the differential equation that we solved when we were studying the rigid rotor. If I notice that this \(\alpha/r^2\) can be identified with the energy we called energy for the rigid rotor, and if I replace the mass of an electron with the reduced mass for a diatomic molecule, replacing \(m\) with \(\mu\) and \(\alpha/r^2\) with \(e\), that gives us exactly the same differential equation we solved when we were solving for the rigid rotor wave functions.
This means two things that are fairly convenient and somewhat remarkable. The first is that, for some reason, the angular behavior of an electron around a hydrogen atom—the problem we’re currently trying to solve, where we have an electron at some distance \(r\) away from a hydrogen nucleus, which I suppose is just a proton—behaves exactly the same way quantum mechanically as if we have a diatomic molecule with one atom of mass 1 at some distance away from an atom of mass 2. Drawing the diagrams makes it clear exactly why this is true. We just have two point masses interacting with no potential energy because we’re only considering the right angular term with a fixed \(r\). It doesn’t matter whether we call them a proton and an electron or whether we call them an atom and another atom; if we keep their distance fixed and leave the radial portion of the behavior for a different day, then at fixed \(r\), a rigid rotor molecule behaves exactly the same as the angular behavior of an electron and a hydrogen atom.
So, we’ve already solved this problem, as it turns out, and the solutions that we found for the rigid rotor are going to be exactly the same as the solutions for this angular term in the hydrogen atom wave function. What those looked like for the rigid rotor was some normalization constant, some polynomial that looks like a spherical harmonic, and I guess I don’t need the plus or minus \(e^{i m \phi}\) since \(m\) could be a positive or negative number. This reminds us that there is a whole sequence of these solutions. We can have the \(l = 0\), \(l = 1\), \(l = 2\) solutions; each \(l\) can have its own family of \(m\) values ranging from \(-l\) to \(+l\). These are exactly what we wrote down for the rigid rotor wave functions; they are also exactly the same as the hydrogen atom angular terms.
Just as a reminder, the \(0, 0\) wave function was just some normalization constant with no angular dependence. The \(1, 0\) wave function had some angular dependence in terms of the \(\theta\) variable. The \(1, 1\) and \(1, -1\) wave functions for the rigid rotor looked like a normalization constant multiplied by \(\sin \theta\) and then \(e^{i \phi}\) or \(e^{-i \phi}\), depending on whether I have a plus one or a minus one here. There are an infinite number of those wave functions; any \(l\) and any \(m\) that we insert into this equation gives us a particular polynomial in \(\cos \theta\) or \(\sin \theta\) and \(e^{i \phi}\) terms, along with a normalization constant out front. These are also the solutions for the angular behavior of the hydrogen atom.
The only last thing we have to consider is that energy means something slightly different now than it did before. Previously, our energy now corresponds to this \(\alpha/r^2\). So, this constant \(\alpha\) that we wanted to know—if I take these derivatives of the angular function, what I get back is an \(\alpha\), or in this new equation, what I get is \(\alpha/r^2\) times the original function. The value of this \(\alpha\) is \(r^2\) times what we used to think of as the energy of the rigid rotor. Those energies for the rigid rotor, the energy of the \(l\) wave function, was this collection of constants \(\frac{h^2}{8 \pi^2}\) that used to be a \(\mu\) for reduced mass; now it’s \(m_e\) for the mass of an electron. \(r^2\) is in the denominator, and that all multiplies the quantum numbers \(l(l + 1)\).
What that means now for our value of \(\alpha\) for the hydrogen atom is that if I just take that energy and multiply by \(r^2\) (since there is an \(r^2\) in the denominator), the value of \(\alpha\) just works out to be \(\frac{h^2}{8 \pi^2 m_e}\). The \(r^2\) has gone away, and we still have \(l(l + 1)\). So, those are the significant conclusions we’ve got so far for the angular behavior of an electron in the hydrogen atom. It obeys any one of these functions for the angular component of the wave function, and the value of \(\alpha\) that we’ll need to solve the radial equation is this collection of constants times some quantum numbers.

title: 50  H Atom (Radial Equation)
text: H Atom (Radial Equation) (5:27)

title: 50  H Atom (Radial Equation)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 50  H Atom (Radial Equation)
section: H Atom (Radial Equation) Video Transcript
text:
H Atom (Radial Equation) Video Transcript
All right, so our next task is to solve the radial equation for the hydrogen atom. Previously, we saw that by separating variables in the Schrödinger equation, the radial piece of the equation looks like this: the various derivatives of the radial component of the wave function, which we denote as \(R(r)\). If I take these various derivatives and multiply them, then add them to these other components, what I get is a constant that we’ve called \(-\alpha\).
By considering the angular component of the Schrödinger equation, we determined that the angular behavior is similar to that of a rigid rotor. Therefore, we knew the solutions to that problem. The value of \(\alpha\) turned out to be a collection of constants multiplied by a quantum number. From what we know about the rigid rotor, we understand that the value of \(l\) can be any non-negative integer.
So, what we have here is this radial function. To solve this equation, we can start by simplifying it a bit. I will multiply by \(r\) to eliminate some terms and divide by \(r^2\) to get rid of others. If I do both of these operations on both sides of the equation, I can amend the equation accordingly. Multiplying by \(r\) and then dividing by \(r^2\) fixes the first term, while dividing by \(r^2\) turns this \(r\) into \(\frac{1}{r}\).
If I multiply by \(R(r)\) in this term, I can divide by \(r^2\) and multiply by \(r\) on the right side of the equation. After rewriting this equation, I will insert the value of \(\alpha\) and bring the entire term \(-\frac{\alpha}{r^2}\) over to the left-hand side. The rewritten equation will look like this:
[ - R(r) + R(r) - R(r) - E R(r) = 0. ]
This is the differential equation that we need to solve, but with one slight twist: we cannot solve this differential equation unless we know the value of \(l\) for which we are solving. \(l\) can take on any of the values \(0, 1, 2,\) and so on.
If I rewrite this equation with \(l = 0\), it becomes fairly convenient because the term \(l(l+1)\) makes the entire second term go away. Thus, my differential equation simplifies to:
[ (r^2 R(r)) - R(r) - E R(r) = 0. ]
On the other hand, if \(l = 1\), I have an entirely different differential equation to solve. Writing out those terms again, I have the first term involving derivatives of my radial function. Now I need to include a term that has \(l\) in it, and when \(l = 1\), \(1 \times 2\) gives me \(2\).
So, I have some constants times the radial wave function itself, to which I add the Coulomb term and the energy term, and again these must sum to zero. I could write out an infinite number of these equations. The point is that the differential equation I have to solve when \(l = 0\) is different from the one I have to solve when \(l = 1\) or \(l = 2\), and so on.
Now, I have a whole family of differential equations to solve, one for each possible value of \(l\), and that is what we will do in the next video.
