title: 70  Rotational Constant
section: Rotational Constant Video Transcript
text:
Rotational Constant Video Transcript
The energy levels for a rigid rotor model are given by this expression. They depend on the quantum number \(l\), and this formula can be used to calculate energy levels for the first few of these levels, which get further apart as they increase. Now, let’s take a closer look at this expression and point out a few things.
First of all, this quantity, if you are particularly familiar with your freshman-year physics, might sound familiar: \(\mu r^2\). This quantity can be given a name; it is called the moment of inertia. You don’t necessarily need to remember that term from physics or have seen it before, but remember that the moment of inertia means that for rotational motion, what matters for how difficult something is to rotate is not just its mass but how far away it is from the axis that it’s rotating around. For example, if I want to rotate this pen, it is a lot easier to rotate it on an axis close to my body than it is to rotate it on an axis that is far from my body.
The moment of inertia tells us how to combine both the mass of the object and the distance away from the rotational axis. For this rotating diatomic molecule that we are treating as a rigid rotor, instead of having atoms with masses \(m_1\) and \(m_2\) spinning around some center of mass, we have chosen to represent those as an object of mass \(\mu\) rotating at some distance \(r\), the bond length of the molecule, away from the center of mass. So, the mass of this rotating object is \(\mu\), and the distance away from the center of rotation is \(r\). The product \(\mu r^2\) gives us the moment of inertia.
Regardless of whether that concept from a physics class you may have taken helps you understand this equation a little better, or whether you think of it as just a way of cleaning up the notation, we can write the energy levels for a diatomic rigid rotor in this form instead, where we have combined \(\mu r^2\) into this variable \(I\) as shorthand. Sometimes you’ll see these energy levels written in that way; sometimes you’ll see them in a different formula. This simplification uses the moment of inertia for another simplification.
I’ll remind you that Planck’s constant shows up in a couple of different forms, either as Planck’s constant or as this reduced Planck’s constant \(\hbar\), which is the ordinary Planck’s constant divided by \(2\pi\). Thus, \(\hbar^2\) would be \(\frac{h^2}{4\pi^2}\). This looks a lot like \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2}\); we just have an extra factor of 2 in the denominator. If we want to simplify things even more, instead of writing \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2}\), we often write \(\hbar^2\), leaving that extra factor of 2 in the denominator. There is still an \(I\), the moment of inertia, in the denominator, and I still have an \(l(l + 1)\) that I haven’t done anything with. That equation has gotten a little simpler still, so sometimes you’ll see the expression written that way. It’s simpler written in terms of \(\hbar\) than in terms of \(h\).
I’ll continue mostly writing it with \(h\), but you’ll often see it in the \(\hbar\) form. A lot of people like this expression because \(\frac{\hbar^2}{2I}\) behaves a little bit like a mass, though it isn’t quite like a mass. It is very similar to the form that energies take in other systems, like we’ve seen in the particle in a box, which have \(\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\).
If we want to continue this process of making this equation as simple as we can by hiding different constants and variables inside of other redefined constants, we can take it all the way to an extreme. I’ll define this quantity \(B_e\), which I’ll call the rotational constant. Let’s go ahead and take all these constants—everything that multiplies the \(l\) and \(l + 1\)—and lump them into one large constant. So let’s let \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu r^2}\) be this thing that we call the rotational constant.
With that definition, whether we call it \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu r^2}\), or whether we use \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 I}\), or we could even call it \(\frac{\hbar^2}{2I}\), those are all different equivalent definitions of this rotational constant. With those, now all of the constants have been lumped into one, and I can say that the energy levels for this rigid rotor molecule are just the rotational constant times \(l(l + 1)\). Rather than constantly having to divide \(h^2\) by \(8\pi^2\) and so on, if we know the value of the rotational constant, that makes the math a little bit simpler.
Let’s see how that works with an example. Let’s take a molecule like carbon monoxide. It is a diatomic molecule with different masses on one side than the other. That molecule can rotate if we treat it with the rigid rotor model that we’ve developed. I can tell you that the bond length of a carbon monoxide molecule is \(1.13\) angstroms, and I would like to know what the rotational constant for that carbon monoxide molecule is.
Notice that I have to ask not just what the rotational constant is in general for all molecules everywhere; that rotational constant depends on the moment of inertia, the reduced mass, and the bond length of the molecule. So every different molecule—a carbon monoxide molecule, a nitrogen molecule—they all have different masses and different bond lengths, so you can have different values of the rotational constant that will be different for each individual species.
To calculate the rotational constant, I need to know not just the bond length, which I’ve given you, but the reduced mass of the molecule, so we have to calculate that first. The reduced mass for carbon monoxide is defined as the mass of atom one times the mass of atom two divided by the sum of the two masses. For us, atom one is a carbon atom, and atom two is an oxygen atom, or vice versa.
If we calculate atomic masses, we find that carbon is approximately \(12\) grams per mole and oxygen is approximately \(16\) grams per mole. Therefore, \(12 + 16 = 28\) grams per mole for the carbon monoxide molecule, which gives us a reduced mass of \(6.86\) grams per mole.
Now, let’s pause for a moment and discuss that value. What does it mean that the reduced mass of a carbon monoxide molecule is \(6.86\) grams per mole? It’s not the same as the mass of a carbon atom; it’s not the same as the mass of an oxygen atom. In fact, it is lower than either one of those. That’s part of the reason we call it the reduced mass: this mass is smaller than the masses of the atoms that I am combining to make this molecule.
Remember what the reduced mass means: this rotating carbon monoxide molecule that spins about its center of mass is equivalent, in a physics kinetic energy and angular momentum sense, to a single point with mass \(6.86\) rotating around the center, with a bond length equal to the bond length of the molecule. So, it has a larger moment arm; it’s at a larger distance away from the center of mass than either the carbon or the oxygen is. To rotate equivalently, it has a lighter mass and a longer rotational arm. Mathematically, the reduced mass works out to this value of \(6.86\) grams per mole.
We need that value not in grams per mole but in kilograms. To convert, grams cancel, leaving us with kilograms. I don’t want moles, so I’ll use Avogadro’s number to get rid of moles. After the conversion, the reduced mass of this molecule works out to be \(1.14 \times 10^{-26}\) kilograms.
We can use that to calculate the rotational constant of the carbon monoxide molecule. Now, I’ll use the formula \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu r^2}\). We know the values of all those quantities now: Planck’s constant, \(8\pi^2\), the reduced mass we’ve just calculated, and the bond length \(1.13\) angstroms. I’ve given it to you in angstroms, but an angstrom is \(10^{-10}\) meters, so I’ll convert that to SI units and don’t forget to square the bond length.
Now we have all the quantities written in SI units. The units should cancel; in fact, we have kilograms times meters squared underneath the seconds squared. This gives us \(1\) kilogram meter squared per second squared, which is a joule. So, I have joules in the denominator that will cancel one of these joules in the numerator, leaving me with one unit of joules left in the numerator. That makes sense because the unit on this quantity that we’re calling the rotational constant should be in units of energy, as if I multiply that by some integers, I will learn the energy of my rotating molecule.
If we ask our calculator what that math works out to be, we find it is \(3.82 \times 10^{-23}\) joules. That number looks very small. What that number tells us now that we have this rotational constant is that if we want to know the energy levels for this molecule, the math has gotten a lot simpler. Remember, the ground state energy level has an energy of zero. The first state is the rotational constant times \(1(1 + 1)\). Now we can say in our new notation that the energy level is just \(2\) times the rotational constant. The next one up is \(6\) times the rotational constant, and then \(12\) times the rotational constant, and so on.
If we want to know any one of these energy levels, we just take this rotational constant and multiply it by \(0\), \(2\), \(6\), \(12\), \(20\), or whichever level we’re interested in. One advantage of knowing the rotational constant is that now we have an easy way to calculate the energy levels themselves. We can also now ask ourselves how big the gaps between the energy levels are. Is \(3.82 \times 10^{-23}\) joules a big number or a small number?
Remember, the way to answer that question is to compare that energy to \(kT\) to use \(kT\) as our benchmark for energies. To understand whether this gap is large or small compared to \(kT\)—in other words, whether these energy levels are easily populated at room temperature or not—we have to compare those energies to \(kT\), and that’s what we’ll tackle next.

title: 71  Rotational Temperature
text: Rotational Temperature (11:12)

title: 71  Rotational Temperature
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 71  Rotational Temperature
section: Rotational Temperature Video Transcript
text:
Rotational Temperature Video Transcript
We have an expression for the energy levels of a rigid rotor model that has been simplified by the definition of the rotational constant. This constant is a collection of fundamental constants and properties of the individual molecule we are discussing. It has given rise to an energy ladder with different degenerate levels and varying spacings between these energy levels. An interesting question is whether these energy levels are large or small compared to \(kT\). We would like to know how the difference in energy (\(\Delta E\)) between these energy levels compares to \(kT\). The reason this is interesting is that it tells us whether the energy levels are likely to be populated. Specifically, it indicates whether many of the energy levels are populated or only the lowest few.
Boltzmann’s distribution tells us the probability of occupying a state with quantum number \(l\) and magnetic quantum number \(m\). This probability is given by \(\frac{1}{Q} e^{-\frac{E}{kT}}\), where \(E\) is the energy of that state. It is the ratio of these energies to \(kT\) that is important. Since we know these states are degenerate, the formula for the probability of occupying a particular state, such as the \(l = 2, m = -1\) state or the \(l = 2, m = +1\) state, can be extended to the probability of occupying an entire energy level.
The probability of being on the \(l = 2\) level, regardless of the \(m\) quantum number, is the same for all states because they all have the same energy due to degeneracy. Therefore, the probability of the entire energy level is equal to the probability for an individual state multiplied by the degeneracy of that energy level. This makes sense because we typically consider the energy of an entire energy level rather than individual states. The degeneracy is given by \(2l + 1\).
Let’s express the energy not as \(E_l\) but as the rotational constant \(B\) multiplied by \(l(l + 1)\), and then divide all of that by \(kT\). Now we can see that the quantity that tells us whether these energy levels are populated or not is the ratio of the rotational constant to \(kT\). If this ratio is a large number, then \(e^{-\text{large number}}\) will be very small, indicating that the energy levels will not be very populated. Conversely, if \(B/kT\) is a small number, then \(e^{-\text{small number}}\) is not that small, and the energy levels might be well populated.
We are particularly interested in the quantity \(B/kT\). The rotational constant \(B\) is defined as \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu r^2}\). If we divide that by \(kT\), we find the ratio we are interested in. We often lump a number of these constants together and rename them as the rotational constant. Now that Boltzmann’s constant has entered the discussion, we can define a new constant. Let’s denote \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu r^2}\) divided by Boltzmann’s constant as \(\Theta_{\text{rotational}}\), which we will call the rotational temperature.
Notice that most of those constants \(\frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 \mu r^2}\) were already present in the definition of \(B\); I have just divided by an extra \(k\). Thus, we can think of the rotational temperature as the rotational constant divided by one more factor of \(k\). Once we do that, \(\frac{B}{kT}\) simplifies to \(\frac{\Theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T}\).
To understand what this rotational temperature means and how it differs from the rotational constant, let’s calculate a value for the rotational temperature. We have previously discussed carbon monoxide, so let’s continue with that. The rotational constant for carbon monoxide is \(3.82 \times 10^{-23}\) joules. To find its rotational temperature, we simply divide this value by Boltzmann’s constant, which is approximately \(1.38 \times 10^{-23}\) joules per kelvin.
Calculating this ratio, \(\frac{3.82}{1.38}\), yields a value slightly smaller than 3, specifically \(2.77\). The joules cancel, and we are left with units of kelvin. This illustrates why we call it a rotational temperature; the units work out to be temperature. Whenever I divide an energy by Boltzmann’s constant, I will obtain units of temperature.
This also highlights why we prefer to talk about the rotational temperature. When I provide the value of the rotational constant \(3.82 \times 10^{-23}\) joules, it is a very small number, and it does not give much sense of scale compared to \(kT\) or whether it represents a lot of energy or a little for something as small as a molecule. However, when I say \(2.77\) kelvin, it immediately conveys a sense of what that number means. Room temperatures are around 300 kelvin, so \(2.77\) kelvin sounds very cold.
This indicates that the rotational temperature is quite small compared to the temperatures typically studied in a chemistry class. If the rotational temperature is very small, it means that the gaps between these energy levels are also very small compared to \(kT\). Multiplying both sides by \(k\), we find that \(k \times \Theta_{\text{rotational}}\) equals the rotational constant, which is similar to the differences in energies. Thus, when the rotational temperature is small compared to room temperature, it means that the gaps between these energy levels are comparably small relative to the thermal energy available at room temperature.
For molecules at this temperature with this amount of thermal energy, they can access many of these energy levels, meaning that many of these states are occupied. Conversely, if \(\Theta_{\text{rotational}}\) is not much smaller than the temperature, or if it is larger, then the gaps between these energy levels will be large or comparable to \(kT\). If \(kT\) represents the size of these gaps, climbing to higher energy levels becomes progressively less likely according to Boltzmann’s distribution, resulting in relatively few states being occupied.
Thus, when we observe that the rotational temperature is a cold number, it indicates that room temperature is quite hot compared to the properties of this molecule. Consequently, because room temperature is hot compared to that number, many of the states can be accessed, and a significant number of these states are occupied. Under different conditions, other molecules may behave differently.
Now that we have begun discussing how many states are effectively occupied under a given set of conditions, this relates closely to the partition function. Remember, one of the meanings of the partition function is to indicate how many states are occupied. Therefore, a natural next step would be to calculate the partition function for the rigid rotor, and that is what we will do.

title: 72  Rigid Rotor Partition Function - Classical

title: 72  Rigid Rotor Partition Function - Classical
section: Rigid Rotor Partition Function - Classical Video Transcript
text:
Rigid Rotor Partition Function - Classical Video Transcript
The ladder of energy levels for a rigid rotor looks something like this, with the states getting further apart as we climb the ladder. The energy levels themselves can be expressed either in terms of the rotational constant or, if we prefer, the rotational temperature. The degeneracy also increases as we ascend the ladder, with this expression giving us the degeneracy of the state of the energy level with quantum number \(l\).
It is an interesting question to ask what the partition function for this rigid rotor is. Now that we know the energy levels, we can calculate the partition function and later use that to determine some thermodynamic properties. As always, the partition function is the sum of the Boltzmann factors, \(e^{-\frac{E}{kT}}\), summed over all the possible states of the system. In our case, the states are all the possible energies corresponding to every possible pair of quantum numbers \(l\) and \(m\). Thus, I need to sum over not only all values of \(l\) (which can go from zero to infinity) but also all values of \(m\) (which can range from \(-l\) to \(+l\)).
This means we have to sum up all those Boltzmann factors. We can simplify that in one step right away because at each level, I have multiple states with the same energy. For example, there are five different states with the same energy when \(l = 2\), or seven states with the same energy when \(l = 3\). The degeneracy tells me how many of them there are, so instead of summing over all the different states, I can sum only over the energy levels \(E_l\) and just remember to include the degeneracy of that energy level.
Thus, we can express the sum as the degeneracy \(2l + 1\) multiplied by the energy \(E_l\), which looks like \(k \theta l (l + 1)\). When I divide it by \(kT\), the \(k\)’s cancel, and I am left with \(\theta_{\text{rotational}}\) in the numerator and temperature \(T\) in the denominator, along with \(l\) and \(l + 1\). So that’s the sum I want to perform over, unfortunately, an infinite number of \(l\)’s: 0, 1, 2, 3, and so on as we climb this ladder.
How do we go about doing that? There are a couple of ways we can consider tackling that infinite sum. First, it may be true that the rotational temperature is quite small compared to the temperature, which means that the energy levels are spaced closely compared to \(kT\). This implies that many of these states are occupied, allowing us to consider a large number of accessible levels without worrying too much about quantum mechanics, similar to classical mechanics where any energy is acceptable and there will be an energy level available. This would be the classical approximation.
On the other hand, if the rotational temperature is not much smaller than the temperature, we will have a quantum mechanical system and will need to consider only the bottom few levels rather than many levels. The good news is that the classical approximation, where \(\theta_{\text{rotational}}\) is much less than the temperature, simplifies this problem. As we have seen for the particle in a box, when we take the classical limit, we can approximate this sum over states (0, 1, 2, 3, and so on) with an integral over all possible values of \(l\).
For case number one, if we are in the classical limit, I can say the partition function is not this sum from zero to infinity, but it will be close to the integral from zero to infinity of the same quantity \((2l + 1) e^{-\frac{\theta}{T} l (l + 1)}\), integrating over all possible values of \(l\). We can indeed compute that integral relatively simply. It may not look super simple, but it yields quite easily to \(u\)-substitution.
The inconvenient part is that \(l^2\) in the denominator, \(l(l + 1)\), is the same as \(l^2 + l\). So, let \(u = l(l + 1)\) to see if we can simplify it. This means \(u = l^2 + l\), and when I make that definition, \(du = (2l + 1) dl\). Notice that \(2l + 1\) is exactly the factor we have out in front of the exponential in this integral. This means the universe is looking out for us and wants our calculus to be relatively effortless today.
Thus, our partition function, with that definition \((2l + 1) dl\), has become \(du e^{-\frac{\theta}{T} u}\). The limits on this integral remain unchanged; \(l\) used to go from zero to infinity, and \(u\) is just \(l(l + 1)\). So, when \(l = 0\), \(u = 0\), and when \(l\) approaches infinity, \(u\) approaches infinity as well.
When I perform this integral, the integral of \(e^{u}\) with some constants out front is \(\frac{1}{\text{constants}} e^{u}\), evaluated between 0 and infinity. I have \(-\frac{T}{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}\), and when I plug \(u\) in first at the upper limit, I have \(e^{-\infty}\) and then subtract the lower limit \(e^{0} - 0\). This is the same as saying \(e^{-\infty} = 0\) and \(e^{0} = 1\), so the quantity in parentheses is negative one. That cancels the negative one out front, and I can conclude that the partition function is \(\frac{T}{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}\).
This is, in particular, the partition function for the rigid rotor of a rotating molecule that we’ve treated as a rigid rotor. I will go ahead and box that result, but let’s make it a dashed box for now because I have some caveats to make about that statement.
First, let’s calculate the value of the partition function for a particular molecule. For instance, if we want to determine the rotational constant at 298 Kelvin for our favorite molecule, carbon monoxide, we find that the rotational temperature of that molecule is 2.77 Kelvin. This is a fairly simple calculation to determine the rotational constant: \(\frac{298 \, \text{K}}{2.77 \, \text{K}}\). Notice that the units cancel, and our answer will be unitless, which makes sense because a partition function is just a sum of unitless exponentials or a way of counting how many states are occupied.
When we divide 298 by 2.77, we get 108. So, what does that mean? The partition function for carbon monoxide, treating its rotational motion, is 108. This means that effectively there are about 108 states accessible to the molecule. Remember that when the temperature is large compared to the rotational temperature, many of the states are occupied, not just a few but roughly 108 states are occupied at 298 Kelvin. Thus, quite a few states are occupied, and our classical approximation was at least somewhat justified.
Now, back to the reason I put this equation in a dashed box: a few caveats about that molecule. First, this equation is only true under the conditions where we’ve derived it. We assumed that the temperature was large compared to the rotational temperature, so we can only use this approximation in the classical limit where the temperature is large compared to \(\theta_{\text{rotational}}\). In this case, the partition function comes out to be a relatively large number.
Additionally, it turns out this equation is only true for heteronuclear diatomic molecules, where the two atoms are different from one another, like carbon monoxide (C and O). If I were to calculate the rotational partition function for \(O_2\) or \(N_2\) or any homonuclear diatomic molecule where the two atoms are the same, I would find that the partition function is not \(\frac{T}{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}\), but rather \(\frac{T}{2\theta_{\text{rotational}}}\).
For heteronuclear diatomic molecules, it is \(\frac{T}{1 \cdot \theta_{\text{rotational}}}\). In general, the more correct formula would be \(\frac{T}{\text{symmetry number}}\), which is one for heteronuclear diatomics and two for homonuclear diatomics. For now, I’ll just note that this equation is only valid for diatomic molecules with different atoms. If we want to calculate the partition function for homonuclear diatomics, just remember to include that factor of 2 in the denominator as well.

title: 73  Rigid Rotor Partition Function - Quantum
section: Rigid Rotor Partition Function - Quantum Video Transcript
text:
Rigid Rotor Partition Function - Quantum Video Transcript
For the partition function of a rigid rotor, we have managed to understand what the answer should be in half of the cases. The partition function, which looked like an infinite sum in the classical limit, can be simplified quite a bit. It works out to be just the temperature divided by the rotational temperature. However, under conditions where we cannot assume the classical limit, we do not yet know what the partition function should be. It turns out we are left with no option other than to compute the sum, which sounds like bad news because it looks like there is an infinite number of terms in that sum. But it doesn’t actually turn out to be all that bad.
To illustrate this, let’s work through an example. Instead of carbon monoxide, let’s take a molecule that is a little more quantum mechanical: HCl. We will see in a moment why HCl is a more quantum mechanical molecule. We will consider it at a temperature that is quite a bit lower, where quantum effects become more important. Seventy-seven Kelvin is a temperature that sounds very cold, but it is not terribly difficult to reach; that is the temperature of liquid nitrogen. If we take some HCl molecules and cool them down with liquid nitrogen, we can ask ourselves what the partition function might be. To answer that question, we will need to know things like the rotational temperature.
First, we would have to compute the reduced mass of an HCl molecule, which is the mass of hydrogen and the mass of chlorine divided by the sum of the masses of hydrogen and chlorine. I will skip the arithmetic because we have seen examples of these types of calculations before. One gram per mole times thirty-five grams per mole for chlorine divided by thirty-six for the sum works out to be about 0.97 grams per mole, or some small number of kilograms. I will point out again that the reduced mass for HCl is smaller than the atomic masses of either hydrogen or chlorine. The fact that it is very close to the mass of hydrogen means that this object, with the reduced mass, is spinning at a bond length of 1.27 angstroms.
An object with mass \(\mu\) spinning about the origin behaves a lot like a bare hydrogen atom spinning around the origin. This is because, in an HCl molecule, chlorine is very heavy and hydrogen is very small; the center of mass is quite close to the chlorine. A rotating HCl molecule is essentially a hydrogen atom spinning around chlorine, or rather, something a little bit lighter than hydrogen spinning around at this distance from the origin.
With that information about the bond length, we could calculate the rotational constant. Now that we know \(\mu\), I will skip the arithmetic and tell you that it works out to be \(2.11 \times 10^{-22}\) joules. We are almost ready to calculate the rotational temperature, which is just this value divided by Boltzmann’s constant. Again, skipping the arithmetic, that works out to be 15.4 Kelvin.
The rotational temperature of this HCl molecule is 15.4 Kelvin. We can stop and think about what this means. Is that hot or cold? That sounds like a small number, 15.4 Kelvin. It is a little higher than the temperature we saw previously for carbon monoxide, which means it is more quantum mechanical, largely because its reduced mass is much smaller than that of carbon monoxide. However, notice that 15.4 Kelvin, although it is less than 77 Kelvin, is not much less than 77 Kelvin. It is no longer multiple orders of magnitude less than 77 Kelvin, so it is in the same ballpark.
We may not be comfortable using the classical approximation and saying that the system is very hot compared to the rotational temperature. Instead, we are left with the task of calculating our rotational partition function directly from this sum. In other words, first, we will do the \(l = 0\) term, then the \(l = 1\) term, then the \(l = 2\) term, and so on until we reach the end of the sum.
Let’s go ahead and start doing that. When \(l = 0\), the degeneracy \(2 \times 0 + 1\) is just 1, and that is multiplied by an exponential \(e^{0}\), which is just 1. So that first term is going to be pretty easy to calculate. The next term is where it gets more complicated: the \(l = 1\) term. For \(l = 1\), \(2 + 1 = 3\), so the degeneracy is 3. The Boltzmann factor looks like \(e^{-1 \times \frac{2 \theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T}}\). The next term will have a degeneracy of 5, and then it will be \(e^{-6 \frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T}}\). The next term will be \(e^{-12 \frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T}}\), and so on.
The bad news is that we have a plus sign followed by dots, indicating that this in principle goes on forever. But if we begin to substitute numbers now that we know what \(\theta_{\text{rotational}}\) is (15.4) and the temperature, we can plug these numbers in. The first term \(1 \times 1\) is 1. The second term gives us \(3 \times e^{\text{some small number}}\), which works out to be approximately 0.67. The next term will be \(5 \times\) an even smaller number because \(e^{-6}\) has become more negative than \(e^{-2}\). This even smaller number is 0.3, and then the next Boltzmann factor is 0.9, and so on.
You can begin to see what is happening here: these Boltzmann factors are becoming smaller and smaller, essentially because the probability of occupying each of these states is getting smaller as we climb the energy ladder. Since we are not under classical conditions, those numbers are getting small quickly enough that we do not have to include too many of these terms before they become negligible. In fact, if I multiply these out, \(3 \times 0.67\) and \(5 \times 0.3\), I think with rounding that actually comes out to 1.51, and then 0.63. The next few terms look like 0.16, 0.03, and now it does indeed look like we won’t have to include too many more of these terms before they are so small that they do not contribute anything.
The good news is that this infinite sum is infinite, but most of the terms are so small that we do not need to worry about them. Only the first few terms in the sum are important enough to include. If we calculate the first ten or so of these terms, that is all we need. It turns out that the partition function works out to be about 5.3, and we can obtain that without doing an infinite number of terms, just a handful of them.
That is the good news. In fact, this turns out to be typical when we are not under classical conditions. When we are under quantum mechanical conditions, by definition, because we are under classical conditions and this temperature is not very large compared to the rotational temperature, these numbers will become small quite quickly. We will always reach a point relatively quickly where we can terminate the sum.
So what does that mean for the partition function? First of all, the fact that we got a partition function that was about five confirms our expectation that we are under quantum mechanical conditions when the temperature is not that large compared to the rotational temperature. We are quantum mechanical enough that not many of these states are occupied. In fact, the partition function tells us that the effective number of states that are accessible is about 5.3.
Not much more than these first few states are occupied. Essentially, these numbers tell us that the \(l = 1\) states are only about two-thirds as occupied (0.67) as the ground state. These states are only about 30 percent as occupied, and these states are only 9 percent as occupied. When I add up all those fractional occupations, I get an effective number of about five states that are accessible, counting these fractional occupations.
It also confirms our expectation that we could not have used this simpler formula. In fact, if we had, we would expect that the rotational temperature is not exactly \(\frac{T}{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}\), which is not what we would have gotten if we used the classical approximation. Just to double-check and see if that is true, if we had said \(\frac{77 \text{ K}}{15.4 \text{ K}}\) for the rotational temperature, that works out to be not 5.34; it actually works out to be 5.0. That is not too far off; we would have made less than a 10 percent error in the partition function if we had been a little lazy and used this classical approximation, even though we know we did not deserve to use it.
We are quantum mechanical enough under these conditions that the correct answer, the full sum of all these Boltzmann factors, turns out to be something a little bit different from what the classical approximation predicts. This tells us what we need to do in order to calculate the partition function. If the temperature is quite large compared to the rotational temperature, we can use this classical approximation, either the version for heteronuclear diatomics or, more generally, with a symmetry number if we want to be able to use homonuclear diatomics as well. If we are unsure about using the classical approximation, then there is no harm in just calculating the sum directly when we are under quantum mechanical conditions.

title: 74  Most Populated Rotational Level
text: Most Populated Rotational Level (12:39)

title: 74  Most Populated Rotational Level
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 74  Most Populated Rotational Level
section: Most Populated Rotational Level Video Transcript
text:
Most Populated Rotational Level Video Transcript
We are now in a position to discuss the probability that each of the energy levels of a rigid rotor is occupied. The ground state might have been expected to be the most populated, but it turns out that is not quite true. If we examine the equation for the population of each of these energy levels, it includes not just a Boltzmann factor for how high up the ladder of energy states one is, but also a degeneracy factor. As we climb higher up this ladder, the number of states at each level increases, leading to greater degeneracy.
If we look more closely at this equation, we see that it contains constants throughout, but it also depends on the quantum number \(l\). If I want to know the probability as a function of the quantum number \(l\), it appears in front of the exponential as a linear term, \(l\), and the exponential takes the form \(e^{-(l^2 + l)}\). In a rough sense, it resembles \(l \cdot e^{-l^2}\). If I were to graph \(l \cdot e^{-l^2}\) or, in fact, the function \(p_l\) as a function of \(l\), at small values of \(l\), this number is small, so the function is small. At large values of \(l\), \(e^{-l^2}\) becomes very small, causing the function to decrease significantly.
What we have is a function that starts out small when \(l = 0\), goes through a maximum, and then comes back down. The key points about this are the degeneracy; as I begin increasing the value of \(l\), the probability of occupying the zeroth state, as I move to the first or second state, increases. This increase is due to the degeneracy. However, at larger values of \(l\), the function begins to decrease again. The Boltzmann factor tells us why this is the case: the higher the value of \(l\), the higher the energy of each energy level, and the Boltzmann factor becomes very small when \(l\) is large. Thus, for large values of \(l\), the probability begins to decrease again.
There exists a point after increasing and then decreasing where the function reaches a maximum. This means there is a particular value of \(l\) at which this function takes on its highest possible value. This is interesting because it indicates that the ground state is not the most occupied energy level. The \(l = 1\) state might be more occupied because, although each of these individual states has a higher energy and is less occupied than the ground state, there are three of them. Therefore, the entire level might be more populated than the ground state. Somewhere up this ladder, we reach an energy level, possibly \(l = 2\) or \(l = 3\), that is the most populated, and then the population starts to drop off as we continue to climb higher.
It is intriguing to determine what the most populated rotational level is. The behavior of a rotating diatomic molecule, whether it rotates with a little energy in the \(l = 2\) level or a lot of energy in the \(l = 5\) level, depends on the conditions. However, we can learn something about what that maximum rotational level is or which rotational level has the highest population. To find this, we look for the point on the graph that is the highest, where the slope reaches zero. Since we have an equation for \(p_l\), we can take its derivative and set it equal to zero. The derivative equals zero when we are at the most populated rotational level, \(l_{\text{max}}\).
Let’s take the derivative of this expression with respect to \(l\). The \(l\) terms appear both in the pre-factor and in the exponent, so we will need to use the product rule. Taking the derivative of \(l\), we have \(\frac{1}{q} \cdot 2\) (the derivative of \(2l + 1\)), and since I have taken the derivative of this term, I will leave the exponential alone, \(e^{-\frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T} l(l + 1)}\). I need to add to this the derivative when I leave this \(l\) alone and take the derivative of the other terms. By leaving \(2l + 1\) alone and taking the derivative of the exponential, I find that the derivative of an exponential is itself multiplied by the derivative of the exponent. Thus, I have constants in front of some \(l\).
This derivative will look like \(-\frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T}\) multiplied by the derivative of \(l(l + 1)\). Writing this as \(l^2 + l\), the derivative of \(l^2 + l\) is \(2l + 1\). Notice that \(2l + 1\) is the same as this \(2l + 1\). In the next line, I will write that as \((2l + 1)^2\). This long, fairly complex expression is what we want to set equal to zero, but fortunately, many terms factor out. I have \(\frac{1}{q}\) in both terms and the exponential in both terms. Therefore, we can say \(\frac{1}{q} e^{-\frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T}} (l + 1)\) are the common terms.
All that remains in the first term is \(2\), and in the second term, we have \(-\frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T}\) and \((2l + 1)\) appearing twice. We need this entire expression to equal zero. Since \(\frac{1}{q}\) is not zero and this exponential is not zero, the only thing that can be zero is the quantity in brackets. The way to make that equal to zero is if \(2\) is equal to \(\frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T} (2l + 1)^2\). Thus, I need \(\frac{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}{T} (2l + 1)^2\) to equal \(2\).
Now, we need to solve that expression. Remember, we are solving for the value of \(l\) that maximizes this function. We can do some algebra to isolate \(l\). If I bring \(T\) and \(\theta_{\text{rotational}}\) over to the right-hand side, I will have \((2l + 1)^2\) on the left side. Taking the square root to eliminate the square, I have this expression. I need to subtract \(1\) from both sides, so on the left side, I have \(\sqrt{\frac{2T}{\theta_{\text{rotational}}}} - 1\) equal to \(2l\). Dividing by \(2\), the final version of this equation will be:
[ l = ( - 1 ) ]
This expression is useful because it gives us \(l_{\text{max}}\), the value of \(l\) at which the derivative of the function is equal to zero, indicating the most populated rotational level.
Let’s work through an example. Consider carbon monoxide, which has a rotational temperature of \(202.77 \, \text{K}\). If we have carbon monoxide molecules at room temperature, \(298 \, \text{K}\), then the most populated rotational level according to this expression is:
[ l = ( - 1 ) ]
Calculating this, we find \(298 \, \text{K}\) divided by about \(5.5 \, \text{K}\), taking the square root, and subtracting \(0.5\), gives us approximately \(6.8\).
This result is a bit strange; we have solved for the most populated rotational level, but rotational levels are integers. They must be \(l = 0, 1, 2, 3,\) etc. Since \(6.8\) is between \(6\) and \(7\), it indicates that there are no molecules occupying the \(6.8\) rotational level. This means the most populated level is likely either the \(6\) or \(7\) level. To be extra careful, we could evaluate both \(l = 6\) and \(l = 7\) to see which has a higher population using this expression, or we can round off to \(7\) and conclude that the most populated rotational level is the \(l = 7\) level.
On the other hand, if we consider a molecule like HCl, which has a rotational constant of \(15.4 \, \text{K}\), and we ask about its behavior at an experimental temperature of \(77 \, \text{K}\), we find that it behaves somewhat quantum mechanically. The most populated rotational level is given by:
[ l = ( - 1 ) ]
Calculating this gives us approximately \(1.08\), which rounds off to \(1\). For HCl, we conclude that it is a fairly quantum mechanical molecule because only a handful of states are occupied. The most populated state is the \(l = 1\) state, meaning there are more molecules in this \(l = 1\) energy level than in the ground state. However, there are also more molecules in this level than in the higher levels, indicating that the population has begun to fall off as we climb beyond \(l = 1\).
This expression is quite useful; it illustrates the tension between the degeneracy that causes states to be more occupied as we climb the ladder and the Boltzmann energy, which causes them to be less occupied. Somewhere in the middle, there exists a maximum, and we can now calculate what that maximum is.

title: 75  Rotational Spectroscopy - Intro
text: Rotational Spectroscopy - Intro (8:45)

title: 75  Rotational Spectroscopy - Intro
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 75  Rotational Spectroscopy - Intro
section: Rotational Spectroscopy - Intro Video Transcript
text:
Rotational Spectroscopy - Intro Video Transcript
Now that we know something about the energy levels for a rigid rotor, one of the interesting things we can discuss is the frequencies or wavelengths of electromagnetic radiation required to excite molecules from one level to another. Remember how light interacts with molecules that occupy these energy levels. If there is a molecule in one of these particular states, it can absorb a photon with a specific frequency or wavelength. If the energy in that photon matches the difference in energy between two of these states, then the molecule can absorb the photon and jump up to the higher energy level. Alternatively, the molecule can fall down to a lower energy level, in which case it would emit a photon with its own different frequency or wavelength.
Now that we know the energies, we can calculate the differences between the states. These differences tell us something about the types of light that can be either absorbed or emitted by molecules that behave as rigid rotors. First, let’s take a look at these energy differences. We know that when we’ve written the energies, either in terms of a rotational constant or, if we prefer, a rotational temperature, the energy levels are either 0, or twice the rotational constant, or 6 times, or 12 times, or 20 times, and so on.
The gaps between those energy levels, denoted as \(\Delta E\), would be \(2B_e - 0\) for the first gap, \(6B_e - 2B_e = 4B_e\) for the second, and \(12B_e - 6B_e = 6B_e\) for the third. Each of these gaps is different; they get larger as they go up. The size of the gaps increases from 2 to 4 to 6, and the next one will be 8, and so on.
In general, if I want to know the transition from level \(l\) to level \(l + 1\), the gap between the \(l\) energy level and the \(l + 1\) energy level will be the energy of the upper level minus the energy of the lower level. We have an expression that tells us how to calculate each of those. The \(l + 1\) level has an energy of a rotational constant \(B_e\) times \(l(l + 1)\), but when I am asking about the \(l + 1\) level, \(l\) has the value \(l + 1\), which is now one higher, so that’s \(l + 2\).
The energy of level \(l + 1\) simplifies to \(B_e \cdot (l + 2)(l + 1)\), while the energy of level \(l\) is still \(B_e \cdot l(l + 1)\). Both of these terms have \(B_e\) and \((l + 1)\) in them. The terms \(l + 2\) and \(l\) cancel, leaving me with just a 2. Thus, I can say that the gap, or the energy level difference in going from \(l\) to \(l + 1\), is twice the rotational constant times \((l + 1)\).
It is no coincidence that these gaps increase as even numbers: twice the rotational constant, four times the rotational constant, six times the rotational constant, and so on. Now, if we want to know the gap between the 21st and 22nd energy levels, it will be twice the rotational constant times 22, the upper level.
Let’s go ahead and use some actual numbers. For the carbon monoxide molecule, we know the rotational constant is \(3.82 \times 10^{-23}\) joules. We can calculate the gap between the levels for the transition from \(l = 0\) to \(l = 1\). This \(\Delta E\) will be twice the rotational constant, which gives us \(7.64 \times 10^{-23}\) joules.
More interesting than knowing how many joules this energy level difference is, is understanding the light required to induce that transition upwards or the light emitted when a molecule falls from the \(l = 1\) to the \(l = 0\) level. If that energy difference is equal to either \(h \nu\) or \(\frac{hc}{\lambda}\), we can ask ourselves what the frequency would be. The frequency would be rearranging this expression to \(\frac{2B_e}{h}\).
So, twice the rotational constant divided by Planck’s constant gives us the frequency. Considering the units, joules from the rotational constant cancel the joules hidden in Planck’s constant, leaving us with seconds in the denominator. The units are \(\frac{1}{\text{seconds}}\), which is correct for a frequency. If we do the math, that frequency turns out to be \(1.15 \times 10^{11}\) per second.
Thus, the frequency of a photon used to excite this rotational transition from a non-rotating molecule to a slightly rotating \(l = 1\) rigid rotor state would be \(1.5 \times 10^{11}\) per second. That number may not be terribly intuitive, so let’s try instead to find the wavelength, which might provide more information.
If I rearrange \(2B_e = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\), I get \(\lambda = \frac{h \cdot c}{2B_e}\). Again, using Planck’s constant multiplied by the speed of light \(3 \times 10^8\) meters per second and dividing all that by twice the rotational constant, we find that the units again cancel out, leaving us with meters, which is correct for a wavelength. Numerically, that answer turns out to be \(2.6 \times 10^{-3}\) meters, or if we prefer, \(2.6\) millimeters.
This would be the wavelength of light required to be absorbed by a non-rotating carbon monoxide molecule to make it rotate the smallest amount that a quantum mechanical carbon monoxide molecule can rotate. If that number had come out in units of several hundred nanometers, we could start discussing whether it was in the red, blue, or green portion of the visible spectrum. However, that is clearly nowhere near the visible portion of the spectrum. This frequency is also nowhere near the visible portion of the spectrum, which means these photons are not visible photons; they are photons in a different part of the spectrum.
To make more sense of that, we will spend a little time discussing the different portions of the electromagnetic spectrum next.

title: 76  Electromagnetic Spectrum
text: Electromagnetic Spectrum (12:30)

title: 76  Electromagnetic Spectrum
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 76  Electromagnetic Spectrum
section: Electromagnetic Spectrum Video Transcript
text:
Electromagnetic Spectrum Video Transcript
When we talk about quantum mechanical energy levels, we are never very far from discussing spectroscopy. Spectroscopy is the idea of using light of some type to induce transitions between systems in different energy levels, or vice versa, using a transition between those energy levels to generate light.
What we mean by this is that if we have a couple of quantum mechanical energy levels on a ladder, the system can have one energy or another, with some difference in energy between those states. If I have a system in the lower state and I bring in a photon—some light with a certain frequency or wavelength—the energy of that photon, if it matches the energy difference between these two states, can induce a transition and lift the system up to the other state. Conversely, if the system is in the upper state and falls down to the lower state, losing energy, it can produce a photon of that same frequency or wavelength.
We know that the difference in energy between two states is equal to \(h \times \nu\), where \(\nu\) is the frequency of the photon that gets generated or absorbed, and is also equal to \(\frac{hc}{\lambda}\), where \(\lambda\) is the wavelength of the photon that gets created or absorbed.
There are multiple frequencies and wavelengths that can be generated, not only in the visible portion of the spectrum. For molecules that behave like particles in boxes, such as electrons in conjugated hydrocarbons, we can use the differences between these states to generate light of frequencies or wavelengths in the visible portion of the spectrum, such as for beta-carotene, or perhaps in the ultraviolet part of the spectrum for smaller molecules. However, these are not the only two portions of the electromagnetic spectrum.
We have ultraviolet (often abbreviated as UV) and the visible portion of the spectrum. Visible photons typically have wavelengths of about 400 to 900 nanometers. The edges of this range are a little fuzzy; depending on how good your eyes are, you might be able to see light deeper into the ultraviolet or beyond the lower wavelength edge of the spectrum. Generally speaking, humans can see light in the range of about 400 to 700 nanometers. Ultraviolet light has lower wavelengths, with 400 nanometers being beyond the violet end of the spectrum, going down to roughly 10 nanometers or so.
On the other end of the spectrum, beyond the red portion, we have the infrared portion, which includes light with wavelengths longer than 700 nanometers, extending up to micrometers and perhaps even a millimeter. At a fairly arbitrary boundary, roughly a millimeter, we enter a different named region of the electromagnetic spectrum called the microwave portion, abbreviated as MW.
While we could discuss the wavelengths of microwave photons, it is more common to refer to their energies rather than their wavelengths. The frequency of microwave photons typically ranges from low gigahertz to hundreds of gigahertz. This frequency correlates to a wavelength in the range of roughly millimeters to hundreds of millimeters.
Interestingly, the most convenient units to discuss not just microwave photons but several different portions of the spectrum are not wavelength or frequency, but a different term. We can define a new quantity, denoted with a tilde on top, as the energy divided by \(hc\). This quantity is called the wave number. By comparing some of these equations, we can see why it is called the wave number. If we compare the wavelength version of the equation \(hc \times \frac{1}{\lambda}\) with \(hc \times \text{wave number}\), we find that the wave number is equal to \(\frac{1}{\lambda}\).
A wavelength is the length of a wave, the distance from peak to peak or trough to trough. It tells you how many units of distance there are in the length of one wave. If we turn that fraction upside down, we get the wave number, which tells us how many waves fit in a unit length. For example, if the wavelength is five meters, the wave number would be a fifth of a wave per meter.
If we know the wave number, we can use it to calculate energy and convert back and forth between frequencies, wavelengths, and wave numbers as needed. Spectroscopists, who shine light on molecules or chemical systems to induce transitions or allow molecules to fall from upper states to lower states, generating light, most commonly talk about light in terms of its wave number rather than its wavelength or frequency.
Let’s work through a quick example for a photon that we are somewhat comfortable with. Suppose we have a visible photon just at the edge of the visible spectrum, with a wavelength of 400 nanometers. To find the wave number associated with that photon, we calculate \(\frac{1}{\text{wavelength}}\), which is \(\frac{1}{400 \text{ nm}}\) or \(\frac{1}{400 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}\). This gives us \(2.5 \times 10^{6}\) with units of \(\text{m}^{-1}\).
This means that 2.5 million of these visible photons, with a wavelength of 400 nanometers, oscillate up and down the electric field over the course of one meter. We typically measure the wave number not in units of \(\text{m}^{-1}\) but in inverse centimeters. Since there are 100 centimeters in one meter, we divide by 100 to get \(2.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-1}\).
Thus, instead of oscillating two and a half million times in a meter, it oscillates 25,000 times in the length of one centimeter. The wave number associated with this wavelength indicates that ultraviolet ranges from 25,000 inverse centimeters and higher, up to about a million inverse centimeters. Visible light has a wave number of about 25,000 on the upper end, while a red photon with a wavelength of 700 nanometers has a wave number of about 14,000 inverse centimeters.
If we calculate the same for infrared and microwave portions of the spectrum, we find that an infrared photon with a wavelength of about a millimeter has a wave number of about 10 inverse centimeters. This broad range covers several orders of magnitude, ranging from 10 up to 14,000 inverse centimeters. The microwave portion can drop as low as 100 inverse centimeters.
As mentioned, in microwave spectroscopy, it is much more common to talk about the wave numbers of the photons rather than their frequencies or wavelengths. This is partly because these are relatively convenient numbers. For every region of the spectrum, it is not too difficult to talk about wave numbers that range from a few hundredths or tenths up to a few thousand, making it a fairly convenient unit that avoids the need to discuss \(10^{9}\) inverse seconds or similar units. This introduces the idea of wave number and provides some statistics on the different portions of the electromagnetic spectrum that we can use when discussing spectroscopy for various quantum mechanical problems.

title: 77  Rotational Transitions Use Microwave Light

crumbs: 77  Rotational Transitions Use Microwave Light

title: 77  Rotational Transitions Use Microwave Light
section: Rotational Transitions Use Microwave Light Video Transcript
text:
Rotational Transitions Use Microwave Light Video Transcript
Here is a collection of things that we know so far about the energy levels for rigid rotors and for diatomic molecules that we can approximate with the rigid rotor model. We know that there is a sequence of energy levels described by an equation where the rotational constant helps us simplify many of these constants. The difference in energy between any pair of these states can be calculated. The difference between the \(L\) and \(L + 1\) energy levels can be given by the expression \(2 \cdot B_E \cdot (L + 1)\). This represents the gap between any two energy levels for typical diatomic molecules.
We could model anything we want using the rigid rotor model. For instance, we could take an object like this marker and model its rotational motion with the rigid rotor model. All we need to know is its reduced mass and the length of the object. However, if we use the rigid rotor model to model diatomic molecules based on the typical reduced masses and bond lengths for these molecules, we get a range of different energy differences. This variation is partly due to the different chemical properties of the molecules and partly due to the fact that each of these \(\Delta E\) values is slightly different from the others.
The transitions for diatomic molecules typically occur at energies that can be spanned by photons. If we want to talk about their frequencies, it’s in the range of roughly \(10^9\) to \(10^{11}\) inverse seconds, or hertz. Depending on the molecule and the specific gap we are discussing, these can span a pretty large range. The wave numbers of those photons are typically in the range of a tenth to a few tens of inverse centimeters.
Particularly light quantum mechanical molecules, like \(\text{H}_2\) or \(\text{HCl}\) or any molecule involving a hydrogen atom, will have a particularly small reduced mass. Consequently, the wave numbers for those transitions will be on the higher end of this range. In contrast, very heavy diatomic molecules, such as \(\text{Br}_2\) (bromine) or iodine molecules, will have wave numbers of only tenths of an inverse centimeter or so to span the differences between their energy levels.
Since we frequently discuss these energy differences in terms of the wave number of the photon required to generate that much energy, it is convenient to rewrite some of these equations in terms of wave numbers rather than in units of energy. For example, if I take the expression for the gap between any two successive states, which is \(2 \cdot B_E \cdot (L + 1)\) for the \(L\) to \(L + 1\) transition, I know that energy is related to wave number as \(\Delta E = h c \cdot \tilde{\nu}\).
If I divide through by \(h c\), the wave number is equal to the energy difference divided by those two constants, Planck’s constant and the speed of light. Thus, if I divide both sides by \(h c\), the equation now has units of wave numbers on both sides. Anytime we write something in units of wave numbers, we place a tilde on top. When I write this difference in energy but want to express it in units of inverse length, I can write it as \(\tilde{\Delta E}\). This notation indicates that we take \(\Delta E\) and convert it to units of wave numbers by dividing by \(h c\).
On the right side, I can write \(2 \cdot \tilde{B}\), which tells me that I take whatever the rotational constant is (which has units of energy) and convert it to units of wave numbers by dividing by \(h c\). This is a more compact version of the equation. It is exactly the same as the previous equation, but this one is written in terms of energies, while the other is in terms of wave numbers.
This suggests that when we are doing spectroscopy and discussing the wavelength, frequency, or wave number of light required to cause transitions between energy levels for a rotating diatomic molecule, we are more interested in the rotational constant expressed in units of wave numbers. To do this, we simply take the rotational constant and divide it by \(h c\).
The rotational constant \(\frac{h^2}{8 \pi^2 \mu r^2}\) with an extra \(h c\) in the denominator becomes a more complicated collection of constants when we want it in units of wave numbers. Let’s work through a quick example to see how this works. If we take the carbon monoxide molecule, for which we know the rotational constant is \(3.82 \times 10^{-23}\) joules, we can find the rotational constant in units of wave numbers.
To do this, we divide the rotational constant by Planck’s constant (joule-seconds) and also divide by the speed of light (\(3 \times 10^8\) m/s). Let’s consider the units. In this fraction, I have joules on top and joules on the bottom. In the denominator, I have seconds and a reciprocal second that cancel. What I am left with is nothing in the numerator and meters in the denominator. Thus, the answer will come out in units of \(\text{m}^{-1}\).
Calculating this gives us \(192 \, \text{m}^{-1}\). However, we prefer wave numbers in units of inverse centimeters rather than inverse meters. Dividing by 100 to convert meters into centimeters gives us \(1.92 \, \text{cm}^{-1}\). Therefore, the rotational constant in units of wave numbers is \(1.92 \, \text{cm}^{-1}\).
What does this mean? It means that if we do not want to think about the energy ladder, but instead want to consider the frequencies or wave numbers of light needed to excite these transitions, we can place a tilde on top of each of these quantities. The gaps between these energy levels are either \(2 \cdot \tilde{B}\) in units of inverse centimeters, or \(4\), \(6\), or \(8\) times that quantity.
If I pull up a picture of the absorption spectrum for carbon monoxide in the microwave portion of the spectrum, we see that each line on the spectrum corresponds to a particular frequency given in units of wave numbers. If there is a line, it indicates that the molecule absorbs light at that frequency or wave number. From this calculation, we would expect carbon monoxide to absorb light with energy of \(2 \cdot \tilde{B}\). Thus, \(2 \cdot 1.92\) would be \(3.84\), which should appear somewhere on the spectrum.
We also see lines corresponding to \(4 \cdot \tilde{B}\), \(6 \cdot \tilde{B}\), \(8 \cdot \tilde{B}\), and so on. For instance, one line appears at about \(7.6 \, \text{cm}^{-1}\), which corresponds to \(4 \cdot \tilde{B}\). Another line, slightly larger, corresponds to \(6 \cdot \tilde{B}\). We also have lines at \(8 \cdot \tilde{B}\), \(10 \cdot \tilde{B}\), \(12 \cdot \tilde{B}\), \(14 \cdot \tilde{B}\), and so on.
These lines correspond to many different transitions, more than I have drawn on this diagram. In fact, we can infer from the heights of these curves how many molecules are present, waiting to absorb light at a certain frequency. The peak with the highest intensity, such as \(2B\), \(4B\), \(6B\), corresponds to transitions beginning at \(L = 0\), \(L = 1\), \(L = 2\), and so forth.
Counting our way up, we find that the peak corresponds to the \(L = 13\) to \(L = 14\) transition. The height of this peak indicates that under the conditions in which the spectrum was taken, the \(L = 13\) energy level had many molecules waiting to absorb a photon. This explains why it absorbed more light at this frequency than the energy levels with lower quantum numbers, which have less degeneracy, and also more than those at much higher quantum numbers, which are so high up that they are not very well populated.
This curve emphasizes what we have already seen about the intermediate levels being most populated. Thus, we conclude that rotational transitions for these diatomic molecules tend to be in the microwave portion of the spectrum, ranging from a few tenths to a few tens of wave numbers depending on the molecule. We can calculate these quantities quantitatively based on what we know about the energy levels and the rotational constants, allowing us to predict or understand the spectrum of frequencies of light that these molecules absorb in the microwave portion of the spectrum.

text:
Rigid Rotor - Gross Selection Rule Video Transcript
If we have a diatomic molecule with several different energy levels, we can represent it as a molecule with various rigid rotor-style energy levels that it can occupy. We have determined that the energy difference between these energy levels can be expressed using a specific formula. The tilde on top indicates that we are using units of wave numbers, which allows us to predict the frequencies of light, or the wave numbers of light, that will be absorbed by the molecule during transitions between states.
The small peak that you can barely see corresponds to the transition from the zero to one state. There is another peak corresponding to the one to two transition, and the two to three transition is represented by yet another peak, and so on. However, this raises a question: why are we limited to jumping up only one level at a time on this energy ladder? Why can’t we jump directly from the \(l = 0\) state all the way up to the \(l = 3\) state, or to the \(l = 10\) state, or any state we wish? It turns out there is a good reason for this limitation, which we refer to as selection rules.
To understand these selection rules, we need to know the following equation, which tells us how strongly a molecule will absorb light when making a transition from one state to another—perhaps from the \(l = 0\) state to another state. We will discuss this in terms of transitioning from one initial wave function to some final wave function. The intensity with which a molecule in state \(\psi\) will absorb the right frequency of light to make a transition to the state \(\psi'\) can be expressed as a somewhat complicated equation.
There is a first term that depends on the difference in energy between the two states and the frequency of light that we provide. There is also another term that resembles integrals we have encountered before in quantum mechanics, but now sandwiched between the \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) wave functions is a new quantity, \(\mu_z\). We will discuss this quantity shortly. Additionally, there are some constants in front of the equation, so rather than stating that it is equal to a specific value, we will say it is proportional to this quantity.
To determine whether a molecule absorbs light or does not absorb light, whether it absorbs light strongly or weakly, or perhaps does not absorb light at all, we need to calculate this quantity, which is composed of two portions that we will discuss separately. The first term indicates that if there is a particular \(\Delta E\) between two of these states, then this term guarantees that the frequency of light absorbed will be equal to or very close to that difference in energy. Normally, we know that \(\Delta E = h \nu\). If we provide the molecule with light that has \(h \nu\) equal to that difference in energy, it will absorb light because this term will be strong.
If I graph \(\sin^2(x) / x^2\) as a function of \(x\), the graph of that quantity is large when \(x = 0\) and oscillates, quickly diminishing as \(x\) increases. Thus, the intensity of absorption is large when \(x = 0\). For us, the quantity inside the sine squared and in the denominator that gets squared is the difference between the energy of the transition and the energy of our photon. When these energies match exactly, the absorption is strong. If I deviate the frequency of light slightly from what is needed to induce that energy transition, the absorption will be quite weak. In other words, if I want to induce this transition, I need to provide the molecule with light that has a photon energy very close to that required for the transition; otherwise, it will not absorb the photon, or it will have a very small chance of doing so.
This brings us to the second term, which we will name because we will refer to it frequently. This is called the transition dipole moment. The name “transition” comes from the fact that we are discussing the transition between state \(\psi\) and state \(\psi'\). The term “dipole moment” relates to the dipole moment of the molecule that is absorbing the light. The dipole moment is typically represented as a vector. For example, if I have a molecule like HCl, which has a positive charge on one end and a negative charge on the other, there is a dipole moment associated with that vector. Chemists tend to draw that vector from positive to negative. This dipole moment vector has both direction and magnitude, denoted as \(\mu\).
The component of that vector in the direction of the oscillation of the electric field of the photon we are shining on the molecule is referred to as \(\mu_z\). If I shine a photon at this molecule and ask whether it absorbs light, making a transition from one state to another, it will do so if the frequencies match and if this condition is well satisfied. The dipole moment we are discussing here is not the vector of the dipole moment I have drawn, but rather the component of the vector aligned with the direction of the light oscillation.
In our spherical coordinate system, \(\mu_z\) is equal to \(\mu \cos(\theta)\). This is how we will use \(\mu_z\) in our integral. When we examine this part of the calculation more closely for a rigid rotor, the transition dipole moment will involve the transition between quantum states described by \(l\) and \(m\) to a different quantum state described by \(l'\) and \(m'\). The transition dipole moment can be expressed as the initial state multiplied by the dipole moment in the z-direction, \(\mu \cos(\theta)\), multiplied by the final state. We need to integrate this over \(d\theta\) and \(d\phi\) because we are discussing a rigid rotor or rotating molecule, and these are the coordinates in which our wave function is expressed. Remember that when we integrate in polar coordinates, we need to include the \(\sin(\theta)\) term inside the integral, and then we will integrate over both variables.
The question arises: when is that integral large, and when is it small? There is one very straightforward answer to that question. One way to make that integral equal to zero is by examining the term \(\mu\). The \(\mu\) in the z-component of the dipole moment depends partially on the angle the molecule makes with the z-axis. However, \(\mu\) without the subscript \(z\) represents the magnitude of the dipole moment. For a molecule like HCl, the dipole moment has a specific value, and every molecule has a different dipole moment unique to it. However, some molecules have no dipole moment at all. For example, homonuclear diatomic molecules like \(\text{H}_2\), \(\text{Cl}_2\), or \(\text{N}_2\)—any homonuclear diatomic molecule—has no dipole moment; they are nonpolar molecules.
For a nonpolar molecule with \(\mu = 0\), substituting zero into this integral guarantees that the integral will equal zero. This means that for those nonpolar homonuclear diatomic molecules, the entire transition dipole moment becomes zero. Consequently, this quantity \(I\) equals zero, indicating that light will not be absorbed, regardless of the orientation of the molecule, the frequency of light, or which transition we are attempting to make. This situation is referred to as a forbidden transition; light will not be absorbed to facilitate that transition.
For instance, I cannot shine microwave light on a chlorine molecule and cause it to change its rotational motion. This is an example of what we call the gross selection rule for rotational motion. We need the dipole moment of the molecule to be non-zero for it to absorb light. Thus, we can get molecules like HCl to absorb light for some transitions because they possess a non-zero dipole moment. However, for nonpolar molecules, because they violate the gross selection rule, all those transitions are forbidden, and they do not absorb light in the microwave range. We cannot induce the rotation of a nonpolar molecule using electromagnetic radiation. This does not mean that these molecules do not rotate; hydrogen, nitrogen, and chlorine molecules do rotate, but their rotation must be caused by some other source, such as a collision with another molecule, rather than by absorbing light.
This raises the question: are there other ways to make this integral equal to zero? Indeed, there are. The next topic we will discuss is a slightly more complicated question regarding the conditions that the wave functions must satisfy in order to make this integral equal to zero or not equal to zero. This will not involve the gross selection rule, but rather another flavor of selection rule.
crumbs: 78  Rigid Rotor - Gross Selection Rule
