title: 79  Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Example
section: Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Example Video Transcript
text:
Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Example Video Transcript
Okay, so let’s continue with the question of what makes a transition allowed or forbidden using the transition dipole moment. One feature of this expression that I didn’t point out in the last video lecture is where this comes from. We haven’t derived this expression; it comes from time-dependent quantum mechanics. If you remember the fifth postulate of quantum mechanics, it explains the time dependence of the wave function. Later on in the course, we’ll be able to derive this expression using time-dependent quantum mechanics, but we’ll put that off for now and just say that this expression is given to us.
Remember that this expression tells us the intensity of an absorption transition that involves light, going from some rigid rotor level \(l, m\) to some different level \(l', m'\). If this integral is equal to zero, then that transition is forbidden; it won’t happen, and light won’t be absorbed to make that transition. On the other hand, if that integral is non-zero, then the transition is allowed. We can compute the integral, which has a value that may be zero or some non-zero value. The value, whether it’s a large non-zero value or a small non-zero value, can tell us something about whether the transition is strongly allowed or only weakly allowed, indicating whether a lot of light is absorbed or less light is absorbed.
For the most part, what we’re interested in is whether the transition never happens at all or whether it can happen at all, either weakly or strongly. So, we’re often interested in just knowing whether this integral will come out to be zero or something non-zero. Doing so will tell us something about the selection rules for those processes. We’ve already seen the gross selection rule. One way to make this integral zero, a forbidden transition, would be for a molecule with a dipole moment of zero; that’s certainly one way to make the integral zero, which is the gross selection rule.
Now, we’re more interested in more specific selection rules about what changes in \(l\) and what changes in \(m\) are allowed, and we’ll be able to determine some rules that dictate those changes. The easiest way to understand that at first is perhaps with an example. Let’s consider a transition from the \(0, 0\) level, the ground state of the rigid rotor \(l = 0, m = 0\), up to a state, let’s say the \(1, 0\) level, which involves the excitation of one quantum number in \(l\) without changing the value of \(m\).
If we recall what the rigid rotor wave functions looked like, the \(\psi_{0,0}\) wave function is just a constant, some normalization constant. The \(\psi_{1,0}\) wave function is a normalization constant times \(\cos(\theta)\). So, those are the rigid rotor wave functions. To determine whether this transition is allowed or forbidden, we just need to take these wave functions and insert them into the transition dipole moment expression to see whether that integral comes out to be zero or non-zero.
If we do that, this transition dipole moment integral looks like a double integral. The initial wave function \(\psi_{0,0}\) is just this constant, the dipole moment \(\mu\) times \(\cos(\theta)\), the dipole moment in the \(z\) direction. The destination wave function, the \(1,0\) state in our case, is \(n_{1,0} \times \cos(\theta)\). For our integration variables, we have \(d\theta \, d\phi\), not forgetting that in polar coordinates we need \(\sin(\theta) \, d\theta \, d\phi\).
So, that’s the integral we have to do. We can simplify that a little bit; the constants can be pulled out of the integral. There’s an \(n\), another \(n\), and \(\mu\). If the dipole moment of the molecule is not changing, we can pull that out of the integral. The integral is over both \(\theta\) and \(\phi\), so if I look at any of the pieces that depend on \(\phi\), there are none. Therefore, the \(\phi\) integral is particularly easy; that’s just the integral of \(d\phi\).
The \(\theta\) integral is a little more complicated. I have a \(\cos(\theta)\), another \(\cos(\theta)\), and a \(\sin(\theta)\). So, that’s the integral we have to perform, and it’s not a terribly difficult integral. The integral over \(d\phi\) from \(0\) to \(2\pi\) is just going to be \(2\pi\). But again, keep in mind that we don’t care about the number; we don’t care whether that \(\phi\) integral came out to be \(2\pi\) or \(\pi\) or \(3\pi\). As long as it comes out to be a number that’s non-zero, then everything’s okay. If any one of the contributions to this transition dipole moment is zero, that will kill the whole thing and make the transition forbidden.
So really, all we need to know is whether the integral is going to come out to be equal to zero or not. The \(\theta\) integral, certainly, we could do this integral; it’s not too difficult to do a \(u\)-substitution with \(u = \cos(\theta)\) to resolve it quickly. But we can take advantage of this shortcut; we don’t care what the numerical value is. We just want to know if this \(\theta\) integral comes out to be zero or not.
To do that, let’s take a look at the symmetry of that function. If I just plot a few of these components of this integral from zero up to \(\pi\), I need to know what this function \(\cos^2(\theta) \sin(\theta)\) looks like. So, \(\sin(\theta)\) looks like this. I’ll draw on a different graph what \(\cos(\theta)\) looks like. \(\cos\) starts at \(1\) and becomes negative by the time we get to \(\pi\). So that’s what the graph of \(\cos(\theta)\) looks like. We’re not interested in \(\cos\); we’re interested in \(\cos^2\).
If I square \(\cos\), it remains zero where the function is equal to zero, but both the negative and positive portions of this function become positive. So that \(\cos^2\) looks something like this. What we’re really interested in is this product \(\cos^2(\theta) \times \sin(\theta)\). If I multiply \(\sin(\theta)\) times this pink curve here, the \(\cos^2\) term is equal to zero in a few places. \(\sin\) is equal to zero at \(0\) and at \(\pi\); \(\cos^2\) is equal to zero halfway through at \(\frac{\pi}{2}\). Everywhere else, I’m multiplying a positive number times a positive number until it hits zero or a positive number times a positive number until the sine function hits zero.
Generally speaking, this function is going to have a shape like that; it’s largest at \(\frac{\pi}{4}\) and \(\frac{3\pi}{4}\), and it hits zero at \(\frac{\pi}{2}\). Even those specific details are too much information; I don’t need to know where the zeros are. All I need to know is that this function is either positive everywhere or it’s sometimes zero, but it never dips negative. That’s enough for me to say that the area under this curve is non-zero and positive.
That’s after all what we’re interested in here: this integral of \(\cos^2(\theta) \sin(\theta)\) is asking for the area underneath this curve, and what that area comes out to be is not relevant at the moment. All we need to know is that that integral is, in fact, greater than zero, non-zero. Likewise, this term is greater than zero. So, without knowing the specific value, that’s enough for me to say this total integral is non-zero.
Thus, this \(0,0\) to \(1,0\) transition for the rigid rotor is, in fact, an allowed transition. If I have a rigid rotor or a diatomic molecule that we’re treating as a rigid rotor in the \(l = 0, m = 0\) state and I shine the appropriate frequency of light at it, it can absorb a photon of that light to excite up to the \(l = 1, m = 0\) state.
So that’s an example of how we would determine whether a transition is forbidden or allowed. The transition dipole moment came out to be non-zero, so it’s an allowed transition. We could work through another example where that integral comes out to be zero, and that would be a forbidden transition. But obviously, it’s going to get a little tedious to work one of these integrals for every possible transition that we can imagine. It’s much more useful to come up with a general set of rules for which changes in \(l\) and which changes in \(m\) are allowed or forbidden. So that’s the next thing we’ll do.

title: 80  Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Magnetic Quantum Number
section: Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Magnetic Quantum Number Video Transcript
text:
Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Magnetic Quantum Number Video Transcript
So, we’re interested in the transition dipole moment: under what conditions is this equal to zero, making a transition forbidden, and under what conditions is it not equal to zero, making the transition allowed? In particular, we want to explore what values of \(l\) and \(m\) make this forbidden or allowed.
To delve deeper, let’s recall the form of the wave functions for the rigid rotor. For the rigid rotor, the wave function for quantum numbers \(l\) (the angular momentum quantum number) and \(m\) (the magnetic quantum number) includes a normalization constant, a piece that looks like a Legendre polynomial, a cosine term, and an exponential term that has \(e^{i m \phi}\) with the quantum number \(m\).
If we think about how to plug the rigid rotor wave functions into this expression in the general case, we will use the \(\psi_{lm}\) wave function along with its complex conjugate. If I write down what this looks like for the complex conjugated form, the normalization constant might need to be complex conjugated, but the Legendre polynomials are not imaginary, so I don’t have to do anything there. For the term \(e^{i m \phi}\), the complex conjugate simply turns the \(i\) into a negative \(i\).
Now, if I write down what that looks like for the \(l' m'\) piece, it will just have a different normalization constant, a different Legendre polynomial, and then \(e^{i m' \phi}\), which does not get a negative sign because that one does not get complex conjugated.
If I insert those into the transition dipole moment, we have, and I’ll go ahead and simplify a little bit as we do this, pulling the constants outside of the integral. Wave function number one and wave function number two both have constants, and the dipole moment is constant, so the constants out front are two normalization constants and a dipole moment. The integral has the \(\theta\)-dependent portions, where I have \(P_{lm}\) from wave function \(\psi_{lm}\) and \(P_{l'm'}\) from the second wave function, along with some cosine terms and a sine term.
For the \(\phi\) integral, I have \(d\phi\), and the \(\phi\) dependence of these wave functions is \(e^{-i m \phi} e^{i m' \phi}\), all multiplied by \(d\phi\) and integrated over \(\phi\). That’s the integral we need to evaluate to determine whether the transition is forbidden or allowed, particularly looking for anything that could cause either of these two integrals to go to zero.
We’ll start with the \(\phi\) integral, which involves the magnetic quantum number because that one’s a little easier. We’ll consider two separate cases. Case number one: let’s consider the possibility that the final quantum number is equal to the initial quantum number, meaning the magnetic quantum number is not changing during the transition. We could also say that \(\Delta m = 0\).
When we evaluate this particular integral, I can write it as \(e^{i (m' - m) \phi}\), combining these two exponentials into one. So that’s the integral I want to perform. \(m' - m\) is the same as \(\Delta m\), and under this case we’re considering where \(\Delta m = 0\), this integral \(e^{0 \phi}\) is just 1. Since we’re considering the case where the quantum number is not changing, this integral evaluates to something that is not zero. Specifically, I’m integrating \(d\phi\) from 0 to \(2\pi\), which evaluates to \(2\pi\). Therefore, this transition is allowed if the quantum number \(m\) is not changing; this \(\phi\) contribution to the transition dipole moment is non-zero, and that does not kill the transition dipole moment.
On the other hand, if we consider the case where the final magnetic quantum number is not the same as the initial quantum number, meaning \(\Delta m\) is something non-zero—perhaps increasing by one or decreasing by one—then this integral becomes the integral of \(e^{i (m' - m)}\), which we call \(e^{i \Delta m \phi}\), integrated over \(\phi\) from 0 to \(2\pi\).
This is just an exponential integral, and although it looks a little scary because it has imaginary numbers in the exponent, the integral of an exponential is simply an exponential with \(\frac{1}{i \Delta m}\) in front. If I were to take the derivative of this function, the \(i\) and \(\Delta m\) will cancel, leaving me with just the exponential that I was integrating.
So, that is the integral I want to evaluate between 0 and \(2\pi\). I will get \(\frac{1}{i \Delta m} e^{i \Delta m \cdot 2\pi}\), which I can write as \(e^{2\pi i \Delta m}\). The second term will be \(e^{i \Delta m \cdot 0}\), which is just 1.
This is a fairly convenient result. One of the few things we need to know about imaginary numbers to deal with them in these rigid rotor wave functions is that \(e^{2\pi i} = 1\), as is \(e^{4\pi i}\) and \(e^{6\pi i}\). In fact, \(e^{2\pi i n} = 1\) for any integer \(n\).
Thus, \(e^{2\pi i \Delta m}\) will equal 1 for any even multiples of \(\pi i\) in the exponent. This means that if there is a change in the magnetic quantum number, the entire first term will equal 1, leading to \(1 - 1\) inside the parentheses. Consequently, this whole integral comes out to be equal to 0.
It turns out that any time the magnetic quantum number changes, the \(\phi\) contribution to the transition dipole moment will equal zero, making the entire transition dipole moment equal to zero. This results in a forbidden transition.
Therefore, the general statement is that if we want to induce a transition between some initial state and some final state using electromagnetic radiation (light), the requirement is that the transition we want to initiate cannot change the magnetic quantum number; \(\Delta m\) must be zero for the transition to be allowed. This is the first of two selection rules we can determine for rigid rotors, which dictates how the value of \(m\) should behave.
Next, we can take a look at the \(\theta\) integral and determine how the value of \(l\) should behave if we want that transition to be allowed. That’s what we’ll tackle next.

title: 81  Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Angular Momentum Quantum Number
section: Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Angular Momentum Quantum Number Video Transcript
text:
Rigid Rotor Selection Rules - Angular Momentum Quantum Number Video Transcript
We can continue our investigation of what it takes to make the transition dipole moment either zero or non-zero, making the transition forbidden or allowed. So far, we’ve determined that the φ portion of this integral, when we insert the rigid rotor wavefunctions into this integral, yields terms that involve Legendre polynomials and \(e^{im\phi}\). The φ portion of the integral tells us that the magnetic quantum number is not allowed to change if the molecule is to absorb electromagnetic radiation to change its rotational state.
The next question is, for this more complicated-looking integral, by how much is the angular momentum quantum number \(l\) allowed to change? We will focus on this θ integral. One step we can immediately take to simplify this integral is to observe that the Legendre polynomials in this cosine θ term all depend on \(\cos(\theta)\), while the integration variable is \(\sin(\theta) d\theta\). If we simplify this a little bit using \(u\)-substitution—or, more accurately, \(x\)-substitution—let’s define \(x\) as shorthand for \(\cos(\theta)\). Then \(dx\) will be \(-\sin(\theta) d\theta\).
Since \(\sin(\theta) d\theta\) already appears in this integral, I can rewrite the whole integral in a simpler form: the integral of \(P_m\) as a function of my shorthand \(x\) times \(P_{l'}^{m'}\) also as a function of \(x\). This cosine θ can now be written as \(x\), and \(\sin(\theta) d\theta\) can be written as \(dx\). This integral will now go from 0 to \(\pi\); \(\cos(\theta)\) makes this go from 1 to -1.
That’s at least a simple way of writing down the integral. To make progress with that integral, we could certainly plug in specific values, like the \(0, 0, 1, 0\) example we’ve considered previously. However, to do anything with that in the general case, we need to remember that there is a recurrence relation between these Legendre polynomials. We’ve previously discussed a recursion relation for the plane Legendre polynomials, the ones with \(m = 0\) as a superscript. I will write down for you now what that recurrence relation looks like for the associated Legendre polynomials, where \(m\) is allowed to be non-zero, as these very well could be.
What that tells us is that the \((l + 1)\)-th Legendre polynomial can be written in terms of \(x\) times the \(l\)-th polynomial minus \((l + m)\) times the \((l - 1)\)-th Legendre polynomial. This is similar to, but not exactly the same as, the recurrence relation we’ve seen previously for the Legendre polynomials with \(m = 0\). More importantly for our purposes, we need to remember that we don’t really care about the numerical value of the constants in this integral; we just want to know if there’s anything that will cause it to completely become zero or not.
So, I will rewrite this equation as the \((l + 1)\)-th associated Legendre polynomial equal to \(x\) times the \(l\)-th polynomial minus the \((l - 1)\)-th polynomial. Recognizing that there are some constants out front that I’m not going to bother with—these might depend on the values of \(l\) and \(m\); they might be twos, threes, or ones, but they will not be zero, and the specific values won’t affect whether the integral equals zero or some other value.
The next step is to recognize that since I have an \(x\) in this integral, and \(x\) times one of these Legendre polynomials looks like this, if I rearrange this equation a little bit, I can see that \(x\) times the \(l\)-th Legendre polynomial is equal to the \((l + 1)\)-th, and if I bring the \((l - 1)\)-th over to the other side, I’m adding the \((l - 1)\)-th Legendre polynomial. Thus, \(x\) times the Legendre polynomial is some combination of the one above it with \((l + 1)\) and the one below it with \((l - 1)\).
Again, remembering that there are some constants out front of these numbers that I’m not bothering to write down, this is enough for me to use in this expression. If I have \(x\) times a Legendre polynomial, either \(x\) times this one or \(x\) times this one, I can rewrite it as the one above it and the one below it. So let’s do that. Let’s take this \(x\) times the \(P_{lm}\) Legendre polynomial so that it’s in exactly the form I’ve got written here. Those terms I’ve underlined in pink can be rewritten as the one above it, \(P_{l + 1}\), and the one below it, \(P_{l - 1}\).
That’s rewriting \(x\) times the \(P_{lm}\) term. Those are still multiplying this \(P_{l'}^{m'}\) term. The good news is I’ve made the \(x\) go away. If I simplify this integral a little bit and distribute this product, this integral of a sum can be written as the sum of two integrals. I’ve got the integral of \(P_{l + 1}\) times \(l'\) from this term multiplied by this term, and I’ve also got the one with \((l - 1)\) from this term multiplying this term, so \(P_{l - 1}\) multiplying \(P_{l'}\).
Now, the last thing we have to remember is that these associated Legendre polynomials are mutually orthogonal to one another. If I have the integral of two different Legendre polynomials over the appropriate coordinates and the appropriate bounds, then that integral will come out to zero. If I integrate one with itself, it will not equal zero, but if I integrate two different ones, they are mutually orthogonal, so that integral will come out to be equal to zero.
This integral looks like I’ve got two different Legendre polynomials, \((l + 1)\) and \(l'\). First of all, let me point out that from the magnetic quantum number, we know that these two numbers are indeed the same. The magnetic quantum number contribution to the integral will go to zero unless there is no change in the magnetic quantum number. Therefore, for any transition that is allowed, the \(m\) values will already be equal to each other. The same applies here; \(\Delta m\) has to be zero, so the superscripts will be the same for any integral that matters.
The question is, what about the subscripts \((l + 1)\) and \(l'\)? If those are different numbers from one another, this whole integral goes away. Thus, this integral will be zero unless \(l'\) is equal to \((l + 1)\). Likewise, this integral will be zero unless \(l'\) is equal to \((l - 1)\). The orthonormality of these Legendre polynomials guarantees that this will be zero unless these are both the same Legendre polynomial.
If \(l'\) is equal to \((l - 1)\), then that will save this integral. What that means is we’ve now determined the selection rule. Let’s say we have \(l\) changing by some value. If \(l'\) changes by five, then since \(l'\) is five higher than the starting value, it is not true that \(l'\) is equal to \((l + 1)\), nor is it true that \(l'\) is equal to \((l - 1)\). Therefore, both integrals will be zero, and the entire θ integral will be zero.
If \(l\) changes by five, both these integrals are zero, and the transition is forbidden. The only way to make at least one of these integrals not equal to zero is if I’m changing \(l\) by plus one. In this case, \(l'\) could be one higher than \(l\), or if I’m changing by negative one, if the value of \(l\) is decreasing by one, then this integral will be non-zero.
What that means is our selection rule for the angular momentum quantum number \(l\) is that it is allowed to change by plus one or minus one. If it changes by any other value, then this θ contribution to the integral will be zero. We had seen previously that the selection rule for the magnetic quantum number is that the change in the magnetic quantum number has to be zero; it is not allowed to change.
So, these two selection rules together tell us which transitions are allowed or forbidden for a rigid rotor. These are the conditions that a transition must meet in order for it to be allowed; anything else in the transition will be forbidden.

text:
Rigid Rotor Properties Video Transcript
If we take a step back and remind ourselves what we’ve done for the rigid rotor so far, we started out by saying our rotating rigid object has no potential energy. We don’t expect its potential energy to change as it rotates. That was enough, plus the use of spherical polar coordinates, to derive some wave functions for the rigid rotor and the energy levels for the rigid rotor. We can use those energy levels to obtain a partition function, and then the next step, which we haven’t done yet, is to use that partition function to calculate various thermodynamic properties. We can calculate the energy, the pressure, the entropy—anything for which we have a thermodynamic connection formula. We can calculate thermodynamic properties from the partition function.
So, we do in fact know what that partition function is for the rigid rotor, at least under classical conditions. It takes this simple form when the temperature is large compared to the rotational temperature of the diatomic molecule. It takes this form for heteronuclear diatomics. Remember, if we want to allow homonuclear diatomics, we need that symmetry factor in the expression as well. So, that’s our task right now: to use that partition function and some thermodynamic connection formulas to calculate properties. We could do that with our small \(q\) partition function, which would tell us the thermodynamic properties for a single molecule of a gas. As usual, when we talk about gases, we’re more interested in a collection of many molecules.
So, we’re talking about \(n\) molecules of a gas that are all indistinguishable from one another. The partition function for those \(n\) identical and indistinguishable molecules would be \(\frac{q^n}{n!}\). Since they’re indistinguishable, we can express this as \(\frac{1}{n!} \left( \frac{T}{\theta} \right)^n\). I can either include the symmetry factor or not. If I don’t include the symmetry factor, it won’t end up changing our result, but if the symmetry factor is included, it would show up in here as \(\frac{1}{\sigma^n}\) as well.
So, what do we do with that? If we want to know a property like the internal energy, then we need to remember what the thermodynamic connection formula is that connects the partition function to the energy. That connection formula tells us to take \(\frac{d \log q}{dT}\) multiplied by \(k\) to get the internal energy. I want to know what the temperature derivative \(\frac{d}{dT} \log q\) is.
The logarithm of this partition function is a product of multiple terms raised to some power. The logarithm of \(\frac{1}{n!}\) is \(-\log(n!)\), the logarithm of \(T^n\) is \(n \log T\), and the logarithm of \(\frac{1}{\theta^n}\) is \(-n \log \theta\). So, I need the temperature derivative of everything here in brackets. There are not many temperatures here; only the \(\log T\) involves the temperature. All the other terms don’t involve \(T\). The rotational temperature is called a temperature, but that’s just a constant—a number that describes an individual molecule—so the derivative of that constant is zero. The derivative of \(n\) with respect to \(T\) is also zero. The derivative of everything but this \(\log T\) is zero.
Thus, this derivative gives me \(k T^2\) out front, and then the temperature derivative of \(\log T\) is \(\frac{1}{T}\). I have an \(n\) multiplying that term that I need to include as well. So, this \(\frac{1}{T}\) cancels one of these factors in the \(T^2\), and I’m left with \(n k T\).
It turns out that the internal energy for my box containing \(n\) identical and indistinguishable diatomic molecules is \(n k T\). If I want an intensive energy, I divide by the number of molecules, and I get \(k T\) or, if I prefer, \(R T\) since \(k\) and \(R\) are the same quantity. Let me point out that the energy we’re talking about is the rotational energy of the molecules because we’re discussing the rotational partition function. What I mean by the rotational energy will be more clear in just a second.
So, our answer is that this energy per mole is \(k T\) or \(R T\). We’ve already seen—let’s say we already know something about monatomic ideal gas molecules from when we studied the three-dimensional particle in a box. We got a similar but not identical answer before for monatomic ideal gases that we described with the three-dimensional particle in a box. We found that the internal energy was \(\frac{3}{2} R T\). Now we found \(R T\).
The reason for the difference between these two, and the reason I’m saying monatomic ideal gases for what we described with the particle in a box, let me draw a small cartoon here of some gas molecules in a box. If I have monatomic gases like argon, neon, or helium that just bounce around in this box, the only type of energy they have is kinetic energy. The only type of kinetic energy they have is this translational energy as their center of mass moves around the box, as the atom itself moves around the box.
On the other hand, if I have a diatomic molecule like \(N_2\), \(O_2\), or \(HCl\), those molecules can also translate and bounce around the box, but they can also rotate. So, the energy that these molecules have is one portion that is translational energy, which comes from the particle in a box, and another portion that is rotational. That’s the part we’ve just figured out the answer to.
The rotational energy, in particular, the energy that we get by describing the molecule as stationary but spinning, is \(R T\). Thus, a diatomic molecule has an internal energy not of \(\frac{3}{2} R T\), but rather \(\frac{3}{2} R T\) from bouncing around in the box (translational energy) and an extra factor of \(R T\) from its rotational energy, giving a total energy of \(\frac{5}{2} R T\).
Now, finally, these expressions are beginning to agree with what you may have learned in a previous chemistry course, like general chemistry. The energy of a monatomic ideal gas is \(\frac{3}{2} R T\), while for a diatomic ideal gas, it is \(\frac{5}{2} R T\). If we also calculate the heat capacities, the constant volume heat capacity, remember, is \(C_V = \frac{dU}{dT}\). Just take the temperature derivative of these expressions, and the temperature goes away.
We’ve already known that the heat capacity of a monatomic ideal gas or the three-dimensional particle in a box tells us that it is \(\frac{3}{2} R\). Now we find that for a diatomic ideal gas, it’s \(\frac{5}{2} R\)—three halves of which comes from the translational energy (particle in a box energy) and one factor of \(R\) which comes from the rotational contribution to the energy.
We also know about a monatomic ideal gas that pressure times volume equals \(R T\). So, the energy and the heat capacity both changed when we started including rotational motion. Let’s see what happens to the pressure of an ideal gas when we include this rotational motion. To do that, we need to back up to this portion, this step, and begin with another thermodynamic connection formula.
This was the thermodynamic connection formula for the energy. The thermodynamic connection formula that lets us calculate the pressure is similar: not \(k T^2\), but \(k T\). Instead of \(\frac{d}{dT}\), we have \(\frac{d}{dV}\). The volume derivative of \(\log q\) is what we need to take.
So, I need to take the volume derivative of the same \(\log q\), the same quantity in brackets that we have discussed before. That is \(-\log(n!) + n \log T - n \log \theta\). I need to take the volume derivative of this quantity in parentheses, and that’s even easier than the last derivative. There are no volumes that appear in here anywhere. The number of molecules in the gas doesn’t depend on the volume, and the rotational constant of the gas doesn’t depend on the volume. None of these quantities depend on what the volume is, so the volume derivative of this quantity is zero.
Thus, the pressure of a diatomic gas is zero. Does that make sense? It does if you remember that this is the rotational contribution to the pressure. This is the extra contribution to the pressure that comes from the fact that this molecule is rotating. The pressure, the force per unit area we feel on the wall when the gas collides with the wall, all comes from the translational energy. The fact that it’s rotating as it collides with that wall doesn’t affect the pressure.
Here’s a diatomic molecule about to collide with the wall. If it happens to be spinning in this direction, it might have a little extra kick as it hits the wall, but equally often, it’s spinning the other way, and its kick is a little bit reduced as it hits the wall. So, on average, the contribution to the pressure from the rotational motion of the molecule is zero, as the thermodynamic connection formula has told us.
For a diatomic molecule, it’s also true that \(PV = nRT\). Pressure times molar volume is equal to \(RT\) for a diatomic gas, which has this rotational contribution as well. So, the rotational contribution has changed the energy and the heat capacity, but it has not changed the ideal gas law, the ideal gas equation of state.
This tells us about the rotational contributions to the energy, heat capacity, and pressure. We haven’t yet talked about the vibrational contributions. In addition to rotating, a diatomic molecule also has a vibrational degree of freedom; the bond length can change. We’ve ignored that bond length when we talked about a rigid rotor, but the next step will be to continue this analysis, do some more quantum mechanics, feeding eventually into thermodynamic properties, and see what effect the vibrational properties of the molecule have. So, that’s coming up next.

title: 83  3D Particle in a Box (Solutions)
section: 3D Particle in a Box (Solutions) Video Transcript
text:
3D Particle in a Box (Solutions) Video Transcript
We’ve seen that the one-dimensional particle in a box is a problem that we can solve using Schrödinger’s equation. Here’s a reminder of what Schrödinger’s equation looks like for the one-dimensional particle in a box. We gained some insights from solving that problem and discovered that it’s a useful model for describing things like an electron in a linear conjugated hydrocarbon molecule. It may also be useful for a small number of other scenarios. However, in general, problems in the three-dimensional world are not well described by a one-dimensional particle in a box. Therefore, we will gain much more knowledge by solving the same particle in a box problem in three dimensions rather than just one dimension. That’s our next task.
To recap how we approached the one-dimensional particle in a box, we started by writing down Schrödinger’s equation. There is a kinetic energy term, and the potential energy term was zero because we assumed the particle has no potential energy. So, that’s our one-dimensional particle in a box Schrödinger’s equation. We could then solve Schrödinger’s equation to write down the wave functions. Any function that resembles a sine wave will have a second derivative that is the negative of the original function, so these will all be solutions to this Schrödinger’s equation. However, we require that the solutions be zero at the edges of the box, which means we can only have sine waves that end at the edges of the box.
Solving Schrödinger’s equation gives us one set of solutions that obey the boundary conditions, making those sine waves end at specific places defined by the edges of our box. This imposes additional restrictions, and we found that the one-dimensional particle in a box wave function must look like \(\sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{a}\right)\), where \(n\) is an integer and \(a\) is the box length. This is a quick recap of what we did for our one-dimensional particle in a box. Remember this step: we required that at the two edges of the box, when we plug in \(x = 0\) or \(x = a\), we must get zero for the value of the wave function. This is how \(k\) became \(\frac{n \pi}{a}\).
Now, we will do the exact same thing for the three-dimensional particle in a box. We can start by writing down Schrödinger’s equation. It is still a particle in a box, and we will assume that when the molecule is inside the box, there is no potential energy, and it is not allowed to be outside the box. Thus, we still have just a kinetic energy operator. The only difference is that instead of having \(\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\), we need the full Laplacian, which means we need the derivatives not just with respect to \(x\) but also with respect to \(y\) and \(z\). So, we take the derivatives of the wave function and sum them together, multiplying by the constants out front to obtain a constant times the wave function.
Now we’ve written the three-dimensional version of Schrödinger’s equation, just like the one-dimensional version, but we have \(y\) and \(z\) as well. I should point out that this wave function itself is now a function of \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) instead of just a function of \(x\) as it was for the one-dimensional problem. If we were to solve this equation formally, as you would have done in a differential equations class, we would invoke separation of variables. We have some \(x\) derivatives that are separate from the \(y\) and \(z\) derivatives.
Without being overly formal, we can write down what the solution should look like and then confirm that those solutions are indeed solutions to Schrödinger’s equation. Since we know how to solve the problem if we only had the \(x\) derivative, the solutions would look like this. That’s a good starting place. However, since we also have \(y\) and \(z\) portions, the wave function turns out to be the product of the \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) portions. There is no difference between \(\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\), \(\frac{d^2}{dy^2}\), and \(\frac{d^2}{dz^2}\) other than the variable names. Thus, we multiply together \(\sin(k_x x)\), \(\sin(k_y y)\), and \(\sin(k_z z)\). In fact, there is no necessity for these \(k\) values to be the same; I could have a different constant \(k_x\) for \(x\), a different constant \(k_y\) for \(y\), and a different constant \(k_z\) for \(z\). There could be three different constants in this expression.
This is the equivalent of the equation I have here, where we’ve solved Schrödinger’s equation. If we took this expression and plugged it back into Schrödinger’s equation, we could confirm that taking the second derivatives of this function indeed gives us back the same function we started with, multiplied by some constants.
Step number two proceeds very similarly to step number two in one dimension, keeping in mind that we’re now in three dimensions instead of one. Instead of having a one-dimensional region confining our particle to keep it in the box, we have a three-dimensional box. The potential energy is zero inside this box, and the particle is not allowed to be outside the box. The wave function must be zero at the edges of the one-dimensional box, and the same must be true for the edges of the box in \(x\). The box may range from \(0\) to \(a\) in \(x\). We can choose a different length \(b\) for the box in the \(y\) direction and let \(c\) be the height of the box in the \(z\) direction.
This means my new boundary conditions require that whenever the particle is at the left edge of the box (when \(x = 0\)), the wave function must be zero. When \(x\) is at the right edge of the box, the wave function must also be zero. This is akin to saying that anytime the particle is at the right face of the box, the entire right side of the box must have a wave function of zero. The first condition is similar for the left face of the box. There are equivalent boundary conditions for the top and bottom of the box, as well as the front and back. Therefore, I also need the wave function to be zero when \(y = 0\), \(y = b\), \(z = 0\), or \(z = c\).
These are the six conditions for the six different sides of the box—not just left and right, but also front and back, and top and bottom. After applying these conditions, we place restrictions on \(x\) that work exactly the same way as they did for the one-dimensional particle in a box. This will turn \(k_x\) into some integer multiple of \(\frac{\pi}{a}\). The wave function must oscillate an integer number of times when it goes from the left edge of the box to the right edge. The conditions on \(y\) will impose restrictions on \(k_y\), and the conditions on \(z\) will impose restrictions on \(k_z\).
By the time we apply all these conditions, the result will look very similar to the solution in one dimension. We will find that the wave function looks like \(\sin\left(\frac{n_x \pi x}{a}\right) \cdot \sin\left(\frac{n_y \pi y}{b}\right) \cdot \sin\left(\frac{n_z \pi z}{c}\right)\). However, these constants do not need to be equal; the number of oscillations in the \(x\) direction does not have to match the number of oscillations in the \(y\) direction or the \(z\) direction. Thus, \(n_x\), \(n_y\), and \(n_z\) can be different values.
Just like in the one-dimensional problem, when we applied the boundary conditions, we went from an infinite number of solutions with any possible value of \(k\) to a discretized set of solutions where these \(n\) values had to be integers. We started labeling the wave function \(\psi_n\). Here, we will label these wave functions not just by a single \(n\) but by the three \(n\) values \(n_x\), \(n_y\), and \(n_z\) that we need to evaluate it.
For any choice of \(n_x\), \(n_y\), and \(n_z\), where each \(n\) is a positive integer, I can write down a wave function that will obey Schrödinger’s equation. To confirm that these wave functions do indeed solve the three-dimensional particle in a box Schrödinger equation, let’s convince ourselves of that. If this wave function solves Schrödinger’s equation, taking the second \(x\) derivative, adding it to the second \(y\) derivative, and adding it to the second \(z\) derivative multiplied by these constants will yield the original wave function.
The second \(x\) derivative of my wave function involves only the sine term in the \(x\) portion; the \(y\) and \(z\) terms do not contain any \(x\) variables. Taking the first derivative of sine gives cosine, and taking another derivative will return to negative sine. Each time I take a derivative, it will pull out some constants, specifically \(\frac{n \pi}{a}\). Thus, I will have \(-\frac{n_x^2 \pi^2}{a^2} \psi\). The same logic applies for \(y\) and \(z\); the second \(y\) derivative will yield \(-\frac{n_y^2 \pi^2}{b^2} \psi\), and the second \(z\) derivative will yield \(-\frac{n_z^2 \pi^2}{c^2} \psi\).
These are all the building blocks I need to evaluate Schrödinger’s equation. If I take that and plug our wave function into Schrödinger’s equation, it tells us that
[ - ( + + ) = E . ]
This collection of terms is equal to some constants times the wave function, and it is true. Everything in parentheses is a constant, so that is equal to constants times the wave function. This also allows us to evaluate the energy of a three-dimensional particle in a box. For this particular particle in a box, with choices of \(n_x\), \(n_y\), and \(n_z\) as integers, we find that the energy is given by
[ E = ( + + ). ]
This is the result for a three-dimensional particle in a box. We now know what the wave function is for a three-dimensional particle in a box given an \(n\) for each of the \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) directions, and we can also calculate the energy of that three-dimensional particle in a box.
The last thing we need to do is consider the common case where we have a cubic box. Notice that I didn’t assume that the lengths of the box in \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) are equal. However, if we assume that \(a = b = c\), then these constants are all equal. In that special case, I can write the energy of the wave function as
[ E = ( n_x^2 + n_y^2 + n_z^2 ). ]
In the fairly common case where we have a cubic box, there is a somewhat simpler expression for calculating the energy. For a non-cubic box, I would use the previous expression, but for a cubic box, I would use this simpler expression.
We are almost done writing down the wave functions for the three-dimensional particle in a box, but I should point out that I haven’t yet specified what this constant \(a\) is, nor have we figured out what that constant \(a\) is. Therefore, we still need to normalize the wave function, and that will be the next step we take.

title: 84  3D Particle in a Box (Normalization)
section: 3D Particle in a Box (Normalization) Video Transcript
text:
3D Particle in a Box (Normalization) Video Transcript
Okay, we know how to write down the wave function for a three-dimensional particle in a box. A particle can exist in a box where it has no potential energy inside and is confined to not leave the box. We almost know how to write down the wave function; however, we haven’t yet talked about the value of the constant \(a\).
The way we determine that constant, as we did for the one-dimensional particle in a box, is by normalizing the wave function. Recall that normalization means the particle must exist somewhere, with a 100 percent chance of being found somewhere in space. For the particle in a box, this means a 100 percent chance of being found somewhere in the box. Therefore, if I integrate the probability everywhere, I must get 100 percent. The probability is the wave function squared, in this particular quantum mechanical way of squaring.
So, I just need to take this wave function, square it, and I don’t have to worry about complex conjugation because there are no imaginary numbers in this wave function. I just need to square the wave function, integrate it, and ensure it equals 1, then figure out the value of \(a\) that makes that true.
Writing down what that integral is equal to, the wave function is this long expression. If I square it, I will get \(a^2 \sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi x}{a}\right) \sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi y}{b}\right) \sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi z}{c}\right)\). I need to ask myself what I’m integrating with respect to; I’m not just integrating with respect to \(x\) because I have a three-dimensional function. So, I have \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\), and I need to integrate with respect to all three. This is actually a triple integral. I could write a triple integral sign here, so let me go ahead and do that.
I have a triple integral over \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\). The integral I have to perform can be separated into three parts: the integral of \(\sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi x}{a}\right) \, dx\) for the \(x\) integral, the integral of \(\sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi y}{b}\right) \, dy\) for the \(y\) integral, and a separate integral of \(\sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi z}{c}\right) \, dz\) for the \(z\) integral.
All I have left to do now is remind myself what the limits of these integrations are. If I’ve confined my particle to a box that runs from 0 to \(a\) in the \(x\) direction, from 0 to \(b\) in the \(y\) direction, and from 0 to \(c\) in the \(z\) direction, then those box lengths match the box lengths to ensure our wave function hits zero at the edges of the box.
The first integral I have to do is the integral of \(\sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi x}{a}\right)\) from 0 to \(a\). That’s exactly the same integral we had to do for the one-dimensional particle in a box. I’ll remind you what the answer was when we discussed that integral previously: it was \(\frac{a}{2}\).
For exactly the same reasons, the integral of \(\sin^2\left(\frac{n \pi y}{b}\right)\) integrated from 0 to \(b\) is the same mathematical problem, so that will also be \(\frac{b}{2}\). However, the box length isn’t \(a\) in this case; it is \(b\). The result of the third integral will be \(\frac{c}{2}\).
So, it’s the same problem we did for the one-dimensional particle in a box; we just do it three times and multiply the results together. The product \(a^2 \times b \times c\) in the numerator and \(2 \times 2 \times 2\) in the denominator must equal 1. Since we’re interested in solving for the value of \(a\), \(a^2\) needs to be \(\frac{8}{abc}\), where \(abc\) is the volume of the box.
Thus, the normalization constant \(a\) is the square root of either of these quantities. I can write it as \(\sqrt{\frac{8}{abc}}\) or \(\sqrt{\frac{8}{V}}\). In fact, I can express it as \(\sqrt{2}\) times \(\sqrt{2}\) times \(\sqrt{2}\), which is where the 8 came from. I can write this as \(\sqrt{\frac{2}{a}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{b}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{c}}\).
The reason it’s worth writing it out in this more complicated form is just to remind us that this is the normalization constant for a one-dimensional particle in the \(x\)-direction, multiplied by the normalization constant for a one-dimensional particle in the \(y\)-direction, and then multiplied by the normalization constant in the \(z\)-direction.
Any one of these is an appropriate way to write the normalization constant for the special case where we have a cubic box, where the length of the box is the same in the \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) directions. In that case, each of these \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) is the same, and the normalization constant simplifies further to \(\sqrt{\frac{8}{a^3}}\) if \(abc\) is the same as \(a \times a \times a\), or we can also write that as \(\frac{2}{a^{3/2}}\).
So, any one of these is the normalization constant for a cubic box, and any one of these is the normalization constant for a non-cubic box. That’s the constant we would insert here if we want to write down the full, complete wave function for the three-dimensional particle in a box.

title: 85  3D Particle in a Box (Applications)
section: 3D Particle in a Box (Applications) Video Transcript
text:
3D Particle in a Box (Applications) Video Transcript
All right, we’ve accomplished the goal of extending the particle in a box problem from one dimension into three dimensions. We now have an expression for the wave function of the particle in a box, including a normalization constant. We can also determine the energies for a particle confined to this three-dimensional box.
We might ask ourselves what real-world problems this could be applicable to, just as we did for the one-dimensional particle box. It turned out that linear conjugated hydrocarbons had electrons that were well described by the one-dimensional particle box. Now, we can consider what problems the three-dimensional particle in a box can help us with.
Remembering the assumptions we’ve made in deriving the particle in a box model, we assume that the potential energy is zero inside the box, that the particle is confined to that box, and that, in this particular case, the particle is three-dimensional. This is not much of an assumption; any problem in the real world will be a three-dimensional problem. We just need to ask ourselves what types of problems exist where a particle is confined to some region of three-dimensional space, where the energy is the same everywhere within that volume, and where it cannot leave the volume.
I should point out that having solved the three-dimensional particle in a box, the one-dimensional particle in a box is actually a special case of this. If we have a problem confined only to one dimension instead of all three dimensions, we simply ignore the y and z portions of the wave function and the y and z contributions to the energy. This portion of the wave function, along with part of the normalization constant, and this portion of the energy, yield exactly the result we had for the one-dimensional particle in a box. Likewise, if we find a particle confined to two dimensions, we can call those x and y and only include the x and y contributions. Thus, the three-dimensional particle in a box can be narrowed down to the solutions for either the two-dimensional or the one-dimensional particle in a box as needed.
To understand what types of systems the particle in a box model applies to, we need to consider what the particles are and what the box is that they are confined to. We have seen an electron confined to a linear hydrocarbon molecule with some conjugation. Linear molecules like this one provide a one-dimensional example. It is not exactly true, but we can imagine that the particle is confined to only one dimension within the box. Between the left and right edges of the box, there is relatively little difference in energy for a conjugated pi electron along the backbone of that molecule, which represents the one-dimensional particle box.
There is a very similar example if we think of a conjugated molecule confined to two dimensions. We can draw organic molecules like naphthalene, or if we add a third benzene ring, we get anthracene. By drawing conjugated planar organic molecules, such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, where the pi electrons in these connected benzene rings are delocalized over the entire molecule, we can conceptualize the box in this case as a two-dimensional box. It has length and height, but no depth, making it analogous to a two-dimensional particle in a box model.
Now we can cover many more molecules than just these linear ones. There are also three-dimensional versions of the same problem. If we consider what a three-dimensional object looks like, where an electron within this three-dimensional volume is free to move anywhere inside but cannot leave the volume, we could think of three-dimensional chemical molecules that allow the electron to flow freely within them. Essentially, we are talking about a solid that is conductive; the electron can flow within the solid but cannot leave it. In this case, we are referring to a metal. If I have a block of metal, the electron can move freely within it, which would be a particle in a box where the metal itself is the box and the electron is the particle. This serves as an example of a case where we could apply the three-dimensional particle in a box model—not just to metals, but to any conductive material.
As a fourth case, we have considered situations where the particle is an electron, which makes sense because quantum mechanics deals with small objects, and electrons are the smallest objects that chemists routinely encounter. Confined small quantum mechanical electrons to a box is well described by these quantum mechanical models. However, what if the object is not an electron? What if the object itself is a molecule confined to a container? Here, I wouldn’t be talking about a molecule confined to a metal; molecules do not diffuse within the interior of a metal. For a molecule to move back and forth inside this box, it must be a literal box or container.
For example, if I have a molecule inside this box that is bouncing around and able to move freely within it, but the box has walls so it cannot escape, I have essentially described the case of a gas—a physical gas. If I have molecules confined to a box, and those molecules can fill their container and move everywhere within it, then that is what we think of as a gas. This will be another three-dimensional example for which we could use the particle in a box energy levels and wave functions to describe how that system behaves.
It turns out we will gain quite a lot of insight from using the three-dimensional particle in a box model to describe how gases behave. But before we move on to that, we need to discuss one more thing about the energy levels for the three-dimensional particle in a box, which will help us understand how to apply it to gases.
crumbs: 85  3D Particle in a Box (Applications)

title: 86  3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy)
text: 3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy) (11:55)
crumbs: 86  3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy)

title: 86  3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy)
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel
crumbs: 86  3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy)

title: 86  3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy)
section: 3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy) Video Transcript
text:
3D Particle in a Box (Degeneracy) Video Transcript
One important feature to be aware of in the energy levels for a particle in a box, specifically a three-dimensional particle in a box, is the degeneracy of those energy levels. To understand this, let’s pay a little closer attention to the expression we have for the energy levels of an individual particle in a box. If I choose \(n_x\), \(n_y\), and \(n_z\) as some integer values, I can calculate the energy of that particle in that three-dimensional box.
Let’s draw an energy ladder. Here are the energy levels for the states of that particle in a box. As I mentioned, for any integers \(n_x\), \(n_y\), and \(n_z\), I can calculate the energy and place that energy on this ladder. The lowest possible energy, or the ground state for that particle in a box, occurs when I make \(n\) equal to one. So, if I choose \(n_x = 1\), \(n_y = 1\), and \(n_z = 1\), that will be the lowest energy level, denoted as \(E_{1,1,1}\).
This energy is equal to
[ E_{1,1,1} = ( + + ). ]
If I ask myself what the next step up on this energy ladder is, I can take one of these ones and change it to a two. Which one I change depends on the relative sizes of the box dimensions. If one box size is relatively large, then it doesn’t cost much energy to increase that dimension. However, in general, without knowing the specific sizes of the box lengths, somewhere above the \(1,1,1\) energy level, there will be \(E_{1,1,2}\), \(E_{1,2,1}\), and \(E_{2,1,1}\) energy levels. Unless the box sizes happen to be equal, there is no reason that the energy for \(E_{1,1,2}\) should be the same as that for \(E_{1,2,1}\) or \(E_{2,1,1}\) because the box lengths will generally not be the same.
I can continue to add more levels to this tree. I can increase some of the twos to threes or have more than one of them be two at the same time. But before we do that, let’s consider what this energy level diagram looks like when I have a cubic box, where the box lengths in \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) are all equal to each other. In that case, the energies simplify a bit because \(a^2\), \(b^2\), and \(c^2\) are all the same number. I can factor them out of this expression and just compute the sums of these \(n^2\) values.
If I draw what the energy ladder looks like for that case, again starting from the ground state, the lowest energy can be achieved by using ones for each of those constants. Then, \(1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 = 3\). So, there is the \(1,1,1\) energy level. The next step up on the ladder can be achieved by making \(n_z = 2\), \(n_y = 2\), or \(n_x = 2\). It doesn’t matter which one I change to two; I will get the same energy in any case. For example, \(2^2 + 1^2 + 1^2\) is the same as \(1^2 + 2^2 + 1^2\) or \(1^2 + 1^2 + 2^2\).
This means that somewhere higher up on this energy ladder, there are three degenerate energy levels: \(E_{1,1,2}\), \(E_{1,2,1}\), and \(E_{2,1,1}\). They all have the same energy, which is
[ E = . ]
This energy is twice as high as the ground state but triply degenerate, indicating a degeneracy of three. Let me go ahead and label these states as having a degeneracy of three because there are three states with \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) excitations that all have the same energy.
Continuing to climb this energy ladder, if I ask myself what the next highest energy level is, I could make two of the indices equal to two. For example, let’s write down the state \(E_{2,2,1}\). Here, I have made \(n_x = 2\) and \(n_y = 2\), while leaving \(n_z = 1\). This gives me an energy of
[ E = . ]
This state is also triply degenerate because the states \(E_{2,1,2}\) and \(E_{1,2,2}\) all have the same energy as each other. It doesn’t matter in what order I sum those \(n\) values; they yield the same result.
Let’s do one more example. If I set all three indices to two, so \(n_x = 2\), \(n_y = 2\), and \(n_z = 2\), the energy will be
[ E = . ]
Notice that this state is not degenerate. The indices \(n_x = 2\), \(n_y = 2\), and \(n_z = 2\) do not have different permutations that yield the same energy level. Thus, there is only a single \(E_{2,2,2}\) state.
Some of the states are degenerate, while others are not. This is one interesting feature of the particle in a box energy levels. Another interesting feature arises if I consider using different integers. For instance, if I lift one of the dimensions to the \(n = 3\) state while keeping the other two in the \(n = 1\) state, say \(n_x = 3\), \(n_y = 1\), and \(n_z = 1\), then
[ E = . ]
This energy level corresponds to a triply degenerate state that includes not only \(E_{3,1,1}\) but also \(E_{1,3,1}\) and \(E_{1,1,3}\).
This highlights a somewhat frustrating aspect of the energy levels for the particle in a box: it can be difficult to determine which energy level will come next without performing the calculations. I want to know if my next state will be the \(3,2,1\) state or one of the other excitations. I cannot know for sure without plugging in the numbers, calculating them, and then placing them on this tree in whatever order they appear.
One last feature I will point out is that we have seen so far that we have singly degenerate states and triply degenerate states. If I consider a state like \(n_x = 1\), \(n_y = 2\), and \(n_z = 3\), the energy of that state will be
[ E = . ]
This energy level is slightly higher than the \(12\) energy level. Now, if I ask myself what the degeneracy of this state is, I need to consider the number of ways I can shuffle the numbers \(1\), \(2\), and \(3\). The number of permutations of these numbers will determine the degeneracy. Since the sum of the squares remains the same regardless of the order, there are \(3! = 6\) different ways to arrange \(1\), \(2\), and \(3\).
Thus, I have six different states that all have the same energy. This means I can have a degeneracy of one, a degeneracy of three, or sometimes a degeneracy of six for different states. It turns out there are multiple different degeneracies that these states can have. Each of these features of the three-dimensional particle in a box will appear in other types of problems we solve quantum mechanically. However, this is the first time we have encountered cases with both degenerate and non-degenerate states, as well as a mix of different degeneracies and some difficulty predicting the order in which those states will appear.

title: 87  Dye Molecules

title: 87  Dye Molecules
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 87  Dye Molecules
section: Dye Molecules Video Transcript
text:
Dye Molecules Video Transcript
As an interesting example of how we can apply the particle in a box model to real-world molecules, let’s consider dye molecules, which take on very distinctive colors. We have seen that not just linear hydrocarbons, but also conjugated molecules that can be thought of as two-dimensional boxes, can be treated with a particle in a box model. A benzene molecule, for example, can be treated fairly well with the two-dimensional particle in a box model, where we say the pi electrons are confined to a box that approximates the size of the benzene molecule.
Just like we observed for the small one-dimensional particle in a box, when the box is relatively small—just a few carbon atoms on a side—the energy levels for excitations of this molecule turn out to be in the ultraviolet range. They are higher energy than the visible portion of the spectrum. If I were to make that molecule bigger by adding more benzene rings onto it, I could increase the wavelength of light that the molecule absorbs. By making it large enough, I could move it into the visible portion of the spectrum. By changing the size of the molecule, I can affect which colors of light it absorbs and which colors it does not absorb, thereby determining what color the molecule appears to be.
One particularly interesting example of this is phenolphthalein, a molecule you are likely familiar with from previous chemistry courses, especially if you’ve done acid-base titrations. In its acidic form, with the two protons present, it takes on one structure. When we remove those protons—typically done when titrating with hydroxide, for example—we form a more basic version of the molecule. The acidic and basic forms of the molecule have distinct structures.
From the perspective of the particle in a box model, we see that the conjugated portion of the molecule, which includes a benzene ring, is not conjugated with the rest of the molecule. This means that the molecule absorbs light in the ultraviolet portion of the spectrum, just like benzene does. The boxes are not large enough for those pi conjugated electrons to absorb light in the visible portion of the spectrum, so a solution containing this molecule is clear and colorless because it does not absorb any visible light.
On the other hand, when you titrate with hydroxide and consume some of these protons, generating the more basic form of the molecule, we can see that the portion of the molecule that I can draw a box around is now fully conjugated. It has alternating double and single bonds, and it is planar. The two-dimensional box in which these pi conjugated electrons are confined is larger than it used to be, and it is pushed just large enough to absorb in the highest energy portion of the visible spectrum. As a result, it absorbs a little bit of the violet portion of the spectrum, and the color of this molecule appears pink. The color it reflects is slightly tinged toward the red side of the spectrum because it absorbs a little on the violet side.
The reason phenolphthalein is such a good acid-base indicator is that it undergoes a color change when it transitions from its acidic to its basic form. This change occurs because the size of the box that the conjugated electrons are confined to changes. As we have seen, the energy levels of those particle in a box models change with the size of the box, which in turn changes the wavelength of the light that gets absorbed.
Here’s another example of how chemistry can be used. I can clear this picture away and bring up another image with a list of molecules. Let me remove these decorations so we can discuss the collection of molecules on the right-hand side. These are all dye molecules, which could be used to dye clothes, for example. The specific details of the molecules are not important other than the fact that they exhibit different colors, as indicated by their names. Each of these molecules behaves a lot like a two-dimensional particle in a box. They have a large conjugated section within which pi conjugated electrons can move freely. The shape of this box determines the wavelengths of light that are absorbed and, consequently, the wavelengths of light that are not absorbed, which is why some molecules look blue.
For example, in the case of Black 29, we have a mix of small conjugated sections of the molecule, larger sections, and moderate sections. This molecule absorbs light via electrons in several different sized boxes. By absorbing almost all the light that shines on it, it ends up with a relatively black color. This illustrates how, by changing the size of the box that these planar conjugated electrons are confined to, we can change the energy levels directly and therefore alter the color of the molecules.
A significant part of what a dye chemist does is adjust the properties and synthesize different molecules to create various colors. While it is not necessary to constantly think about quantum mechanics when performing organic chemistry to prepare these molecules, it is helpful to consider what should happen as you increase the size of a molecule. You should expect a redshift in the color of the light that gets absorbed. Alternatively, if you change the substituents on these molecules—adding electron-withdrawing or electron-donating substituents—you can change the number of molecules that occupy these energy levels, consequently altering those energy levels as well.
At a qualitative level, it is useful to think about the particle in a box model when considering the colors of these molecules. This also illustrates that even a qualitative understanding is sufficient. If we were to try to quantitatively predict exactly what color a molecule would be, we would find that it is not exactly a rectangular box. It is not entirely true that the electrons feel no difference in energy anywhere they go within the molecule; they might have different energy levels near a carbonyl group compared to far away from it, or in the middle of one of these six-membered rings, and so on. Thus, while the particle in a box model is not an exact representation of these molecules, it is certainly good enough to provide a qualitative idea of how they behave.

title: 88  Molecule in a Box
text: Molecule in a Box (11:07)

title: 88  Molecule in a Box
text:
Note



You will find more excellent videos at this content creater’s YouTube Channel

title: 88  Molecule in a Box
section: Molecule in a Box Video Transcript
text:
Molecule in a Box Video Transcript
All right, let’s treat an example of a particle in a box with some quantitative calculations. In fact, we’re going to compare two different examples of particles in boxes. We have seen that we can use the particle in a box model to describe both electrons confined to a molecule and a conjugated section of a molecule. An example of that would be something like three fused benzene rings, which is an anthracene molecule.
If we measure the frequencies of light absorbed by anthracene, we find that it absorbs light at around 375 nanometers. This corresponds to a photon of light with a wavelength of 375 nanometers. That value is approximately \(10^{-19}\) joules, which is the energy of a photon absorbed by an anthracene molecule. Remember that this corresponds to a pair of energy levels separated by this much energy. When I shine a photon with the same amount of energy, it can excite the electron from the lower energy level up to the upper energy level. Thus, a photon with 375 nanometers has this much energy, which is enough to excite the electron between a pair of states that differ by exactly that much energy. These states can be predicted reasonably well with the two-dimensional particle in a box model.
I mention the energy just to point out that this value, compared to \(kT\), is significant. Remember that the value of \(kT\) at room temperature is about \(4 \times 10^{-21}\) joules. Therefore, the \(10^{-19}\) joules that represents a photon on the red end of the visible spectrum is several orders of magnitude larger than \(kT\), which has a magnitude of about \(10^{-21}\) joules. The energy level spacing between levels for the anthracene molecule is much larger than \(kT\). By comparing those energy differences to \(kT\), we can conclude several things. For example, we know that because the energy difference is so many \(kT\) (about 100 or so \(kT\)), the upper state will not be very populated. Most of the electrons in this molecule, an overwhelming majority, will be in the lower energy levels.
We also know that if we were interested in a partition function, since the only states that are populated are those low compared to \(kT\), the partition function will be relatively small for this molecule. These factors contrast with the other example we’ve discussed using the three-dimensional particle in a box model. If the particle is not an electron but an actual molecule, let’s take the example of a nitrogen gas molecule. Instead of confining an electron inside a molecule, we can confine an electron inside a physical box. Nitrogen gas will stay confined to a box that you place it in.
For our example, let’s say we are in a cubic room whose length, width, and height are all about 5 meters, roughly 15 feet on each side. If I have a nitrogen gas molecule confined to a room that is about 15 feet on a side, we can use the particle in a box model, specifically the three-dimensional particle in a box model, to describe the energy levels of that system. We can ask similar questions about the difference in energy levels for that system.
To do that, we need to know that for a cubic box, the energy levels look like
[ E = ( n_x^2 + n_y^2 + n_z^2 ). ]
In order to calculate these energies, I certainly know what Planck’s constant is. I’ve assumed a value for the size of the box, and we need the mass. Remember that we are not talking about an electron anymore; we are talking about a nitrogen molecule. We need the molar mass of a nitrogen molecule, which is 28 grams per mole. However, we need to convert grams to kilograms to cancel one of the kilograms hiding inside the units of joules.
We also need to get rid of the factor of moles in the denominator, so we use Avogadro’s number of molecules in a mole. So far, I’ve managed to cancel moles and grams, which takes care of the mass. We will use 5 meters for our box size, and that number gets squared. Then we will multiply by the sum of \(n\) values. I will leave that un-substituted for now. Regardless of whether I want to talk about one excitation or another, I will use different values for \(n\), but this number will be a relatively small integer.
The exact value will depend on which excitation level I’m interested in, but let’s calculate this first prefactor term. For that, I need a calculator or I need to have done the calculation ahead of time. What I find is that this number works out to be about \(10^{-42}\) joules. This is the quantity for which I have numbers. Without plugging in specific values for \(n\), I will just say that I’m multiplying by a relatively small number.
The important thing about this value, \(10^{-42}\) joules, is that the units do indeed work. I have kilograms times meters squared in the denominator and seconds squared in the numerator. A kilogram meter squared per second squared is a joule, so that cancels one of my joules in the numerator, leaving me with just one joule. Thus, the energy difference between these states is a small number times \(10^{-42}\) joules compared to \(kT\). Remember, \(kT\) is \(10^{-21}\) joules.
There is not just a two orders of magnitude difference; there are 20 or more orders of magnitude difference between the energy level spacing for the nitrogen molecule in a room-sized box and the value of \(kT\). In particular, the inequality works the other way around: this energy level spacing is considerably less than \(kT\). In fact, saying it is just much less than is an understatement; 20 orders of magnitude is so small as to be negligible.
The energy level spacing for molecules confined to a box, with an energy gap between levels of \(10^{-42}\) joules, is so small that we cannot possibly measure it with any technology we currently have. It is so many orders of magnitude below energies that we can measure. Therefore, those energy levels are essentially super closely packed together.
It turns out that there is a significant difference: electrons can find energy differences that are quite large compared to \(kT\), whereas molecules confined to boxes have energy spacings that are incredibly small compared to \(kT\). This is a very important difference that arises from two different factors: the masses are different, and the box sizes are different. We have changed two numbers when we went from electrons confined to conjugated hydrocarbons to molecules in boxes. A nitrogen molecule is heavier than an electron by a couple thousand fold, which is part of the answer.
An even bigger factor is the fact that I went from a box that was less than a nanometer on a side when confining an electron to boxes that are meters on a side. This is roughly 9 or 10 orders of magnitude, and when I square that number, it becomes enormous. This is the biggest reason why these energy level spacings are so tiny: I have confined this molecule to a large box, which is not much of a confinement at all for a nitrogen molecule in a whole room.
As we will see coming up relatively soon, this very small spacing between the energy levels for a gas molecule in an actual macroscopic-sized box is going to be convenient. It will allow us to calculate several properties of gases by using those energy level spacings as well as some statistical mechanics. That is the next step.
